text:
Friends from the worldwide TFH commu-
nity continue to practice the works of this 
loving man who touched so many during his 
lifetime. His greatest legacy is the continued 
health and healing that will be passed along 
again and again in spirit with each “touch.” IN MEMORIUM John Francis Thie, DC
January 25, 1933 - Aug 3, 2005 As many people became aware of his diagno-
sis in February 2005, the aggressive prostate 
cancer had spread to the bones throughout 
his body. He had a period of pain-free living 
following his recovery from prostate surgery 
and was able to visit with many of his fam-
ily and friends as well as put his affairs in 
order. He also found the energy to work 
many hours on revising the TFH manual, 
determined to have it completely up to date. Shortly after finishing his work on Touch 
for Health Complete Edition, he was able to 
review the final galley copy before it went to 
the printer. John was also delighted with the 
eTouch for Health software and was able to 
use the eTouch Internet Gateway to upload 
sessions for our research project, now named 
the John Thie Memorial Research Project 
(www.etouchforhealth.com/john_thie.html). John was particularly grateful to Earl and Gail 
Cook, whose tireless efforts fulfilled his dream 
of having a TFH computer program— includ-
ing detailed record-keeping, and an internet 
database for collecting outcomes from practi-
tioners around the world. “I would like to be cremated and have my 
ashes scattered in the Pacific Ocean off our 
home in Malibu. I would like to celebrate 
the life that God gave me and be remem-
bered as husband of Carrie; brother of 
Alice; loving father of John Jr., Luther and 
Matthew; and grandfather to Destine, Tim, 
Amanda, Jerrel & Rachel, founder of the 
Touch for Health system, Chiropractor by 
profession and  recognized by local peers as 
Chiropractor of the Century in 1995, and by 
the California Chiropractic Association in 
2005, lay speaker for the United Methodist 
Church of Malibu, internationally known 
speaker and seminar leader in Chiropractic, 
AK and TFH Kinesiology.” John Thie died at 1:11 AM August 3, 2005. We gathered in Malibu on August 10, 2005 
to celebrate his life and share our many 
feelings at his passing. We were joined by 
community friends, family, and members of 
the International TFH family from around 
the world. That same day, many joined us in 
spirit around the world by lighting candles at 
4PM Pacific Standard Time. John considered his diagnosis a blessing for 
it allowed him to hear from all the people 
who loved him. With the love he received, he 
felt he was already in heaven. John died as

text:
he had wanted, living fully, 100% each day, 
until dying within a very short period of time. It was a swift passage. We know he has gone 
home to his Father in heaven. Share your Stories of John Millions of people around the world have 
been affected by John’s work. If you have a 
story, please send it to us either via email or 
regular mail. Send to:
memories@touch4health.com with a subject 
of “A Story of John” Matthew Thie, M.Ed. continues in the 
Tradition of John Matthew has taken on the role of President 
of Touch for Health Education, Inc. and con-
tinues to teach the intensive TFH courses that 
John held at Serra Retreat in Malibu. He also 
leads the TFH 1-4 Workshops (TFH Synthesis), 
TFH Proficiency Assessment Workshops, TFH 
Goal Setting & Metaphor Workshops, and 
the advanced TFH Training Workshops (for 
practitioners, IKC Instructor Certification, and 
IKC Consultant Certification) in Malibu and 
Los Angeles. He also continues his extensive 
international schedule, attending Kinesiology 
conferences and teaching at Kinesiology insti-
tutes worldwide.

text:
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
AND THANKS George Goodheart, my good friend and chi-
ropractor who discovered that tension and 
muscle spasm may be treated by strengthen-
ing opposing weak muscles — for applying 
the manual muscle test (Kinesiology) as 
biofeedback in a clinical setting, to identify 
muscles that “switch off” and for innovating 
so many techniques to turn muscles back on, 
balance them, and stimulate the healing sys-
tem. For the hours of discussion and encour-
agement about presenting this material to 
patients and the general public, I thank you. Don Foster and Sally Lunie — for many suc-
cessful seminars at the Pepperdine University 
Adult Enrichment Program, I am grateful. Victor Frank and Leroy Perry — how for-
tunate for me that you were my associates, 
assisting with the early classes. Stella Terril Mann, author, patient and friend 
— my heartfelt gratitude. Pat Gill, who arranged the initial gathering 
of people that launched the first Touch For 
Health book into production and contrib-
uted greatly to its success — I shall never 
forget. Joe Heller and Katie Wedderien, directors of 
Kairos — for organizing the first class in your 
home and encouraging me to offer similar 
one-day workshops through Kairos and for 
countless hours of listening when the ideas 
for this book were first written. Dr. and Mrs. John C. Thie — for all the 
reasons a son has for thanking his parents, I 
thank and bless you. Virginia Satir – your loving encouragement 
and enthusiasm have meant so much. And special thanks to Carrie, my wife — for 
the hours and days we spent just talking 
about the basic ideas. To my son, John Jr., 
who helped with the classes and seminars — 
my eternal gratitude. Jane Levenburg – your encouragement and 
confidence made it possible for me to go 
ahead and begin.

to compile and release a new book that would 
allow them to use Touch for Health protocols 
in the most updated methods that I personally 
advocate and which they have felt personally 
or observed me doing while teaching clinical 
seminars. DEDICATION I want to give warm thanks to Mary Marks, 
who wrote the first edition of Touch for 
Health with me. Her dedication to our sim-
ilar goals of disseminating health to the gen-
eral public through natural means brought 
us to where we are today. She was the first 
director of the Touch for Health Foundation 
and co-teacher with Carrie and myself of the 
first Instructor Training Workshops. First Edition Dedication Dedicated to the entire family of humanity 
for whom health enhancement is the best 
preventative medicine. (1973) Dedication Touch for Health is committed to healing 
the wounded world in hope that it will bring 
about more harmony and wholeness. I ded-
icate this 2005 edition of Touch for Health 
to Carrie — my wife, lover, confidant, friend, 
editor, co-author, co-worker — and to our 
children, John Jr., Mari (1960-1963), Luther, 
and Matthew. More than anyone can know, 
they contributed to the development of Touch 
for Health. I thank Carrie profoundly for 
the hours, days, weeks and years of sharing 
in my personal journey from “being of this 
world” to becoming born in the Holy Spirit 
with Christ and God the Father. Thanks to 
all my family for being the ones I treated, 
upon whom I experimented with new tech-
niques and worked out ideas to create what I 
now call Touch for Health. It would not have 
been possible were it not for my co-author 
Matthew, my Carrie, and for so many people Special thanks to Toni Gralton, Dean of the 
Touch for Health School of the International 
Kinesiology College, for her years of dedica-
tion, teaching Instructor trainers all over the 
world and supervising the work that these 
people do. Thanks to Gordon Stokes, former Director of 
Training at the Touch for Health Foundation. Were it not for Gordon and the faculty we 
trained to certify Touch for Health instruc-
tors at the International Kinesiology College, 
we would not have over 5,000 Touch for 
Health Instructors worldwide. Thanks to Grace Baldridge, Alice and Kim 
Vieira, Phillip Crockford, Paul and Gail 
Dennison, Jimmy Scott, Richard Utt, Wayne 
Topping, Hap and Elizabeth Barhydt, Joel 
Shain, Marie Olds, Roberta Woods, Seth

Health to those who have worked to develop 
effective teaching materials for TFH, from 
the ITW training manuals to the Level I - IV 
workbooks, and to those who helped produce 
the original book. Having taken part in three 
productions of a TFH computer program, cre-
ating the TFH Metaphor book, as well as edit-
ing the classic TFH manual, I salute you one 
and all who have contributed your gifts and 
talents. And to everyone who touches for the 
purpose of health — taking a moment to be 
present for another human being — whether 
applying TFH, Energy Kinesiology, following 
another modality or your own intuition, more 
power to you in your dedication to helping one 
another feel better and enjoy life! Thanks also to all the people who have used 
Touch for Health with their families, friends, 
students, clients and patients, and to those who 
have shared with me with their results and anec-
dotal reports. I thank all of the people who pray 
for the injured and sick and offer a comforting 
touch, including the Touch for Health energy 
balancing techniques. To all the doctors and 
health practitioners, coaches, counselors, edu-
cators, and other professionals who have had 
the courage to integrate the Touch for Health 
synthesis in their practices, my deep gratitude. And to all the people reading this dedication, 
I thank you in advance for sending me your

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
i Kinesiology. It continues to be extended by 
his workshop leaders and others utilizing 
the skills and sharing their results. John’s 
vision for the future of Applied Kinesiology 
included a combined college that would pro-
vide for the needs of professionals of many 
disciplines and also the training of lay people 
in the safe and simple techniques that would 
enhance their lives. FOREWORD Bruce A. J. Dewe, M.D. Unfortunately, others did not share this 
vision. Sadly, John was mistaken in his 
assumption that all health professionals were 
primarily motivated to share, as widely as 
possible, what would help others to greater 
health and wholeness. Dr. Goodheart did, 
however, tell John that if he felt that a book 
was needed for the public, he could write it 
himself. In late 1973, the first edition of this 
book, Touch For Health, appeared. One of the many things I enjoy about John 
Thie is his willingness to share, at a very 
practical level, the things that have worked 
well for him. This book, Touch For Health, 
is a compilation of many techniques that 
John uses every day of his working life as a 
chiropractic doctor. It is his daily workbook. This would immediately make one suspect 
that Touch For Health is a book for pro-
fessionals, but this is not the case. When, 
in 1964, John Thie was introduced to Dr. George Goodheart D.C. and the biofeedback 
technique of manual muscle testing, he rec-
ognized it as more than just another useful 
technique. John realized that posture could 
be changed more rapidly with the techniques 
Dr. Goodheart was teaching, than with any 
other system he had researched or studied. John became one of the first Goodheart 
Workshop leaders and he kept urging Dr. Goodheart to make this information more 
widely available to the general public. John believes that the simple, safe methods 
he describes in this book will help enhance 
people’s own recuperative powers. It was 
and still is his thesis, that any tool for health 
enhancement that is safe and easy to use 
should be available to everyone, regardless of 
their level of previous training. John wanted 
to see a forum of all who worked profession-
ally in enhancing the health and wholeness 
of living things. This has not occurred within 
the professions – but it has occurred in the 
world of Touch for Health. Touch for Health is no longer just the title 
of a best-selling book or the name of a non-

and remains abundantly true today, TFH 
is not in competition with the practice of 
Kinesiology by physicians or other healthcare 
professionals, or with other approaches to 
healthcare. There are plenty of people for

self-awareness and self-care.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
xix Releasers, Top Ten Pain Releasers, Enhanced 
Learning, TFH for Children, TFH for Balanced 
Vision, TFH for Pets, etc. The faculty mem-
bers of the International Kinesiology College 
continue to develop the standard TFH pro-
gram. Most recently we have added the TFH 
Metaphors Workshop, building on the creative 
dialogue, goal-setting, visualization and the 
symbolism of the Chinese 5 Elements. The TFH Kinesiology model is an Educational, 
Assistive model, not a diagnostic, prescriptive 
model. We don’t necessarily identify or even 
seek out “what is wrong”, or presume to solve 
the mysteries and figure out the “cause” of 
our ills. We help people identify their own 
positive goals within their own philosophy 
and worldview. Only they know their tal-
ents, aspirations, desires, missions. We can 
serve to help them identify the conflicts, and 
harmonize their energy. Sometimes we may 
be an unknowing messenger, providing a 
thought, idea, or metaphor which rings true 
and catalyzes vision, but it is for the individual 
to decide. We offer suggestions for them to 
accept or reject. There are now many Kinesiology institutes 
which offer up to 3-year professional train-
ings in Kinesiology. Some of them offer a 
professional training exclusively in Touch for 
Health Kinesiology. The demand for these 
trainings is only growing, and I think there 
is a public need for professionals trained 
in this way. I am happy to see that there 
is an increased integration of the educa-
tional model into the concept of professional 
Kinesiology training. Some programs even 
integrate training as a Kinesiology instruc-
tor into their professional training, so that 
there is an assumption that even professional 
Kinesiology practitioners would also offer 
lay/client education and training (such as the 
standard TFH workshops) in parallel with 
their individual work with clients. We are not seeking a static, “ultimate” state 
of health, or a magic potion that will fix 
everything in our lives. We are seeking to 
help people live more fully in the moment, 
appreciating the dynamic dance of life and 
the flow of energy and creativity. Part of 
this process is that we do need interaction 
with other people to discover who we are. Being ourselves is an interactive happening. TFH provides a supportive, sheltered struc-
ture for a positive experience of present time, 
immediate confirmation and enrichment of 
our sense of self, balance, purpose and ful-
fillment in our lived lives through human 
interaction and bio-feedback. A professional Touch for Health style kine-
siologist must be a fundamentally different 
kind of therapist. With a primary emphasis on 
teaching people to be aware of their choices 
and care for themselves, they also offer exper-
tise and experience in the specific techniques 
and applications of energy balancing with 
Kinesiology.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
xx can serve to safely guide those we cherish 
toward a more abundant life. I invite YOU 
to join in the chain of passing the light. Some 
of us have a natural predisposition and will 
thrive in the role, excel through our interest 
and joy in the work, whether at home with 
our families, as part of our professional 
practice, as a workshop teacher,  personal 
coach,  as a professional TFH Kinesiologist 
seeing clients one-on-one, or all of the above. I invite you to discover the gift of healing 
that each of you possess in your own hands 
that can be realized simply by touching one 
another for the purpose of healing, as you 
enjoy your contribution in making the world 
a better place. Keeping in Touch, 
John Thie, DC
Malibu, California
June 1, 2005 this your first exposure to TFH and energy 
balancing, or you are “coming back” to TFH 
to learn about this powerful system again 
“for the first time”, or a dedicated TFH’er 
in your home, community, and professional 
practice. All of the texts have been rewritten, but 
in such a way that they will sound MORE 
like what you thought they said the first 
time. For example, we have adjusted some 
of the vocabulary so that our meanings are 
emphasized and clarified throughout the 
book. Thus the terms “strong” and “weak” 
are still used, but they are clearly defined as 
sensations related to muscle response and 
energy flow, rather than evaluations of actual 
physical integrity or capacity in the muscle 
itself. We also emphasize alternative terms 
such as “unlocking” or “locking”, inhibited 
or facilitated, and balanced or imbalanced. Having had the opportunity to travel, teach & 
share around the world at TFH Conferences, 
Energy Kinesiology Conferences, and even 
Energy Psychology conferences, I have had the 
pleasure to see so many ways that the TFH 
concept and system have developed. A Note from Matthew Thie I hope you will find a valuable resource in 
the Touch for Health Complete Edition that 
contributes profoundly to your own self-care 
and care for others. This Edition contains 
all of the information taught in the Official 
Worldwide Curriculum of the International 
Kinesiology College: The 60 hour TFH 
Synthesis training. It is also contains the 
up-to-the minute details of Dr. Thie’s use 
of Dialogue, Goal Setting, and Metaphor to 
make TFH energy balancing more profound 
and effective. This Edition will truly com-
plete your understanding of TFH, whether The grassroots tradition is alive and well. Many people are using TFH in the home, 
and teaching small classes in the “home-
school” approach, and are able to help 
others with “just the basics”of a 14-muscle 
balance.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
xxi TFH for children,  learning enhancement, 
for the classroom setting and so forth. The 
lay self-health, empowerment model remains 
revolutionary today and it is very encourag-
ing to see it established and developing in 
many forms, growing into new niches for the 
purpose of sharing these safe and effective 
techniques with the widest possible audience 
for the greatest benefit. because it is within these primary relation-
ships that we find the most fundamental 
and profound power to create and nurture 
day-to-day Wellness, growth and fulfillment 
which is the ultimate goal of the TFH system. TFH was created to empower families to rec-
ognize and revitalize the importance of home 
care, which probably accounts for more than 
90% of the healing that we experience. At the same time, so many people have come 
to me and said, “Matthew, I am doing “just” 
Touch for Health in my professional prac-
tice, [maybe in combination with some mas-
sage, some physical therapy, or psychology, 
or personal coaching] and I am getting really 
great results, my clients are very satisfied 
and give lots of referrals, and I am able to 
make a living at it.”  Or they say, “Matthew, 
you need to stop saying basic, simple, easy, 
because people think it is just for use in the 
home. I was told that I needed to know 
the muscle test for X, Y, & Z condition or 
I couldn’t consider myself a “professional”, 
and after studying many different systems, 
I have come back to TFH and found that it 
really is a very powerful, profound, complex, 
sophisticated, intricate and complete system. Don’t say simple, don’t say easy. Say clear 
and logical. Say gentle, non-invasive, safe. Say non-diagnostic, non-medical, holistic, 
Wellness-oriented. Don’t say basic,  say fun-
damental or foundational. I also appreciate the sentiment that TFH is 
not “just” TFH,  not just a basic teaching for 
beginners. Now there are Professional TFH 
programs being developed based on the same 
principals and techniques. These programs 
allow students more time to study in depth, 
to practice with supervision and feedback, to 
demonstrate and document knowledge and 
mastery, and to be assessed for competency 
and coached for excellence and success, 
including components on practice manage-
ment in an educational, self-empowering 
model of kinesiology practice. And I have 
seen that the TFH approach is a very valu-
able and necessary model to be applied in a 
TFH kinesiology clinical practice. Though 
most kinesiology practitioners also apply 
techniques from “other” kinesiology systems, 
they consistently report that TFH is a core 
part of their practice, and I know that my 
father practiced chiropractic for 35 years 
using TFH as his primary intervention.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
xxii psychology, relationship, career or spiritual 
Counseling, yoga or Pilates instruction, mas-
sage therapy, dance instruction etc., etc. So it 
is abundantly clear that TFH is valuable in the 
context of clinical therapy, whether as a core 
system or adjunct to a particular specialty. of Energy Kinesiology with the TFH philoso-
phy of education and empowerment. Of course it does take some practice, and the 
more opportunities to practice and to prove 
to yourself your own competence, the better. With more training, support, and opportuni-
ties to practice, a greater percentage of people 
will be able to benefit from the TFH system. I know that we can study any of the aspects 
of TFH to great depth throughout our life-
times. The muscles may lead us into detailed 
anatomy, university study of kinesiology and 
body mechanics, functional neurology, phys-
ical therapy, massage, and so on. The study 
of the Meridian Energy can lead us into a 
vast field of Traditional Chinese Medicine as 
well as other and ancient, traditional ener-
gy-based arts- Tibeten Medicine, Ayurveda, 
Yoga and so on. We can also become inter-
ested in the physiology of the muscles and 
organs and of course have a vast resource 
in our universities for studying physiology 
and all the other aspects of human function 
associated with the bio-medical approach to 
health and pathology. The reflex points that 
we work may lead us to study many parallel 
systems that utilize similar reflexes or pro-
tocols. Now, after 30 years, there are many 
systems of energy-based kinesiology that are 
based in whole or in part on the fundamen-
tals of TFH or Applied Kinesiology. These 
systems were developed in a parallel holis-
tic model of energy balancing for optimum 
wellness and function of our life-creating and 
healing system. So it IS easy. If you just try it and practice 
a bit, you may find it not only works, but 
you really like it. It is also worth reinforcing 
and expanding with further training. You 
might take (and repeat, perhaps with different 
instructors) all 4 basic TFH Workshops, TFH 
Proficiency Workshop and Assessment, and 
the Advanced Training Workshop. Consider 
forming a Practice Group, and attending our 
annual TFH Kinesiology Conference (check 
www.tfhka.org for TFH instructors and con-
ference information), or the Annual confer-
ence of the IKC (www.IKC-info.org). These 
organizations have the objectives of increasing 
awareness about the TFH energetic approach 
to Kinesiology and providing more opportu-
nities for mutual support, development and 
training. You will be inspired in new ways, 
enhance your knowledge of kinesiology, cre-
ate new friendships and revitalize your life. I 
look forward to seeing you at a conference or 
maybe I’ll see you in Malibu! (Don’t forget to 
bring your boogieboard).

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
3 THE EVOLUTION OF 
TOUCH FOR HEALTH 
KINESIOLOGY majority of dis-ease encountered in life, the 
holistic approach of balancing one’s life and 
energy is the most powerful and safe healing 
modality. From ancient to contemporary 
times, the practice of prayer, meditation, 
exercise, balanced diet and positive think-
ing have been the potent tools supporting 
humanity in its quest for optimum health in 
body, mind and spirit. The Touch for Health (TFH) Kinesiology 
approach to health draws from various 
holistic traditions, particularly the energetic 
model of traditional Chinese medicine/ acu-
puncture, lay training in effective interper-
sonal communication, and the Vitalist tradi-
tion in chiropractic. The Vitalist tradition parallels the energetic 
model within the Chinese traditions. The 
idea is that there is a life force that, when 
in balance and flowing, naturally supports 
Wellness; the complete healthy function of 
the person in their experience of life. In this 
model, discomfort, pain, illness and disease 
are seen as symptoms of imbalance or block-
age in the vital energy or life force of the 
person. However, there are times — particularly with 
sudden or acute conditions — when there is 
injury, imbalance of microorganisms or the 
internal chemistry or circulation within the 
person, that death or grave damage is immi-
nent. In such circumstances, specific treat-
ments for specific symptoms must be applied. Advances in biomedical science have given 
us a powerful array of drugs, surgery and 
other treatments to help with specific symp-
toms such as high blood pressure and other 
recognized sets of symptoms (named diseases 
and syndromes). The great power of these 
treatments in creating drastic change in bio-
logical function creates considerable danger While certain disease agents such as viruses 
and bacteria may play a role in the specific 
symptoms a person experiences, the holistic 
approach addresses the whole person and 
their life energy, allowing the natural heal-
ing system to function effectively. For the

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
4 of harm or negative side effects, even from 
their correct and optimal use. For this rea-
son it is vital that such treatments be applied 
judiciously by experts who are trained in their 
safest usage. The chiropractic tradi-
tion maintains that there 
is an innate intelligence 
governing the body. This intelligence is con-
nected to the universal 
intelligence running the 
universe. Each person 
is “plugged into” the 
universal system. The chiropractor’s role is to 
facilitate this communication and insure the 
body’s optimal functioning. “If your spine is in line, you’ll The education of the modern chiropractic 
doctor encompasses a great deal of bio-
medical training including the diagnosis of 
medically recognized diseases. A chiropractic 
physician may name and treat specific symp-
toms or diseases and/or 
refer patients to special-
ized physicians for such 
treatment. However, the 
chiropractic tradition is 
holistic in its approach, 
so that while symptoms 
and named diseases give 
important information 
about a patient, the chiropractor’s emphasis 
is not the treatment of a disease. Rather, the 
chiropractor treats the whole person. The 
primary chiropractic modality is the balanc-
ing of physical posture and spinal alignment 
so that there is optimal flow, circulation and 
balance in the whole person. feel fine.” A Chiropractor At the end of the 19th century, D. D. Palmer, 
a magnetic healer, rediscovered chiropractic 
through use of the spinous and transverse 
processes of the vertebrae as levers to correct 
posture and readjust the spine. Chiropractic 
evolved from this basic premise of moving 
the spine to improve health. Drs. Bennett, 
Chapman, and De Jarnette were pioneers in 
working with reflexes as we use them today. Their work proved how the activation of skin 
areas through deep massage or light touch 
causes muscles to move bones without the 
practitioner working on the bones directly. treats the whole person. The spine is the central core of the nervous 
system, the master system of the body. Health comes from within and the body 
takes care of itself when there is no inter-
ference in the communication and flow of 
energy. People who have good posture, with 
the all of the various parts of the body work-
ing in harmony, have good health.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
5 The Opposing Muscle Theory spring will rebalance the system; the weak 
spring must be strengthened or replaced. This 
principle applies to the muscles of the human 
body. For each motion a muscle makes, there 
is a corresponding muscle which opposes 
that motion. If the tight muscle pulls a limb 
away from the body, look for a muscle which 
pulls it in to test weak. Rather than working 
the tight muscle, we use the TFH techniques 
to strengthen the opposing weak one. If the 
tightness persists, then it may become neces-
sary to relax the tight muscle with an inhib-
iting reflex. Now the balance of tension in 
the muscles will result in greater and longer 
lasting comfort. In the early 1960s, Dr. George Goodheart, 
founder of Applied Kinesiology, made this 
seemingly common-sense yet revolutionary 
observation: Although we feel pain when 
muscles are tight or in spasm and the spine is 
pulled out of alignment, the problem isn’t nec-
essarily located in the muscle that hurts. Most 
of the time the muscle isn’t actually having a 
spasm. Rather, weak muscles on one side of 
the body can cause opposing normal muscles 
to become tight. This mechanism works as 
depicted in the following illustration. Fig. 1
Fig. 2 When we find a tight or knotted muscle, 
our first inclination is to work directly on 
the muscle itself, kneading and stretching 
to relax it. However, there may be a reason 
why the muscle is tense. The tension, even if 
temporarily released, will return. Think of a kitchen door held in place by two 
springs (Fig. 1). As long as the tension on both 
springs is equal, the system is in balance. If, 
however, one spring weakens, the opposing 
spring ties itself in knots taking up the slack 
(Fig. 2). No amount of oiling the knotted

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
6 Combining Muscle Testing and 	
	
Touch Reflexes and self-knowledge to determine whether 
TFH Kinesiology is advisable or sufficient 
to 
address 
their 
current health con-
dition. If a person 
does not seem well 
enough to make 
that 
assessment 
on their own, they 
should be referred 
to someone qual-
ified to diagnose 
their medical con-
dition. In practice, 
the emphasis on 
self-awareness and 
self-care leads peo-
ple to seek needed 
care earlier and to 
proactively evalu-
ate the quality of 
care they receive. APPLIED KINESIOLOGY Touch for Dr. Goodheart observed changes in muscle 
response using various reflexes, including the 
acupuncture meridians. He soon integrated 
the use of touch reflexes with manual muscle 
testing. Goodheart used muscle testing for 
postural assessment and balancing. He also 
began to use muscle testing as biofeedback 
related to energy flow and balance in the 
acupuncture meridians. The original system 
that integrated this set of tools – Applied 
Kinesiology – was designed for physicians 
who are licensed to diagnose named diseases; 
however, the judicious use of Kinesiology is 
intended for holistic balancing of the whole 
person. Where muscle testing has been used 
in the diagnostic process it has always been 
as an additional clue to be considered along 
with the customary biostatistical measure-
ments and tests – never as a means of diag-
nosis by itself. Health empowers individuals to be productive in their own Wellness with- out replacing any branch of health care. The TFH approach is to become aware of 
even subtle symptoms that may affect the 
whole system. But our focus is on balancing 
the whole system rather than trying to diag-
nose the cause of the symptom. What we 
think about, what we take into our hearts 
and minds and what we put into our bodies, 
affects the whole person. When something 
happens to one member of the family, it 
affects everyone else in the family — per-
haps some more than others; but everyone is 
affected at some level. It is the same within 
our bodies. TFH Kinesiology was developed early in the 
evolution of Applied Kinesiology. Touch 
for Health is a clear system of utilizing the 
postural and energetic balancing techniques 
of Applied Kinesiology in a holistic, non-di-
agnostic model of Wellness and health main-
tenance. Touch for Health makes available 
powerful yet safe tools, so that people with-
out biomedical training may help each other 
feel better within a framework of self-respon-
sibility. Self-responsibility means that each 
individual uses his or her own common sense

cases where there is no diagnosable condition

to maintain their health and recognize when 
they need additional care. For that reason, 
there is a great need for professionals whose

ing modalities appropriate for them. The 
additional help they seek may include a

a Holistic Multifactorial Model The TFH Definition of Health In the Touch for Health system, health is 
defined as our ability to feel fully alive and 
vital, 
regardless 
of 
whether 
we 
are “sick” or not. Health is not merely 
the absence of signs 
and 
symptoms, 
nor is it defined by 
the 
biostatistical 
parameters of nor-
malcy. When we 
experience health, 
we fulfill the role of 
the healthy person. We work towards 
the realization of 
goals and a sense 
of fulfilling our mission in life with some hap-
piness. A more detailed model may be illustrated 
with a four-sided pyramid comprised of and 
supported by 12 equally important facets. Health is an experience of well THE PYRAMID OF HEALTH being — physically, mentally, emotionally, Balance
Homeostasis
Redundancy spiritually, socially Balance refers to the dynamic balance that 
must be constantly regulated in order to main-
tain the homeostasis of certain functions within 
a narrow range (such as body temperature). Redundancy refers to the multiple parallel sys-
tems that can compensate for any dysfunction, 
but are most efficient when all are functioning 
together. — integrated in the whole  person. A holistic model of health is often repre-

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
11 The 12 Factors of the Pyramid Both positive and negative aspects of our 
history can affect energy balance, whether 
or not we are aware of it. We may have 
survived traumatic experiences, yet continue 
to be affected by unhealed wounds, which 
create limitations or uncomfortable adapta-
tions. We identify with various groups and 
the shape of our lives are affected by both 
the triumphs and traumas of our families or 
all of humanity. Our lives can be shaped by 
the group’s fundamental assumptions, which 
then direct our perceptions. INSIDE THE SKIN: STRUCTURAL, CHEMICAL, 
INTELLECTUAL, EMOTIONAL The pyramid structure itself represents aspects 
of life that are “inside the skin.” Each side 
reflects qualities that are equally vital to the 
integrity of the whole: STRUCTURAL (pos-
ture), CHEMICAL (the metabolic, physiolog-
ical functions), INTELLECTUAL (conscious 
and subconscious), and EMOTIONAL (feel-
ings, moods, responses, reactions that inter-
pret experiences). CONTEXT refers to the whole situation, back-
ground that surrounds the content of the 
individual: those events that are happening 
around the person when the content of the 
person’s life is being affected. Being injured 
or having an illness is the content, but the 
world situation, like war or a terrorist attack 
in another part of the country, is the context 
of the lived life. What our experience means 
to us and how it affects us often has more 
to do with the circumstances and intention 
than with the actual physical impact. Getting 
kicked with the same amount of force – on 
purpose or by accident – say, during a soccer 
game, by one’s little brother or by a stranger 
on the bus, may have completely different 
effects upon our sense of Wellness. ENVIRONMENT is another factor that affects 
our health and wholeness. We have all expe-
rienced both positive and negative environ-
ments, whether we are talking about the nat-
ural (biological) environment or our social 
(family or community) environment. Driving Although each of these aspects exhibits dis-
tinct properties, they are all connected and 
part of one integrated whole which is made 
completely of energy. On an energetic level, 
all of the aspects are continuously interacting 
and thus affecting one another. When there 
is an imbalance in any one aspect there is 
a corresponding imbalance in every other 
aspect. In the TFH system we use the muscle 
test to help develop awareness of energetic 
imbalance, which reflects imbalance in any 
aspect of a person. OUTSIDE THE SKIN: HISTORY, CONTEXT, 
ENVIRONMENT, THE FUTURE Surrounding the pyramid are four external 
aspects that have profound effects upon our 
experience and all aspects of our being. HISTORY includes our personal life story, the 
genetics of our family and ethnic group and 
the collective cultural and political histories 
of our communities, nations, and world. Surrounding the pyramid are four external 
aspects that have profound effects upon our 
experience and all aspects of our being. HISTORY includes our personal life story, the 
genetics of our family and ethnic group and

of our communities, nations, and world.

12 behind a toxic, smoking vehicle can really 
knock us out of balance. Living or working 
in a sterile environment with no organic 
forms of living plants or animals may lead to 
a deficiency of energy. In addition to Nature: 
fresh air, clean water, nutrition, etc., we 
know that Nurture exerts a major influence 
upon our well-being. The type of interaction 
we have with other human beings — partic-
ularly parents, family, peers and community 
— is a powerful aspect of our physical, emo-
tional, spiritual health and energetic balance. Knowing our aims, we may assess how we 
interact with our environment and determine 
whether we need to simply balance our ener-
gies to skillfully handle our circumstances or 
make changes in where we live, what we do 
and with whom we spend time. and attract to us the things and people that 
will support us in our life purpose. Goal-
setting is part of the process of balancing our 
subtle energy to gracefully fulfill our destiny 
through the sharing of our God-given talents 
and fulfilling our personal mission. The Future hasn’t happened yet, so it remains 
open to multiple possibilities. There may be 
dangers or consequences to our actions, but 
there may also be many wonderful outcomes 
of choosing what we really want and going 
for it. We are affected by our attitudes and 
expectations, positive or negative. Another 
aspect is that we don’t only experience time 
in a linear, chronological order of equivalent 
seconds and minutes ticking away. Time can 
zip by or expand so that hours seem like 
moments, and certain moments seem eternal. This is sometimes called “kairos” time, the 
eternal moment where all time is connected 
— so that we may create and be connected 
with the future even while in the present 
moment. Just as we can allow our feelings 
about past events to color our current feel-
ings and attitudes, we can balance our energy 
and ourselves for the future that we envi-
sion. When we feel that the future is already 
real, we simply need to allow chronological 
(Chronos) time to unfold. We can experience 
faith and joy in our present circumstances, 
based on the future that we have consciously 
chosen. THE FUTURE involves the recognition that our 
perceptions of our life today and in our past, 
may be greatly influenced by our outlook, 
and hopes and dreams for the future. How 
many people have overcome impossible odds 
and adversity by keeping alive a vision for 
themselves, for their children and for those 
who would come after them? We can be dominated by our past experience, 
dragged down by failure or frustration; or we 
can keep our eyes on our goals, persevering 
until we reach them.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
13 honest. When we ourselves fail to be truthful 
in a particular situation or relationship it 
can affect our energy and have health conse-
quences. THE FOUNDATION OF THE PYRAMID: 	
	
TRUTH, FAITH, HOPE, LOVE For a pyramid to be strong and withstand the 
test of time, it requires a rock-solid base and 
foundation for support. For the TFH model 
of health, your core beliefs and spirituality 
represent the unbending basis your life is 
built upon. Consider what truth means to you in your 
life. In what activities or actions has truth 
— or falsehood — been important? Truth 
covers a wide range of positive and negative 
possibilities in our lives: authenticity, validity, 
sustainability, reality, fidelity, devotion, accu-
racy, transparency, functionality; or, on the 
other hand, evasiveness, deception, dishonesty, 
hollowness, hypocrisy, treachery, unfairness. We need a sense of truth in our lives, some 
feeling that we know what to expect and what 
is really happening. When our ideas of reality 
are consistently contradicted, or when lies or 
deception make us doubtful of ourselves or 
other people, it’s hard to stay in balance. Every aspect of your experience of life will 
be different, depending upon whether you 
see yourself as essentially a random result of 
biological processes or as a precious, unique 
individual, created with an intelligent design 
and purpose for your life and meaning in the 
overall Plan. Your life and its meaning for 
you depends to a great extent upon whether 
you relate to a benevolent and loving Energy 
(and even Personality) in the universe con-
necting all life and all things, or whether you 
see life as a short, harsh struggle in a brutal 
physical landscape. FAITH is an unquestioning belief. Faith is the 
knowing that something, though perhaps 
unseen, is real. Faith is complete reliance, 
confidence and trust in something — such as 
the faith that small children have in their par-
ents. When we have faith we can be enthusi-
astic about an idea or a project because we 
believe in it wholeheartedly. When we lose 
money we lose a lot; when we lose a friend 
we lose much more, and when we lose faith 
we can feel like we have lost everything. So 
a loss of faith is potentially a stressful, even 
heartbreaking, event or process. The good 
news is that we can balance our energy to 
recover from lost truths and restore our faith 
in something greater than ourselves. As the source of our being, spirituality 
becomes the base of the pyramid, upon 
which all else is founded. Spirituality involves 
our inner values and philosophy about the 
nature of reality and how Truth, Faith, 
Hope, and Love direct our lives and purpose 
from within.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
15 love is used to sell things, equating material 
objects and status symbols with sexual grat-
ification. It can be used to express power, 
aggression and even self-destruction. Erotic 
love can be intoxicating, misleading, hollow 
and damaging. Yet in the right circumstances, 
expressing and sharing our erotic nature is 
powerfully life-affirming and healing. the family of humanity, often test the bounds 
of love yet increase the existence, depth and 
strength of love in our lives. Love of a yearning nature can be very heal-
ing, even life-saving. When we think of a per-
son who is not present we may be comforted 
by our fond feelings for them. We may be 
able to carry on because of the hope of see-
ing our loved ones again. But yearning love 
can be harmful when we are seduced into 
wanting something that doesn’t really exist, 
that we can never have, or that won’t bring 
us the joy and satisfaction that is promised. It may be worth it to stand in line all day, or 
even all night in the rain, to see our favorite 
film or musical group, but it’s not healthy to 
cause ourselves harm or to throw our lives 
out of balance because of an obsession with 
a person or group that has no idea we exist. Love between parents and children is fun-
damental. Depending on how this love is 
expressed, it can be empowering and healing 
or it can be damaging. The ideal of parental 
love serves as our model of God’s love for 
us: our capacity to love unconditionally, to 
support one another through our successes 
and shortcomings and to take care of one 
another. Yet many actions taken in the name 
of love and the best interests of our children 
can have negative impacts and negative con-
sequences. The unconditional love of God, also called 
Agape love, is our model for the most sus-
tainable love to practice in general. It is the 
kind of love we need to practice when we 
touch for the purpose of health. Agape love 
means that we are conscious and aware of 
our feelings, whether they be of affection, 
desire, respect or compassion and that we 
take great care to act only in the best interest 
of the person we are helping. We do nothing 
that will harm the person. This unconditional 
love is less a feeling than it is an act of will 
that requires conscious, ethical rules and 
boundaries, as well as careful attention to the 
needs and desires of others to ensure that our 
actions have the most positive impact. Our parental roles, whether with our own 
offspring or with others whom we care for or 
mentor are some of the most sacred and ful-
filling.

16 posture. What each person experiences and 
learns through muscle testing depends upon 
the beliefs that they hold individually and col-
lectively. These beliefs are usually implicit and 
not explicitly discussed. MUSCLE TESTING, 
CHECKING OR 
MONITORING The muscle tests used in TFH were originally 
developed to evaluate the actual physical 
integrity of specific muscles. The positions 
and ranges were literally a test of the muscle 
itself to determine if there was some degree 
of paralysis or lack of strength or function. The basic idea is to contract the muscle and 
isolate it to some degree by using a specific Even after 40-plus years of developing and 
teaching TFH, there remains some mystery 
about the phenomenon of the manual mus-
cle test. The TFH muscle testing technique 
is a mechanical skill that may be learned by 
anyone with some practice. Yet what the test 
means and what we do about it has more 
to do with the context of the muscle-testing 
relationship between two people than it does 
with the precise observation of individual 
muscle response. The muscle test offers us an immediate, sub-
jective (though objectively observable) and 
personally meaningful measurement. It pro-
vides us access to powerful techniques for 
effecting change and reinforcing that change 
in the whole person: physically, mentally, emo-
tionally, and spiritually. For the most part, we 
are not measuring the gross physical strength 
of the muscle, but are monitoring the muscle 
response as an indicator of changes in energy. TFH muscle testing is both a biofeedback 
mechanism, giving us subtle indications about 
how life energy is affected on multiple levels, 
and a concrete exercise for tapping into the 
neuromuscular system to balance the physical Pectoralis Major Clavicular Test: Monitoring Muscle Response in a 
Specific Range of Motion

to Monitor Energy Flow starting position and range of motion and 
then see how well the muscle holds against 
gravity or manual force. The 42 muscles in 
the Touch for Health system were selected 
for their ease of use and their importance for 
posture and movement. Although we depend on (and will probably 
affect) the physical integrity of the muscle 
and the related neurology or brain func-
tion, most of the muscles we check are well 
within the range of normal function. We 
want to take note of 
any injury, dysfunc-
tion, or pain related 
to the muscle or 
muscle test — but 
the primary focus 
is on monitoring 
the response of the 
muscle as an indica-
tion of energy flow 
or balance. Each 
muscle in the TFH 
system 
has 
been 
mapped to a specific 
meridian and tends 
to respond better 
to certain reflexes. So when we use the 
word “test” in the 
TFH system, we are 
talking about checking or monitoring the 
dynamic function of the muscles as an indi-
cator of energy flow or balance in the person 
and in specific meridians, rather than evalu-
ating specific muscle strength or injury. Over the course of 40-plus years, thousands 
of people have found that the positions and 
ranges that we use are very effective for check-
ing energy flow in the meridians. Continued 
study of muscle function has yielded a wide 
variety of techniques and opinions regarding 
the most accurate test of a given muscle, so 
many practitioners adapt the tests to fit with 
their training or perspective. However, we 
are not overly concerned with whether we are 
using the “correct” test for the named muscle. For any given muscle there is variation from 
one anatomy text to another, from Kinesiology 
school to Kinesiology school and most impor-
tantly, from person to person. Our focus is 
on monitoring changes in responses as an 
indication of changes in the energy system for 
the individual we are helping. We have made 
some changes in the standard muscle tests as 
taught throughout the world based on the 
feedback and experience of many instructors. But even the older tests for the meridians are 
perfectly good indicators of energy flow and 
are effective in developing our subtle aware-
ness of our own energy. Indicator Muscle: Any muscle that functions correctly can be used to evaluate stress response, energy imbalance, or the circuits/reflexes of other muscles under specific The muscle test illustrations in this edition 
of the Touch for Health manual have been 
completely updated for greater clarity and to 
correspond with the current standard tests as 
established by the International Kinesiology 
College, and as they are used by thousands of 
TFH instructors and practitioners through-
out the world.

20 upon signal. If you are directing someone to 
touch you, be sure that they agree to stop 
when you say so. Feel free to breathe, make 
sounds, or do whatever you need to while the 
reflexes are being worked. If you are the one 
doing the testing, watch for signs that a discus-
sion is needed regarding if and how to proceed. Sometimes it takes time for people to speak up 
for themselves and be directive in their own 
energy balancing, so we need to consistently 
check with each other about the process. ing what we want to achieve. It is a good 
idea to explain the reflex points and their 
locations in as clear and specific terms as 
possible. Showing the person the points in 
the manual teaches them how to use the sys-
tem, gives them the choice to rub their own 
points or decide whether to rub a particular 
point at all. It also gives you an opportunity 
to remember/confirm the locations. Find a comfortable and safe position both for 
yourself and the person being tested. Do not 
use awkward postures that feel strained or 
risk injury to either person. The person/muscle 
being tested must be stabilized to get an accu-
rate test result. For example, when pushing 
on an arm it may be necessary to hold the 
opposite shoulder to counteract the tendency 
to turn the body. This can eliminate confusion 
in test results and protect from unnecessary 
strain as the person attempts to maintain their 
posture while focusing on a subtle test. Avoid 
stooping or straining to perform a test. Before doing any physical touching, we ask, 
“Is it okay to touch you?” Check whether 
there is any emotional, physical or medical 
condition that prohibits certain kinds of touch-
ing or movement. If someone is injured or has 
an aversion to certain kinds of touching, find 
out ahead of time so that the condition is not 
exacerbated. We may be able to work around 
an injury or simply work gently with sore 
muscles — but sometimes we need to refrain 
from trying to help a person in any physical 
way.  (See “Surrogate Testing,” pg. 256 to find 
out how to help a person whose muscles can’t 
be tested, or who can’t be touched.) During 
the test, or as you place a muscle in the test 
position, encourage the person being tested to 
relax the muscle if it hurts or if it is a strain for 
them to hold the muscle in place. Remind them 
to say, “stop,” if they feel pain. Apply an even, gradually increasing pressure 
— not a sudden “surprise” to the person/mus-
cle.

21 The Rule of 2’s muscles at a disadvantage, it’s easier to injure 
a related muscle if you ignore pain. The test is used to indicate subtle differences 
in muscle response, not to see if one person 
can overpower the other. It is unnecessary 
to apply more than 2 pounds of pressure. See if you can use just 2 ounces of pressure 
and have both participants feel whether the 
muscle locks in place or gives way. Apply 
and release the pressure within about 2 sec-
onds. Testing for more 
than 2 seconds can often 
fatigue an isolated mus-
cle and cause it to give 
way. Test and see if the 
muscle locks without 
tiring it and/or forcing 
it to move. It’s okay if 
the muscle moves a little 
bit and then locks. If the 
muscle response seems 
wobbly, mushy, or the muscle does not seem 
to lock into place within about two inches, 
this indicates that the energy is probably not 
balanced within the related meridian, or that 
the flow is blocked to the muscle. Go ahead 
and work the related reflexes. Look for small 
improvements in the muscle “locking” in 
place. (The improvements may be dramatic!)
 
Stop testing if it hurts. Consider any pain — 
whether in the tested muscle or in some other 
place — an indication of inhibited energy 
flow, then move on to the reflex points. Pain 
in another muscle may indicate the person 
is recruiting other muscles to make up for 
inhibition in the muscle we are testing. Since 
the muscle test is designed to put synergistic Stop testing if you experience pain when 
conducting a test. Each individual needs to 
be aware and responsible for him or herself. You cannot be as effective if you do not listen 
to yourself and protect yourself. If you expe-
rience pain performing the test, your posture 
will show it and it may have an effect on the 
outcome. You also risk self-injury and loss 
of the ability to be of further help to anyone. It’s a good rule for all muscle tests — whether 
there is a previous awareness of injury or not — 
to just relax and let go if there is pain or a sense 
of strain or struggle. Any pain during the test 
is considered the same as muscle inhibition and 
meridian imbalance. There’s no need to push 
against a muscle that has pain. Go straight to 
the balancing techniques for the muscle and 
recheck. You may get a lot of improvement 
in the pain and be able to check the muscle 
gently; or you may simply use some improve-
ment in range and comfort as your indicator.

person being tested. Obviously a child will not 
be as strong as an adult. Some of the muscles 
— especially the neck and abdominal muscles 
— are not fully developed in the early years, 
so adjust the amount of pressure applied to 
the person you are working with.  (Try using 
just two fingers.) However, the amount of 
resistance that can be shown in a muscle test is 
often surprising. If the energy is flowing, you 
will be able to feel the muscle lock in place 
even on a child or a very weak person. Education If you are working with a person for the first 
time, it is important to explain what you are 
trying to do and what they might expect to 
feel. It is often helpful if the person tries to be 
aware of the specific muscle’s location in the 
body, what it feels like at rest and through 
the range of motion. Invite them to become 
aware of how easy it is to hold the position 
as you ease into the pressure, while observ-
ing any compensation, discomfort or pain. If the energy is not at an optimal, balanced 
flow in the related meridian, the muscle may 
feel slightly weak, shaky, mushy, or it may 
seem completely switched off and fail to hold 
against any pressure. If there is any pain, 
or it isn’t easy to hold, just relax and let go 
rather than struggle to hold the position. When testing a person who is very strong or 
rigid, it is sometimes difficult to detect the dif-
ference in muscle response when the muscle is 
inhibited. With a body builder, you may direct 
them to focus on using an individual muscle. Their training and awareness often allows them 
to monitor the function of specific muscles. Counter-intuitively, it is often more effective to 
apply less pressure with a very strong person 
so that they have less to resist. Then observe 
the movement of the particular muscle that 
is unlocking. Try switching a muscle on and 
off with any facilitating/inhibiting reflex to see 
what kind of change to expect. (See “Test for 
Accurate Indicator Muscles,” pg.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
23 Learning to Test some experience, we begin to get a deeper 
sense of whether or not energy is flowing. We may also be able to feel what’s happen-
ing with our energy before we actually test 
the muscle. Some people will feel subtleties 
even if the muscle seems strong and locking 
in place, so we always want to check what 
each person feels, not just whether the arm 
moves or not. The TFH system includes 42 important pos-
tural muscles for balancing our postural struc-
ture and overall energy through the 14 major 
meridians. Many people have picked up the 
Touch for Health manual and through exper-
imentation have learned to check muscles and 
balance energy. Sometimes a self-taught prac-
titioner will use TFH in a way that looks quite 
different from the standard way used by TFH 
instructors certified through the International 
Kinesiology College. As long as TFH is used 
safely in a non-diagnostic model and it helps 
enough to be worth the time, it’s fine for peo-
ple to figure it out and apply it on their own. We get calls, letters and emails from people 
whose lives have been changed for the better 
just by picking up the book and experimenting. We really don’t know how many hundreds or 
thousands of people have found help on their 
own using the Touch for Health manual. Compensation If it is difficult to muscle-test a person, 
explain that the body may unconsciously 
compensate by adapting our posture to “win 
the contest.”  We may tense up generally or 
recruit other muscles to support the muscle 
being tested. Watch out for elbows bend-
ing, torsos twisting, hands clenching, breath 
holding etc. It may also be necessary to 
monitor your vocabulary. Descriptions such 
as “strong” and “weak,” instead of “lock” 
and “unlock,” and 
“resist” instead of 
“hold,” may create 
situations where the 
person recruits other 
muscles or mentally 
overrides the test. Assist them to relax 
and to think of the 
muscle and its posi-
tion in the body. Clear communication and 
feedback is vital, so taking the time to edu-
cate the person is worth it. What you want 
is cooperation, not competition. But many people do need some instruc-
tion and coaching to gain confidence and 
receive the full benefit of the TFH techniques. For this reason we have developed — first 
through the Touch for Health Foundation and 
now through the International Kinesiology 
College — a standard training program cov-
ering all of the protocols and techniques in 
the Touch for Health system. This training 
is available throughout most of the world 
and follows a consistent syllabus, which is 
normally taught in four weekends.

even though the system was designed for safe

therapists, physical therapists, psychologists,

ologists and other 
professional 
help-

clear and accessible 
system for both lay 
people and health 
care

to implement in a

enhancing Wellness. Depending upon the

different 
professionals adapt 
the techniques to fit

or simply comple-

found that the TFH system, in and of itself, 
is sufficient for becoming a professional 
Kinesiologist — one who earns their living Today there are many Kinesiology systems 
that build on the TFH model of energy 
and Kinesiology. There are also programs 
being developed that offer a professional 
TFH training — to add depth, background, 
guidance, practice and assessment to the 
TFH fundamentals, and to enhance profes-
sional excellence and success for professional 
TFH Kinesiologists. Contact your local TFH 
Kinesiology Association or Kinesiology train-
ing center to learn more about available 
training. (See back of book) TFH is a very clear and acces- sible system for both lay people and health care practitioners when implementing a holistic model of Wellness.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
26 ance in our posture. Then our partner may 
look at our posture, note any deviations and 
invite us to note what it might mean to us and 
about us. The body tries to come into balance 
against the force of gravity. A deviation in the 
normal posture may indicate the first stage of a 
problem or be the result of years of compensa-
tion. Recognizing deviations or distortions is 
the first step in reversing them and preventing 
more serious problems down the line. POSTURE Reading body language is one way to 
become aware of what is occurring within 
a person and what their muscles are doing. Subconsciously, we are all experts at it; we 
know and understand body gestures more 
than words. We can look at a person and 
intuitively sense how he or she feels. We can 
also think about how posture reflects literally 
or metaphorically in a person’s life. In order to distinguish deviations from nor-
mal posture that require balancing, we must 
know what constitutes good posture. Because 
of variations in the general body build, there 
will be individual postural differences. From 
the FRONT, a plumb line centered between 
the feet should go equally between the legs, 
through the navel, the breastbone, up the mid-
dle of the neck, the center of the mouth and 
nose, and through the center of the head. With 
the shoulders dropped naturally, the arms hang 
against the sides at the same length. The hips, 
shoulders, eyes, and ears are level. The knees 
should face directly forward, the feet comfort-
ably apart and at an angle of no more than 45º 
away from each other. In the BACK, the line 
should follow straight up the spine and through 
the center of the back of the head. A 14-muscle balance is a very effective 
method for making changes in posture. This 
is not only because of the energy changes, but 
also because we are making sure that the pairs 
of muscles we are testing and correcting are 
supporting the structure equally. A balanced 
posture enables the body to function efficiently 
with a minimum of strain, and reflects poise 
and beauty. When we balance muscles and 
posture, we often find attitude improves as 
well, and vice versa. Improved posture and 
attitude go a long way toward helping a per-
son feel better and stay healthy. Becoming 
aware of our posture before a balance and 
then noticing and reinforcing positive changes 
with clear feedback may enhance these effects. A line up the SIDE of the body should start just 
in front of the anklebone and pass through the 
side of the knee, the center of the thigh, the 
center of the upper arm, and the center of the 
neck and ear.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
28 The collarbones, or Clavicles, go across the 
front of the chest from the shoulder to the top 
of the Sternum, cover the first rib and form the 
two knobs at the base of the neck in the front. The two shoulder blades, or Scapulae, in the 
back are triangular shaped bones which are held 
against the back by muscles. In a relaxed posi-
tion, they partially cover the back of the 1st-8th 
ribs. T5 is about midway between the shoulder 
blades. T8 or 9 is at the tip of the shoulder 
blades. T12, the last Thoracic Vertebra, can be 
found by following the bottom of the ribcage 
around to where it attaches to the spine. UNDERSTANDING 
THE BODY 
STRUCTURE Structural energy is inherent in the body struc-
ture itself. First, let’s examine the structure 
of the spinal column, or backbone, and the 
neighboring bony parts with which we need to 
become familiar. Note that the bones provide 
some landmarks; understanding how they relate 
to one another will make it easier to find a given 
vertebral level and the corresponding spinal 
reflexes or Neurolymphatic reflexes. The area of the spine below the last Thoracic 
Vertebra, T12, is the Lumbar Region, with 
five Lumbar Vertebrae, L1-L5. The first 
Lumbar Vertebra (L1) is just below the end 
of the ribcage. L3 is about level with the top 
of the hips. L4 is about level with the highest 
part of the sides of the hipbones. L5 attaches 
to the Sacrum, a large bone in the center of 
the Pelvis, which is attached to a hipbone, or 
Ilium, on each side. At the base of the Sacrum 
is the Coccyx, or tailbone, the very lowest 
portion of the spine. The first Cervical Vertebra (C1) is at the base 
of the skull at the back of the head. There 
are seven Cervical Vertebrae in the neck, 
C1-C7. C7, at the base of the neck, is the 
last vertebra that moves with the neck, while 
T1 remains stationary. The first Thoracic 
Vertebra (T1) is usually the most prominent 
knob at the base of the neck. The muscles are all attached to the bony frame, 
and it is their pull against these attachments that 
affects the positioning and posture of the body. One end of the muscle is called the origin. The 
origin of a muscle is relatively stationary and 
usually attaches to the bone itself, in a rather 
spread-out fashion. The other end of the muscle, 
the insertion, is considered more mobile because 
it attaches to the part that is doing the moving. It is bound into heavy, unyielding tissue called 
tendons, which then attach to the bone.

The other end of the muscle, 
the insertion, is considered more mobile because 
it attaches to the part that is doing the moving. It is bound into heavy, unyielding tissue called 
tendons, which then attach to the bone. There are 12 Thoracic Vertebrae, sometimes 
called Dorsals, and each is associated with 
one of the 12 ribs. The 1st through 7th ribs 
attach to the Sternum, or breastbone, in the 
upper part of the chest. The 8th to 10th ribs 
are joined by cartilage along the lower half of 
the rib cage. The 11th and 12th ribs do not 
go all the way around the body or connect in 
the front; hence they are called floating ribs.

THE MERIDIANS Yin/Yang Energy Flow In oriental medical philosophy we learn that 
there is energy that is magnetic. Consisting of

expressed in the body as positive and negative

or male, and the negative is called Yin, or 
female. The Yin energy flows, in general, from 
the feet toward the head and the Yang from 
the head toward the feet. The body as a whole 
and in its subdivisions has both positive within 
the negative, and negative within the positive. Human beings and other living things are 
capable of self-healing in the cycle of life. This cycle is governed by universal laws, which 
are discovered and utilized by human beings to 
fulfill their purposes. These laws are physical, 
mental, emotional, chemical and spiritual. All 
of the laws work together and do not contradict 
one another. The discovery of universal laws 
is not the monopoly of any one group or any 
individual. If one makes the discovery of an 
apparent law, it may or may not apply to all 
other humans under all possible circumstances. It may, therefore, not be accepted by others as a Life Force Energy or Life Force is called “Chi” in 
Chinese, “Ki” in Japanese, while others inter-
pret it as a “universal energy.” The Hebrew

as the “breath of life,” breathed by God into Yin Meridians

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
31 points are electro-magnetic in character and 
consist of small palpable spots that can be 
located by hand, with micro-electrical volt-
age meters and with muscle testing. These 
points form connections between the positive 
and negative meridians and functions of the 
body, including the internal organs and mus-
cles. There are some 500 points that have 
been mapped and used for centuries to bring 
specific improvements in energy flow and 
human performance. the first human, who then became a living 
being. This hand of God holds the life of 
every creature and breathes the breath of all 
humanity. In the Christian tradition, the energy or life 
force is synonymous with the “Word of God” 
by which all things were made — in which was 
life — and that life was the light of mankind. That divine light gives light to every person 
who comes into the world, sustains all things, 
was before all things and holds all things 
together. In this tradition, which is my per-
sonal belief, energy or life force is not merely a 
nebulous force or impersonal universal energy. Rather, this energy is in the person of Jesus 
Christ, who is the Word, the Truth, the Life 
and the Light of the world, in whom we 
live and move and have our being. Learning 
the laws of how this energy force is used for 
wholeness and health is one of the purposes of 
becoming fully human. Discoveries by people 
all over the world help us to understand our 
intelligently designed bodies, how to conduct 
our behavior and perform our personal best. The meridian system is comprised of ten 
organ meridians, two function meridians and 
two midline meridians. The two function 
meridians are Circulation-Sex and Triple 
Warmer, both of which are generalized func-
tions of the body. Circulation-Sex, sometimes 
referred to as Pericardium, is related to the 
overall circulation of all bodily fluids. Triple 
Warmer is related to temperature regulation, 
immune response, metabolism, passions and 
survival responses. The two midline systems are Central and 
Governing. Central Meridian flows from 
the pubic bone up the center of the body to 
the lower lip. Governing flows from the tail-
bone up the spine, over the top of the head 
to the upper lip. These do not fluctuate with 
the body’s 24-hour cycle, also called vessels; 
they are more akin to storage batteries for 
the meridian energies and deal with energy 
entering and leaving the body. The Meridians Acupressure vessels, or Meridians, are located 
throughout the body. They are the pathways 
of the positive and negative energy that carry 
on some of the communications between the 
parts of the human being.

32 of the day and night, each meridian reaches a 
peak of energy, one after another, in this same 
order. When this energy flow is unrestricted, 
the body harmonizes the flow to optimize body 
functioning. Sometimes, however, the life we 
lead and the abuses we heap upon our bodies 
cause stress. This stress may be intense or so 
constant that, in effect, it “overloads” the cir-
cuit. The “circuit breaker” pops and needs to 
be reset before energy can flow properly once 
again. The indicator of whether or not this has 
occurred is the muscle test. If a muscle seems 
weak, we can go to certain spots on the body 
and press or hold to reset the circuit and restore 
proper flow. This restoration of flow is immedi-
ately indicated by a strengthening of the muscle. cases, the meridian name is the same as that 
of the name of a gross organ with which we 
are familiar. When we test a muscle and it 
unlocks, it does not mean there’s damage 
to the physical organ. We cannot say that 
a weak muscle equals a weak organ; we can 
only note that a portion of the meridian 
energy flow is blocked or constricted. A traditional Chinese physician can detect 
imbalances in the meridians by feeling the 
pulses. This is a highly sensitive touch, and 
may take 10-20 years to develop proficiency 
in all of its intricacies. TFH uses muscle test-
ing to detect some of these same imbalances, 
taking advantage of the body’s intelligently 
designed mechanisms to let us discover what 
is occurring on the inside, from the outside. Since there is no beginning and no end to this 
flow, we represent the order of the meridians 
as a wheel, or a 24-hour clock. As we go 
around this wheel, following the meridian 
lines, the flow follows this order on the body: 
     
From FACE to FEET
along outside of leg-Yang The Meridian Cycle Meridians are classified as Yin or Yang on the 
basis of the direction in which they flow on the 
surface of the body. Meridians interconnect 
deep within the torso, but we work with the 
part that is on the surface and is accessible to 
touch techniques. Yang energy flows from the 
sun and these meridians run from the fingers 
to the face, or from the face to the feet. Yin 
energy, from the earth, flows from the feet to 
the torso, and from the torso along the inside 
(“Yin-side”) of the arms to the fingertips. From FEET to TORSO
along the inside of the leg-Yin From TORSO to FINGERTIP
along the inside of the arm-Yin

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
36 THE BALANCING 
PROCESS Zip Up, Switch On and Tune In! Before we start a balancing session or any 
challenging activity, we may want to quickly 
achieve a general balance and clear random 
energetic confusion. If we “Zip Up,” “Switch 
On” and “Tune In,” it helps develop a 
mutual sense of energy flow by clearing away 
any energetic scrambling or reversals which 
might confuse the results of the muscle tests. These exercises may be performed as a short 
break from any activity to clear the mind and 
get back on track. Zip Up Central Meridian from pubic bone to just below lower lip. Zip Up TECHNIQUE This is a simple exercise we can do for a 
quick general balancing and for reducing 
unexpected results when muscle testing. For 
example, this is how we would Zip Up the 
Central Meridian which runs straight up 
the center of the body. Zipping a finger or 
the hand from the pubic bone to the lower 
lip reinforces the normal flow of energy in 
this major meridian and serves to help clear 
general energy blocks, especially related to 
energy which needs to leave the body. Zip Up: Within 3” of the body  and 3” to either side of the meridian line, is considered to be concretely within the meridian path. It is not necessary to make contact with the skin or even to touch the clothing — try to feel the energy. Trace it 3 times.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
37 Switch On Technique Part One: (left) With one hand over naval, rub K27, the 
points below the collarbone; (right) Switch hands and repeat. Switch On Technique Part Two and Three: (left) Rub above upper lip and below 
lower lip, switch hands and repeat; (right) Rub tailbone, switch hands and repeat. Switch On TECHNIQUE “Switching” (energetic reversals or other 
types of confusion) is apparent during read-
ing or writing when numbers or letters are 
visually reversed or when we mix up people’s 
names, etc. Switching often occurs when a 
person is under stress. This means that if we 
start testing and balancing without attending 
to this confusion, it may hamper our effi-
cient progress. Especially when we are first 
learning to muscle test, it is helpful to reduce 
confusing results due to switching. For this 
reason it’s a good idea to Switch On before 
a balance: Switch On Part One: Hold one hand over the navel and with the other hand rub in the depres- sions below the collar bone/breastbone junctions (K27, the endpoints of the Kidney Meridian). Switch hands and repeat. Switch On Part Two: You can get additional ben- efit by working with the endpoints of the Central and Governing Meridians and the beginning of the Governing Meridian. Hold the navel and rub just above the center of the upper lip and just below the center of the lower lip. Switch hands and repeat. Switch On Part Three: Then, hold the navel and rub the tailbone. Switch hands and repeat.

38 Emotional Stress Release (ESR) Tune In The TFH Emotional Stress Release tech-
nique, though extremely simple and easy to 
use, has been found to be profoundly effec-
tive and helpful by thousands of people, in 
almost any type of situation. Don’t forget to 
use it often and early! Since the acupuncture system of the whole

pg. 41-44; Auricular Exercise pg. 251-252.) 
This exercise helps with any range-of-motion 
issues and also helps us to focus our attention. By gently holding your own (or another per-
son’s) forehead, it allows you to feel calm,

from work or relationships, etc. A light touch on the Neurovascular points 
has a soothing effect on the meridian energy

40 TECHNIQUE In addition to balancing energy, the ESR and 
Neurovascular points also stimulate subtle 
changes in vascular circulation, particularly 
in the brain. It is helpful to engage diverse 
areas of the brain – and make the visualiza-
tion more vivid – by ask-
ing the person to con-
template aspects of their 
goals or stressful issues 
through the 5 senses. You might ask, “If your 
stress had a smell, what 
would it be? What color 
do you see when you 
think about the stressful 
issue? When you think 
about the stress, what 
sounds, voices, words do you hear? What 
physical, bodily or tactile sensation do you 
have?”  As you develop your sensitivity, 
you will be able to feel a pulse in each of 
the points. As you contemplate different 
aspects, the pulses may go out of sync — 
continuing to hold the points will bring 
them back into sync. For physical pain, hold the painful area and the ESR points simultaneously. This also sometimes gets amazing results! The ESR ESTABLISH A SAFE PLACE points When using ESR to defuse the stress of a 
traumatic experience, it may be necessary to 
set up a “safe place.” First of all, this means 
that both people are comfortable and in 
agreement about dealing with the stress in 
the current setting. You can also establish a 
real or imagined place for the person to take 
“time out” from the process if necessary. You 
can designate a particular area of the room, 
a piece of furniture or an imaginary place in 
the mind’s eye. An IM should lock when the 
person thinks about their safe place. (If using 
a “safe place,” ask the person to indicate 
when they are ready to resume the process.) 
Sometimes, if an emotion comes up and it 
is overwhelming, we can ask the person to 
think of something neutral or pleasant, such 
as “fresh bread.”  They can then choose to 
work with the emotion or set it aside for 
another time or a different setting. Some peo-
ple are comfortable working through tears, 
but we respect both persons’ right to stop or 
to not explore a given issue. are 	also the Neurovascular points. ESR can work in times of mental strain or 
mental block – tiring or challenging tasks, 
exams, interviews, overwhelming circum-
stances, confrontations, and for accidents or 
trauma, etc. It is also helpful for relieving 
stress related to past experiences or antici-
pated future events. (See additional explana-
tions of ESR for Future Performance and Past 
Trauma on pg. 258-259.)
