41 Pretests Pretest exercises are very helpful both in clear-
ing extraneous problems that can confuse our 
muscle test results and in orienting ourselves 
to each other. Especially when we’re starting 
out, it is very helpful to get a clear sense of the 
difference between a facilitated (locking) mus-
cle — one that feels strong or holds effortlessly 
against pressure — and an inhibited (unlock-
ing) muscle — one that feels weak, shaky or 
mushy. This can vary greatly from person to 
person, so it’s beneficial to establish a baseline 
of the range and kind of muscle responses to 
expect before checking the 14 meridians. Monitoring Anterior Deltoid as an Indicator Muscle. Note that we are checking primarily for energy 
flow and balance in the meridians, as indicated 
by muscle response, rather than assessing gross 
muscle strength or function. If we use the 
terms “strong” and “weak,” it is because the 
muscle normally feels weak if energy is not 
flowing to it, and it feels strong when it receives 
a balanced amount of energy. When we say 
that we “strengthen” people with a muscle 
balance, we mean that we enhance and balance 
the flow of energy so that there is improved 
muscle function and the whole person reaches 
full potential. We are not necessarily enhancing 
the muscle bulk or physical integrity of the 
muscle. muscles that are “switched off” (responding 
weak and failing to operate optimally) so we 
can “switch them on” and restore their func-
tionality. Since we associate each muscle with 
a specific energy meridian, we say that an 
imbalance in any particular muscle within that 
meridian is an indication of some imbalance 
in the meridian. Balancing a weak muscle may 
balance the meridian. We can use any muscle that is functioning nor-
mally as an Indicator Muscle (IM). Once the 
muscle is isolated, it can be used to monitor 
responses related to the energy of the person. The muscle response gives us a useful biofeed-
back mechanism that provides vital clues about 
the energetic balance or imbalance. This will 
also serve to orient us to each other and the 
kind of muscle responses to look for, increase 
our communication and self-awareness, reduce Accurate Indicator Muscle Test:

balancing. This is achieved by identifying the

42 much more than 2 inches. Your arms will stay

blockage or flow. If balancing is needed, the

important to have a convenient way of check-
ing to determine if the muscle we want to use 
as our indicator is functioning normally and 
whether its related energy circuits are clear. This way, there will be no confusion about 
the muscle response. If mushy or unlocking, refer to (pg. 193) and 
use any of the reflex points for the Anterior 
Deltoid. Stretch the skin up and down between 
the shoulder blades over T4 if both muscles 
are mushy; or, rub firmly between the 3rd, 4th 
and 5th ribs near the breastbone and beside the 
spine and lightly hold the Anterior Fontanel. This will get the energy flowing and the muscle 
will lock. The following exercise, called the Accurate 
Indicator Muscle Test (AIM), is used before 
any balancing procedure that requires an 
indicator muscle. It is also a great first exper-
iment in muscle testing. We suggest you read 
the whole muscle testing section first, and 
then try these techniques. Physical Challenge We can utilize known reflexes in the muscle 
itself to check if the messages/energy are flow-
ing as expected. At first you may find it is 
easier to see the improvement by stretching the 
muscle rather than the inhibition in response 
to pinching the muscle. To feel the very subtle 
differences, you might repeat switching off and 
on a few times. Check the Muscles First Regardless of the muscle, you first need to 
“Zip Up, Switch On, and Tune In” before you 
start any AIM test. From here, you must select 
a muscle to test. For this test, the Anterior 
Deltoid is a convenient muscle to use as an 
example. Lift your straight arms to about 30° in front of 
the body. Have your partner ease into down-
ward pressure on each arm above the wrist as if 
to move it back to the side of the body. Use just 
enough pressure to feel if it’s easy to hold each 
arm in place or if either seems weak. Try using 
less than 2 pounds of pressure for about 2 sec-
onds to see if the muscle locks without moving TECHNIQUE Physical Challenge: To momentarily inhibit the muscle, push together or gently pinch in the belly Physical Challenge: To momentarily inhibit the muscle, push together or gently pinch in the belly of the muscle and retest. It should now unlock. Stretch apart in the belly of the muscle to facilitate and retest. It should now lock.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
44 Flushing the Meridian If the Central Meridian is not functioning as 
expected based on the muscle test, we want 
to balance it or reset it to the normal base-
line. It may be reversed (switches off when 
we zip up, and switches on when we zip 
down.) It might stay switched off regardless 
of whether we zip up or down, or it may stay 
switched on regardless of whether we zip up 
or down. In any case, we will do the same 
thing: flush the meridian to clear the pathway 
and get the energy flowing. Biochemical Challenge Try a healthy snack (chewing and then holding 
it in the mouth, or even just thinking of the 
food) and test the Indicator Muscle. Compare 
with a less healthy food, which will switch off 
the IM. Just thinking of rancid grease will usu-
ally switch off the IM. Ammonia and smelly 
markers will switch off the muscle, but we can 
skip exposing ourselves to anything toxic just 
to see the effect! Flushing the Central Meridian TECHNIQUE Flushing the Meridian: Think of the meridian as a channel of water. Maybe there are leaves, rocks, dirt or debris in the channel. Flush the meridian by brushing your hand up and down several times to remove any debris, ending with two or three traces from the pubic bone to the bottom lip to encourage the normal direction of the flow. Now see how the indicator muscle responds to tracing the Central Meridian forwards and backwards. After checking our indicator muscle with the AIM tests, we can use the IM to indicate energy in its related meridian, or the energy response to different challenges.

Switching Points Second only to air, water is a primary physical 
need for survival, good performance, clarity of 
thinking and proper function of the brain and 
body. Our bodies are made up of about 70% 
water. We are dependent on the fluid flow of 
the different systems in our bodies. Every living 
cell requires water, just as it does nutrients and 
oxygen. Water acts as a solvent in the body 
and the purer it is — free from minerals, soft-
eners, and pollutants — the more body toxins 
can be released into it and the more nutrients 
it can carry to the body’s cells. There are no 
enzymes in rocks: our mineral consumption 
should come from the living foods we eat — 
vegetables, fruits, grains, and meats. Animal 
trainers know to give their animals the purest 
possible water; human beings certainly deserve 
the same kind of treatment. We can use “circuit-location” to see which 
switching points need to be rubbed, just as we 
can circuit-locate any of the other reflex points. Even if we have already switched on, if we 
circuit-locate the points and check an indicator 
muscle, we might find there is more we can do 
to balance the related energy. Touch the points 
for Switching On and check an indicator muscle. TECHNIQUE Circuit-Location: Touch the points below the collar bone (K27) and check IM. Then touch above and below the lips and check IM. Then, touch the tailbone and check IM. For any points that switch off the IM, rub the points and recheck. Checking for switching using an indicator 
muscle is a good idea before each balance. 
(See section on Circuit-Location for more 
information pg. 63) A healthy person should drink a minimum of 
1/3 of an ounce of water for every pound of 
body weight and at least double that in times

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
47 the TFH system, we are mainly looking for 
blocks or imbalances in meridian energy, as 
indicated by particular muscles that unlock 
or feel weak when we test them. We can 
use any one or a combination of the fol-
lowing strengthening or energy balancing 
techniques. The basic “balance-as-you-go” 
approach described on pg. 68 is easy to learn 
and apply. It has had profound results for 
thousands of people, throughout the world. There is no such thing as a Touch for Health 
“treatment.” TFH does not cure or heal any-
thing. In fact, no “healer” ever heals; it is the 
body itself that performs the healing process. Each cell contains the complete blueprint for 
constructing an entire person. All of the cells 
in our bodies (except those in the central ner-
vous system) are replaced at least every seven 
years. Some authorities now believe that all 
elements are replaced every nine months, 
and many cells are replaced much more 
quickly than that — cells of the intestinal 
tract live only 36 hours. Nothing is beyond 
the power of the body’s own healing process. Our understanding is not great enough to 
know why or even precisely how this process 
works. So we balance the energy flow and the 
posture, thus optimizing the capacity for the 
body to heal itself. Begin strengthening with the spinal reflex 
for bilateral muscle inhibition, or with the 
Neurolymphatic reflex if the muscle only 
unlocks on one side. Work with a given 
reflex until some kind of change is noticed in 
the muscle response (usually some improve-
ment). Then challenge the reflex and con-
tinue with the other reflexes as needed in a 
set order. (See pg. 60-62 for explanation of 
Challenging.) Also consider any metaphori-
cal meanings related to goals and imbalances 
by drawing on possible symbology of muscle 
function/test, meridian/organ functions and 
5-Element Metaphors. Muscle Test Positions Most of the tests can be done with the person 
standing, sitting or lying face up (supine), Always retest a muscle after touching, hold-
ing, or massaging the reflex points. Expect 
at least some improvement! If there doesn’t 
seem to be any change, review the details of 
the technique and repeat with more care and 
presence. After detecting some improvement, 
challenge the reflex to see if more of the same 
or some other reflex will be of additional ben-
efit. In the rare case where the muscle actually 
seems to get weaker, we check for over-energy 
in the meridian (pg. 71: Pulse Check.) Standing and Lying (Supine) Test for Pectoralis Major Clavicular (PMC)

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
48 When there is bilateral weakness, the Spinal 
Reflex often corrects both sides, so it is pref-
erable to start with this technique. If a mus-
cle is only weak on one side, begin with the 
Neurolymphatic massage points. Begin by 
checking the muscles in meridian order. Test 
the muscles on both the right and left sides. If 
both muscles are weak, find the appropriate 
spinal level to be massaged. If you are not sure 
of the exact location of the vertebral levels, 
refer to the diagram on the following page. Use the pads of 3 or 4 fingers to massage not 
only the specific vertebral level but also above 
and below on the adjoining spinus processes, 
moving the skin about one inch at a time over 
the bones for 5-10 seconds on each area. although when lying down, a few tests must 
be done face down (prone). When becoming 
familiar with the tests and muscle action, 
try to duplicate the positions shown. Once 
these have been learned, as long as the mus-
cle is given an accurate test with the other 
muscles sufficiently immobilized, the testing 
position itself – lying, standing or sitting – is 
not important. A massage table is a helpful 
accessory for regular use of the techniques 
but certainly isn’t necessary. The floor is 
good, especially with a soft rug or pad. A 
bed is usually too soft, since the testing 
pressure will go into the mattress instead of 
against the muscle action, making an accu-
rate evaluation difficult. After stretching the skin over the spine, recheck 
the muscles to see if they lock. Usually both 
muscles will feel stronger and lock easily in 
place. If only one muscle is strengthened, it 
means additional reflexes should be massaged. Continue with the Neurolymphatic reflexes 
and retest. If the muscle improves, challenge 
the reflex (Challenging pg. 60) and follow 
through with any other strengthening tech-
niques required, in the order presented. Spinal Vertebrae (Bilateral) Reflex Technique The spine and the central nervous system that 
it protects have many reflexes within and sur-
rounding them. One of those reflexes resets 
the muscles when they are bilaterally inhibited, 
i.e., the same muscle tests weak on the right 
and left sides of the body. This external reflex 
is located in the skin and can be activated by 
stretching the skin up and down over the spi-
nal vertebrae tips — the actual bumps you feel 
along the center of your backbone. Don’t slide 
across or chafe the skin. The bones provide some landmarks that will 
make it easier to find a given vertebral level. The 
first Cervical Vertebra, C1, is at the base of the 
skull.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
50 tip of the shoulder blade. T12, the last Thoracic 
Vertebra, can be found by following the bottom 
of the ribcage around to where it attaches to 
the spine. The first Lumbar Vertebra, L1, is just 
below the end of the ribcage. L3 is level with 
the hips and L4 and L5 are below that. Use firm, deep massage with the fingertips, 
searching in the general area indicated for 
particularly sore spots. Pay particular atten-
tion to the sore spots for 20-30 seconds. The 
amount of tenderness can be an indication 
of the extent of the problem. If the person 
becomes too uncomfortable and wishes to 
stop, it is important that their judgment be 
respected. They might be able to do the 
massage on themselves, which is also effec-
tive. The tenderness tends to decrease as 
balance returns to the muscle and meridian. Retest the muscle, which should show at 
least some improvement. Challenge by hold-
ing one hand over one of the reflex points 
and testing the muscle again. If the muscle 
becomes inhibited on the challenge, move on 
to the Neurovascular points. Neurolymphatic Massage Points While the blood stream operates under pres-
sure — up and down in a closed circuit — the 
lymphatic system flows only in one direction. The lymphatics act as a drainage system of 
the body. Lymph also carries proteins, hor-
mones, and fats to all the cells. It produces 
antibodies and makes up one quarter of the 
white blood cells. There are twice as many 
lymph vessels and lymph as there are blood 
vessels and blood in the body. The energy to the lymphatic system is reg-
ulated by what we call Neurolymphatic 
reflexes, located mainly on the chest and back. These reflex points act like circuit breakers or 
switches that get turned off when the system is 
overloaded. The location of the reflex points 
does not seem to correspond to the positions 
of the lymph glands, but they are related. The 
Neurolymphatic Message Points vary in size 
from a little pellet to a small bean, and they 
occur either alone or in groups, sometimes 
scattered over an entire muscle. Some are 
palpable — they can be felt — others are not. They are usually tender spots, and the ten-
derness is normally greater in the front of the 
body than in the back. The reflexes, or areas 
that are found to be the most sore, often seem 
to be the ones in greatest need of massage. Challenging the Neurolymphatic point for PMC

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
52 Neurolymphatic Massage for Pain Relief Neurolymphatic Release There is an alternative to the “exquisite 
pain” or ticklishness that can occur during 
deep massage of the Neurolymphatic points. Simply hold a Neurolymphatic point and 
either the beginning or ending of the related 
meridian (whichever end is more convenient 
or least invasive) for at least 30 seconds. This 
will have the same energy balancing effect — 
clearing the meridian and strengthening the 
related muscle — but actual deep massage 
may provide the greatest benefit. Neurolymphatic massage is particularly help-
ful when there is pain or soreness in the mus-
cles in general, or in a specific muscle. If there 
is any pain in the test range of motion or upon 
testing, consider this an important indication 
of imbalance or inhibition in the related energy 
flow. Work the related Neurolymphatics and 
gently recheck. The pain may disappear and 
the muscle may be strengthened, or there might 
simply be a reduction of pain in the range of 
motion or test. Challenging will indicate what 
else might be beneficial. A massage of all of 
the Neurolymphatic points is also good for 
general soreness, illness etc. We interpret this as clearing blocks and bal-
ancing the energy related to the muscle and 
meridian. Because these reflexes are particu-
larly related to the lymphatic system, we may 
also see improved function in that system and 
the related organs. Light pressure/massage 
or “feathering” over the Neurolymphatic 
reflex points will have a temporary inhibiting 
effect on the meridian and muscle, causing 
the muscle to unlock when retested. This 
relaxing effect on the muscle can also relieve 
pain, spasm or cramps. Even the inhibiting 
technique tends to return the muscle to a bal-
anced tonus and after a little while the muscle 
will test strong. For muscle pain and sore-
ness, the usual deep massage is very effective 
in restoring comfort and function, and it may 
also reduce recovery time. Challenging the Neurovascular point for PMC Neurovascular Holding Points Neurovascular Holding Points are found, 
primarily on the head and are usually bilat-
eral. Using simple contact with the pads of the 
fingers (they are held very lightly) apply just 
enough pressure to slightly tug the skin. Try 
to feel for distinct pulses on each side and see 
if they come into sync. Even the points located 
on the midline can be split into two halves 
that will have distinct pulses. Usually 20 or 30

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
54 seconds of holding is sufficient, but they may 
be held longer. Even if no pulse is felt, simply 
recheck the muscle after 20 to 30 seconds and 
it will usually be strong. Challenge to see if 
more needs to be done. These points were adapted from a system 
in which they were associated with blood 
circulation, particularly in the brain and to 
particular organs. We may see improvement 
in mental clarity and function, circulation 
and organ function. If you develop your sensitivity, a slight pulse 
can be felt at a steady rate of 70-74 beats 
per minute. This pulse is not related to the 
heartbeat, but is believed to be the primitive 
pulsation of the microscopic capillary bed in 
the skin. After a pulse has been felt on both 
sides and has become synchronized, then the 
Neurovascular points may be held for about 
20 seconds or up to 10 minutes, depending 
on the severity of the problem. Challenge 
by holding one hand over one of the reflex 
points and testing the muscle again. If the 
muscle becomes inhibited on the challenge, 
move on to tracing the meridian. It is beneficial to activate diverse areas of 
the brain when using this technique. While 
holding the points, visualize your desired 
outcome, focusing on each of the five senses 
in turn. As you shift your attention to differ-
ent senses, the pulses may go out of sync and 
then return to rhythm after a moment. We 
particularly use the Neurovascular points on 
the forehead for emotional stress release, but 
all of the Neurovascular points tend to be 
very effective for mental or emotional strain.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
55 Tracing the Meridian We use a technique called Tracing the 
Meridian on the person with an open palm 
or with the fingertips to help balance the 
energy in the meridian and clear any blocks. You do not have to actually touch the per-
son. Try to stay within 3”, either off to the 
side or even above the skin and clothing, to 
be most effective. Although the meridians 
may only be illustrated on one side of the 
body, they are located bilaterally and should 
be traced on both sides of the body, except 
for Central and Governing Meridians, which 
travel the midline. It is sometimes more ben-
eficial when tracing is done with more care 
and precision on the meridian pathway, but 
a general approximation of the pathway is 
still very effective. Challenging the Stomach Meridian Reflex by circuit locating either 
endpoint of the Meridian. Meridian Massage/Dance TECHNIQUE When we trace all of the meridians at once, 
in the order of the 24-hour cycle — either 
on ourselves or on each other — it is called 
a Meridian Massage. When done in a group, 
it is called the Meridian Dance, following the 
details on pages 34, 35 and the procedure 
on page 273. See also, Simple Pain Control by 
running or flushing the Meridian (pg. 273) and 
Meridian Walking (pg. 280) in the Additional 
Balancing Techniques. Tracing the Meridian: To balance a particular muscle, trace the related meridian pathway, with an open hand, within 3 inches of the body, 2 or 3 times, on both sides of the body. CHALLENGE Challenge the Meridian Tracing by holding one hand on the beginning or end point of the merid- ian and testing the muscle again. If the muscle becomes inhibited on the challenge, move on to the reflexes related to the origin/insertion of the muscle or the Acupressure Holding Points.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
56 Acupressure Holding Points cannot be felt — it may take practice and patience When a muscle is inhibited we can use the 
Acupressure Holding Points to draw energy 
into the related meridian from the meridian 
that naturally feeds it, strengthening the 
related muscle. To control the energy flow 
and assure it is balanced, make a connection 
with the meridian that naturally controls it. If there is too much energy in the meridian 
(over-energy), then draw energy away from the 
over-energy meridian to the meridian where it 
naturally flows. Again, to control that energy 
flow and assure that it is balanced, make a 
connection with the meridian that naturally 
controls it.  (See Acupressure Holding Points 
Theory, pg. 245 for more information.) — the muscle is usually affected after about 20-30 seconds of light pressure and will be strong when retested. Sedation: To balance a muscle/meridian with over-energy, do the same as explained above but with the Sedate Points, drawing off excess energy with the first set of points and then controlling the flow with the second set of points. CHALLENGE Challenge the Sedation Points by placing one hand over either of the first two points and retesting the muscle. TECHNIQUE Tonification: To strengthen a muscle using the Pain Tapping Points Acupressure Holding Points, use the Tonify Points The Acupressure Holding Points are also 
used for pain control. One of these points 
is identified as the Pain Tapping Point. When looking at the first set of tonification 
points, the one on the related meridian will 
be the Pain Tapping Point. Tapping this 
point is very effective for pain that is related 
to over-energy in the meridian. (See Pain 
Tapping on pg. 280 along with Pulse Check 
on pg. 70 and Acupressure Holding Points 
Theory on pg. 245 for more information 
about sedating over-energy.) on the same side of the body as the muscle that is weak. Hold the first arm and leg points at the same time, one with each hand. Place as many fingers on or around the point as will fit, using the pads of the fingers rather than the fingertips. Maintain light pressure for about 30 seconds or until a pulse (70-74 beats per minute) is felt in the leg. Then place the hands on the second two points and hold these, again waiting for 30 seconds or until a pulse is felt. The location of these points may vary from person to person, but using many fingers helps insure locating the exact spot. Even if the pulse

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
57 Origin/Insertion Techniques HARD HEAVY PRESSURE When a muscle has been strained or over-
worked, the circulation and lymph systems 
have been overloaded. This turns off the mus-
cle’s strength, and causes minute tearing of the 
fibers. It is this tearing that causes the stiffness 
and pain that follow irregular exercise. Situations may arise where you need to stimulate 
a muscle at the muscle Origin and Insertion. In 
TFH, we achieve this through Jiggling and 
Hard Heavy Pressure. Occasionally — particu-
larly in cases of strain, spasm, or injury – it may 
be necessary to work more specifically with the 
two sensory feedback systems that are built into 
muscles: the Spindle Cell Mechanism and the 
Golgi Tendon Apparatus. TECHNIQUE Hard Heavy Pressure: For this type of injury, it is beneficial to apply heavy pressure against the attach- JIGGLING ment areas. Normally, this is done at the origin, but Sometimes a weak muscle must be manually 
stimulated before the effects of the energy 
balancing techniques can be seen. If the 
various reflex techniques are applied yet 
no noticeable change is found, locate the 
origin of the muscle (the end attached to 
non-moving bone) and its insertion (the end 
attached to moving bone). the muscle insertions may also be massaged. This tends to re-establish the contacts, like pinning up a wisp of hair that has gone astray. TECHNIQUE Jiggling: Place your fingers at each end; jiggle the ends back and forth. In most cases this will suffi- ciently “wake-up” the muscle and cause a change in its response to the reflex technique. You may also jiggle the entire muscle and have the same effect. These are some of the first techniques that Dr. Goodheart applied to dramatically change 
muscle response and function, leading to the 
development of Applied Kinesiology.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
58 GOLGI TENDON APPARATUS THE SPINDLE CELL MECHANISM As its name implies, the Golgi Tendon 
Apparatus is located in the tendons at either 
end of a muscle. Receptors occur all along 
the origin and insertion of the muscle. The Spindle Cell mechanism, which is located 
in the belly of the muscle, senses the relative 
length and rate of change of muscle fibers, and 
sends this information into the nervous system. TECHNIQUE TECHNIQUE Spindle Cell Mechanism: To strengthen a muscle Golgi Tendon Apparatus: To affect muscle tone, through this mechanism, apply firm pressure in the place your fingers along both the origin and the belly of the muscle, pressing toward the muscle insertion of the muscle. Press firmly back and forth ends. Work with the thumbs, beginning in the cen- along the direction of the muscle fibers. ter of the muscle and stretching the muscle. STRENGTHENING
STRENGTHENING Push on the 
O/I toward the 
center/belly of 
the muscle. Push away 
from the 
center of the 
belly of the 
muscle. RELAXING Push on the 
O/I toward 
and along the 
attachment(s) 
to the bones. RELAXING Push toward 
the center of 
the muscle 
next to each 
other. CHALLENGE To challenge the Golgi Tendon Apparatus, touch 
the origin or the insertion of the muscle and retest 
the muscle. Only one hand is required from either 
the testor or the testee to touch the point while the 
muscle is tested. CHALLENGE To challenge the Spindle Cell mechanism, touch the belly of the muscle and retest the muscle. If a muscle has been strained and is placing 
too much tension on the tendons, the Golgi 
Apparatus will send a message to the brain. The 
brain then turns off the energy to that muscle 
to avoid injury. The tendons may in fact have 
pulled away from the bone slightly, and firm 
pressure toward the bones of attachment may 
help the tendon to reattach properly. With the 
danger eliminated, the apparatus is reset. This stretches the Spindle Cell receptors, which 
in turn send a message to the brain, “This muscle 
is too long.” The brain replies by sending more 
nerve impulses to the muscle, causing it to tighten 
up and become shorter. This may be done in 
reverse to relieve a cramp/spasm, by manually 
shortening the belly of the muscle, pressing 
together in the direction of the muscle fibers.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
59 Double-Checking and Challenging Your 
Reflex Techniques THE MUSCLE FEELS BETTER AFTER 	
WORKING THE REFLEX If even slight improvement is noticed, 
Challenge the muscle by placing one hand 
over the reflex point just worked and retest the 
muscle. (See more details in the Challenging 
section, which follows on page 60.) If any technique appears not to work, do not 
hastily give up and apply a different reflex or 
technique. (There is probably a reason why 
you have not found the expected response.) 
Usually, if both people make an effort to 
increase their awareness of the technique 
details and are sensitive to slight changes 
or improvements, the techniques will work. When working with balancing the subtle 
energy, small changes may be all that are 
necessary for a big improvement in the whole 
person. If a technique doesn’t seem to work 
at first, give it another chance; become more 
subtle in your awareness and double-check 
your performance of the technique. THE MUSCLE SEEMS TO RESPOND THE SAME 
AFTER THE REFLEX If the muscle response doesn’t seem to change 
in any way in response to a particular reflex, 
increase awareness of the subtle differences. Encourage the person being tested to be more 
present in the process. Invite them to involve 
their awareness in the location and the feeling 
of the particular muscle. Double-check the test 
position, range of motion and amount of pres-
sure used in the test. If there is still no change, 
jiggle the muscle to wake it up. Finally, review 
your pretests for accurate indicator muscles 
and the other pretests, including the tips for 
unexpected muscle response. Taking the time 
to perform the pretests will help a lot. If you 
notice even a slight improvement, consider this 
a positive indication even if the muscle is not 
completely locking. If even slight improvement 
is noticed, then challenge the reflex. On the Other Hand… There is no danger if you decide to stop in 
the middle of a balancing or technique. If 
time or comfort require you to skip, post-
pone or discontinue a particular technique or 
balancing, you will still get the benefit of the 
techniques you do complete. However, after 
working with a reflex, expect some change 
in the response of the muscle being checked. Normally the muscle feels stronger, holds in 
place with less effort or feels like “you’re not 
pushing as hard.” The muscle should feel bet-
ter, although sometimes it feels just the same; 
infrequently it will feel even worse. THE MUSCLE SEEMS TO GET WEAKER If the muscle seems to get weaker after working 
a balancing reflex, check for over-energy with 
the Pulse Check (pg.

THE MUSCLE SEEMS TO GET WEAKER If the muscle seems to get weaker after working 
a balancing reflex, check for over-energy with 
the Pulse Check (pg. 71). If there is over-energy 
in the related meridian, use the Acupressure 
Holding Points for sedation to draw the excess 
energy into another meridian (pg. 56). Recheck 
the muscle and it will now probably be strong.

60 Challenging next strengthening technique for that muscle 
and repeat the retest/challenge process. If the 
muscle is equally strong, even if not 100% 
on the challenge, consider the strengthening 
complete and proceed to the next muscle test. After working a particular energy-balancing

using one will often eliminate the need for 
further reflexes. Challenging the reflex you 
just used allows you to decide if another 
reflex would be helpful at that time. Basic Order for Balancing Reflexes As with most of the TFH system, the order 
of the strengthening techniques was devel-
oped based on ease of use, simplicity and 
efficiency. The order was established based 
on thousands of balancings to produce the 
best results in the shortest amount of time. Normally, the spinal reflex is the quickest 
one to work on; the Neurolymphatic reflex 
takes a little longer, the Neurovascular reflex 
longer still and so on. 2. NEUROLYMPHATIC (NL): Challenge by touch-

only about 50% of the time when only

efficient to continue directly with the NL.)

63 Circuit-Location (CL) are held, this is an indication that working the points When working the reflexes in a set order, you 
may work through various reflexes for one mus-
cle/meridian while balancing various aspects of 
the meridian energy, or fine-tuning the balance 
on more subtle levels, like “peeling an onion.” will balance the energy. If the muscle stays weak, then the given points are not involved. Repeat the process with all of the related reflex points, using the same points you would use to challenge. Now you know which reflexes will be most effective for Circuit-Location is another effective way to 
find out which reflexes are involved and thus 
using only the reflexes that are active at the 
moment. All the reflexes affect the whole per-
son, so by this method you may be starting 
with the primary reflex involvement and save 
some time. The method of use is described in 
the Technique box following. strengthening the muscle in a specific case. Indications The left hand column of the Muscle Reference 
pages includes information that discusses the 
action of the muscle, how it relates to other 
muscles to maintain or distort posture and 
what factors might be involved in persistent 
imbalance of the muscle, including possible 
dietary or pathological factors. For those 
working in the diagnostic model, these may 
be additional clues for developing a diagnosis 
or treatment plan. However, in the holistic 
model these factors are considered to see 
what is true for the individual person and 
are then used to work towards positive goals 
rather than for treating named diseases. TECHNIQUE Circuit-Location: Just as the reflex points are touched after having been worked to see if it was sufficient, touch the points before working them to see if work is needed at all. When touching them, a circuit is completed to see whether they will con- tribute to balancing the energy. We call this circuit locatimg the involved reflex. It may be that only the meridian is indicated. By starting the balancing first with tracing the meridian, time is saved compared to Muscle Function first working the spinal reflex, Neurolymphatic points, Understanding Muscle Function assists in feel-
ing and developing awareness of muscles and 
energy. Biofeedback through muscle monitor-
ing will be more effective if one is knowledge-
able and aware of the function of the muscle 
being checked. The functions of the muscles 
also provide clues about which muscles to 
check related to postural imbalances, pains, 
or other symptoms. The TFH preference is Neurovascular points, and then the meridian. If a muscle is weak on both sides, touch the related spinal reflex points and recheck for balance on both If a muscle is weak on both sides, touch the related spinal reflex points and recheck for balance on both sides. text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
64 made through the balancing process. If we see 
improvement in specific symptoms that relate 
to a diagnosed disease, check back with your 
physician for any appropriate adjustment in 
treatment or medication. to begin by balancing the 14 meridians as a 
whole, then look at the mechanics of the pos-
tural muscles and physical symptoms. Consider 
the anatomy, location and function of the mus-
cle, as well as the gesture of the muscle test, to 
generate symbolic and metaphorical possibili-
ties in the context of your life and goals. The term, “allergies,” as used in the Touch 
for Health manual, may be more accurately 
described as “energetic sensitivities.” It is 
not used to refer to the medical definition of 
an allergy, but rather to foods or substances 
that inhibit energies, determined by checking 
the indicator muscle. While these substances 
may or may not be actual allergens by med-
ical definition, muscle testing has been very 
effective in increasing our awareness of the 
quality of our diet. Checking the effect that 
certain foods have on energy allows us to 
apply common sense and supplement our 
diets with foods that energize — or abstain 
from other foods — and observe how we feel 
as a result. Association with Organs and Functions In the TFH system each muscle relates closely 
with a particular meridian. The meridians have 
organ names and are associated with the func-
tion represented by that organ. This is so on 
a global scale within the whole person as well 
as within each cell. In addition, each muscle 
may be associated with a certain specific organ 
or physiological function. However, this does 
not determine the status of the actual organ 
itself, as every organ requires both a balance of 
meridian energy and a balanced physiological 
functioning in the whole person. These associa-
tions are best understood as global functions of 
the whole system, rather than literal indications 
of the function of a specific part, which may or 
may not correspond in a given person. Foods For Balancing Certain foods/nutrients have been found to 
consistently aid in balancing the meridians/
muscles. However, any food may be effective 
for a person at a given time. Try tasting a 
small amount of the food to see if it facilitates 
the muscle. This food then may be used as a 
balancing reflex for the muscle/meridian and 
as nutritional support for ongoing balance. Likewise, abstaining for a time from foods that 
inhibit energy is often beneficial. (See Food 
Testing and Balancing, pg. 301-302 for more 
information.) Relation to Certain Allergies/Diseases The diseases mentioned are not given for the 
purpose of diagnosis or treatment per se; they 
are included to offer some clues that may 
increase your awareness of your specific expe-
rience.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
65 Related Muscle Testing At the bottom of the muscle testing photos is 
a list of related muscles that may be checked 
to deal more directly with structural/muscu-
lar imbalances. First do at least a 14-muscle 
balancing to get the energy flowing in the 
whole system. Then go back and retest any 
muscle suspected to have pain or discomfort. 
(You may find that these have already gone 
out of balance or show inhibition related to 
continued discomfort.) Balance these muscles 
again using the usual reflexes. Then test 
and balance the related muscles. Check any 
muscles related to the same meridian. This is 
often very effective in relieving subtle or nag-
ging pains. (See Postural Awareness pg. 26 & 
27, Postural Analysis pg. 283 and Reactive 
Muscles pg. 290-298 for more resources.) Cerebrospinal Fluid Reflex: Gently stretching the scalp from center 
of skull to sides while slipping fingers over the skin. It needs to be 
repeated at least 5 times to be effective in most situations. TECHNIQUE Simple Cerebrospinal Fluid Reflex Cerebrospinal Fluid Reflex: First, check the When checking the abdominal muscles, we 
may find them consistently inhibited; they 
may even switch off shortly after balanc-
ing. Since the back depends greatly on the 
Abdominals for stability, low back pain may 
be involved. This problem can be aggra-
vated by wearing tight hats, headphones, 
sweatbands, etc, all of which inhibit the 
microscopic movement of the cranials. They 
become “jammed,” thus inhibiting the move-
ment of cerebrospinal fluid around them. This is reflected in abdominal inhibition. Abdominals (usually weak) and strengthen them using the usual reflexes: spinal reflex if bilat- eral, Neurolymphatics, Neurovasculars, and so on. Then take a walk around the room to acti- vate the Abdominals. Retest. If the Abdominals have switched off, gently stretch the skin apart at the top of the head. This will help balance the Abdominals so that they stay switched on. Place your fingertips along the Sagittal Suture on the top of your head and gently pull apart so there is a tug- ging feeling on the scalp. This should be done five or more times. Retest Abdominals — they should now lock. Take a little walk as before. Retest to confirm that the Abdominals stay switched on.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
66 moment of insight. This may be a highly 
transcendent, sudden, miraculous moment of 
enlightenment, like those attained in prayer 
or meditation. It may also be a step-by-step 
process of development through small, every-
day insights as we deal with our problems 
and our life’s work as we seek our Telos, the 
purpose in life we were born to fulfill. TOUCH FOR HEALTH 
METAPHORS On each Meridian introductory page, you’ll 
find some background information about 
the functions and concepts associated 
with the Meridian and its related Element. Each Meridian is associated with one of 
the five Elements from Traditional Chinese 
Medicine: Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water. This ancient symbolism may yield all kinds of 
insights into your life. This edition of the Touch for Health Manual 
is the first edition to integrate the Metaphors 
related to the muscles, meridian/organ func-
tions, and the traditional Chinese Five Elements 
following the publication of the Touch for 
Health Pocketbook with Chinese 5-Element 
Metaphors, in Spring of 2002. There is also a 
new TFH Metaphor Workshop, which com-
plements the standard TFH training. The TFH Metaphors help access mental/
emotional and sensory/associative aspects of 
one’s positive goals and related imbalances, 
making the balance more meaningful, pro-
found and effective. On the following Meridian pages there are ques-
tions to ask yourself relating to the Meridian 
and the Element, along with a table of specific 
aspects associated with each Element. There 
are more questions on the muscle reference 
pages, related to the possible metaphors asso-
ciated with the muscle anatomy and function 
as well as the gestures suggested by the specific 
motions of the muscle tests. The 5-Element Metaphors, the Organ 
Function Metaphors and metaphors derived 
from the test motions/muscle functions offer 
a rich resource for exploring the meaning 
of our experiences, our feelings, our imbal-
ances and our goals. Using the metaphors 
helps us to verbalize and think about the 
many possible aspects of our goals and the 
related imbalances. When we think about a 
metaphor related to an imbalance indicated 
by a muscle test, we often have that “Aha!” Observe what the metaphors/questions asso-
ciated with the muscle/meridian/Element 
bring to mind. Think of it and if you want 
to, say it out loud, as this alone may be 
enough to correct the imbalance. It is also 
beneficial in conjunction with any touch 
reflex.  (For more information see section on 
Metaphor Balancing Protocol, pg. 315-334.)

68 form on pg. 90, and the reference section over-

positioning of the muscle by explaining where 
the muscle is in the body and how the muscle

MUSCLE/ENERGY 
BALANCING Say, “Ready… Hold,” then ease into the pres-
sure and ease off the pressure within 2 seconds. Did the muscle lock or unlock? Was it easy 
or difficult to hold? Any extra effort or com-
pensation? Balance-As-You-Go Protocol The basic energy balancing protocol in the 
TFH system is to balance at least one muscle 
of the 14 meridians, which helps to balance 
the overall system. The easiest way is to 
check and balance one muscle pair at a time, 
then move on to the next pair. Later, you 
may learn to check all the muscles, assess 
several different types of energy patterns and 
balance the whole system with a key muscle/
meridian. (See Assessment Balancing follow-
ing the reference section pg. 232). Was the test pressure comfortable — too 
much or too little? Does direction of the range of movement 
need to be changed slightly? Decide together whether the muscle response 
seems less than optimal. If the muscle holds 
easily in place, proceed to the next of the 14 
muscles. If not, use the balancing reflexes 
as described in the previous sections. Retest 
& challenge (pg. 60) each reflex. Repeat for 
each muscle. Remember: the individual is the 
authority! Permission to Test Ask the person for permission to touch them 
and inquire whether there is any reason 
why they shouldn’t be muscle tested and/
or whether there is any injury or issue to 
be aware of. Complete all pretests (pg. 41). Adding a goal is recommended (pg. 72). Now there is at least enough energy in all 
14 meridians. To assure that there is not 
too much energy in any meridian, check the 
Pulse Points (pg. 71) or the Alarm Points (pg. 235). If there is any residual over-energy, 
Sedate (pg. 56) the related meridian with the 
Acupressure Holding Points. Check and balance each muscle pair in order of 
the meridians starting with Central Meridian, 
Governing Meridian, and then Stomach 
Meridian (as indicated on pg. 70, on the session Notice and appreciate how you feel better 
after the balance including 0-10 subjective 
measurements for your goal, any pains, etc.

If a muscle unlocks, test the other muscles related to that meridian which are shown “inside the wheel” on pg. 70. 6. Recheck the goal statement with the IM and any subjective 0-10 measurements. 78 HOW TO USE THE 	
MUSCLE REFERENCE 
SECTION (PGS. 92-229) How you use the reference section will vary 
according to your philosophy, approach and 
purpose. If you are totally new to TFH, then 
we suggest you read through the entire 90 
pages of the first section of the book and then 
refer back to it as you try out the techniques. The techniques are presented in a systematic 
sequence to help build a more holistic appre-
ciation of your body, your energy and your 
life. Of course, many people with previous 
knowledge have been able to apply the mus-
cle tests, reflex points, and other energy bal-
ancing techniques to their existing practice 
with great success. If you follow the specific 
instructions of this book, you will not only 
learn to check and switch on muscles, you 
will begin an ongoing process of self-aware-
ness and self-care that will contribute to 
your accomplishment of your goals and your 
enjoyment of life. healing. With TFH and your talent, skills, 
and dedication to carry out your mission, 
you not only assume more responsibility for 
your destiny, but you will help others to live 
a healthy life. These protocols will assist you 
in having a more fulfilling life. They are defi-
nitely worth learning and using. First of all, we recommend that you CUT 
OUT THE TABS that run along the right side 
of each page. If you cut out the area bounded 
by the dotted line on each of the Muscle 
Reference pages, you will find you now have 
a list of all the muscles, running down the 
right hand side of the reference section. By 
placing your thumb on the meridian you 
want to find, you can flip to the reference 
page quickly. The Reference Section is organized and sorted 
by Meridian in the order of the 24-hour cycle 
of energy followed by each Muscle related to 
that meridian. The muscles are then divided 
according to the usual testing priority. The human being is capable of self-healing. As you become aware and conscious of this, 
you can uncover the energy blockages that 
may be preventing continuous growth and

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
79 Each meridian includes at least one muscle. In TFH, we reference only 42 muscles out of 
the 600 or so muscles classified in the human 
body for use in our model of meridian energy 
balancing. We recognize that our model 
is incomplete – as are all models of health 
enhancement and disease care – since we can 
only partially observe a whole person at any 
one time, as we described in the “Pyramid of 
Health” section. Spinal Reflex point is abbreviated under-
neath the page number. Each meridian is described as being part of 
one of the 5 Elements that represent the func-
tions of the human being. As the meridians 
are presented, you will find that each one 
provides a brief description of the function 
and a 5-Element Metaphor. Each meridian 
has at least one muscle to test for meridian 
function along with other related muscles. Since the muscles are related to a specific 
meridian, the 5-Element Metaphors apply to 
each muscle associated with that Element. You will notice that on pg. 92 there is a series 
of Thumbnail Illustrations of the 42 muscle 
tests, which are also presented in Meridian 
Order. You can perform a quick 14 muscle 
check by testing just the first muscle of each 
meridian – these are the Basic 14 Muscles 
that we use for each meridian. If you find 
one of the basic 14 muscles inhibited, first 
strengthen it, then check the additional mus-
cles related to the same meridian. You can 
also check and balance all 42 muscles. The 
page number for the Muscle Reference page 
is listed in the upper right hand corner of 
each thumbnail illustration, and the related The Reference Section starts with the Central 
and Governing Meridians and their related 
muscles, which we recommend be tested and 
facilitated before any of the other meridians 
are evaluated. These two meridians are found 
in the center of the body. The other 12 meridi-
ans are bilateral: that is, they are found on both 
sides of the body. We test a muscle related to 
a specific meridian on both sides of the body 
(14 meridians mean at least 28 muscle tests).

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
80 Meridians The first page of each Meridian will introduce 
you to the meridian and describe the function 
and associated Element. The two pages fol-
lowing will provide the specific details of each 
meridian with supporting illustrations and 
graphics. These characteristics apply to all the 
muscles related to that Meridian. 5-ELEMENT METAPHORS Color	
Violet: What might the color violet mean? (Also the nurturing aspect of 	
	
	
shadow and darkness) Season	
Creative, nurturing aspects of the 5 seasons: How does the cycle of 	
	
	
changing seasons nurture you or not? Climate	
All beneficial Climate: Is the climate and atmosphere enriching your life? Odor	
Nurturing through smell: How do smells nurture you – or how do you 	
	
	
nurture your appreciation of smell? ON THE LEFT HAND MERIDIAN PAGE Taste	
Creative aspects of taste: Are the tastes of your meals and experiences 	
	
	
nurturing your creativity and vitality? You will find the 5-Element Metaphors 
Chart, which gives the metaphors asso-
ciated with the Element and can be used 
as starting points for consideration. These 
relate to the characteristics that a traditional 
Chinese Medicine professional would dis-
cover when testing the pulse. We have taken 
these Oriental concepts and translated the 
pictographs to our understanding of them in 
a Western context. You may add your own 
mind-images and how they relate to improv-
ing your life, as you desire. Emotion	
Nurturing aspects of Emotions: Do your emotions in general flow and 	
	
	
enrich your life or are they blocked or toxic? Sound	
Creative aspects of sound: Does sound have and enriching role in your 	
	
	
life or are you sensitive to noise, easily annoyed? Fortifies	
The integrated body as a whole: How is the overall structure and 	
	
	
interrelation of all of your body parts? Does your body support and enrich your life? Personal Power	
Creative & procreative Powers: Do the changes of season and stages 	
	
	
of your life allow you new ways to fulfill your purposes? Do you find inspiration 	
	
	
in changing circumstances and your personal evolution? Faith/Worldview	
Creative and nurturing aspects of Belief: Do your beliefs and thinking 	
	
	
processes support your vitality and creativity? In addition to the 5-Element chart, you will 
find the following: Peak Function – The time of day at which 
the Meridian is most active. Energy Flow – This will be either YIN or 
YANG to indicate the energy direction. Related Muscles – A list of all the muscles 
related to that Meridian with the basic 14 
muscles listed first. Questions to Consider – Some questions 
related to the possible symbolic meanings of 
your issues and goals.

84 Learning to Perform a Muscle Test ON THE RIGHT HAND MUSCLE PAGE Our experience has proven that the most 
effective way to learn how to perform a mus-
cle test is by feeling the test first performed 
on you by another person. Following your 
instructions, another person can test your 
muscle for the “locking” effect, as you hold it 
in position. This allows you to become aware 
of the intrinsic sense of that muscle as part 
of your whole body. You are becoming the 
expert on the changes that take place in you, 
with the assistance of the other person. Here you will find a new graphic showing the 
muscle, superimposed on the skeletal struc-
ture, with the points of Origin and Insertion, 
the Neurolymphatic Massage Points, and the 
Spinal Reflex Points. The Neurolymphatic 
Points are shown as heavy dots or bands on 
the body, which indicates the approximate 
areas to look for tenderness on the person 
begin tested. This page also describes the muscle and 
its location of origin and insertion, where 
to “feel the muscle” and the associated 
Metaphors. First, become aware of the muscle moving the 
body part through its range of motion, and 
then place the body part in the test position 
(usually where the origin and insertion are as 
close together as is comfortable). If this causes 
discomfort, consider it inhibited/weak, an indi-
cation of a lack of balance in the associated 
meridian function. Do not cause more discom-
fort by having your assistant apply pressure 
to separate the origin and insertion by testing. NEUROLYMPHATIC 
MASSAGE POINTS Origin Insertion As your partner applies pressure, as noted 
on the Pressure Target on the Muscle Test 
Positions chart, you will notice whether you 
can easily lock the muscle in position, feeling 
strong. If the muscle tests strong – facilitated 
– on both sides, we assume that there is no 
problem with the balance of the associated 
meridian. When there is a lack of balance 
in the meridian associated with the muscle 
being tested, the muscle test will reveal a 
weak, mushy, or wobbly out-of-control feel-
ing in the muscle, apparent to oneself and the 
assistant. SPINAL 
REFLEX

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
95 Central Meridian The Central and Governing Meridians 
relate to the balance of energy in terms of the 
utilized amount leaving the Soul (the whole 
person) compared to the amount of energy 
retained and stored for future use. These 
two Meridians are closely associated with 
the Lung Meridian for their similar func-
tions of intake and release of energy as 
with the breath cycle in which subtle 
energy and air are drawn into and 
released from the Soul. nation of your internal wisdom. These are the 
functions of doing some things automatically 
(or autonomically), while doing other things 
consciously; doing more than one thing at a 
time and changing what you’re doing as cir-
cumstances change. What makes the Central and Governing 
Meridians unique is that they are related to 
all of the Elements and Organs in a global 
sense. With these global qualities, they work 
with all of the elements together, particularly in 
the creation (sheng) cycle. The Supraspinatus 
and Teres Major muscles are used as indica-
tors of the Central and Governing Meridians 
respectively and are the first two muscles that 
are balanced (restored to a facilitated state, 
testing strong). The associated metaphor of the 
Central Meridian, also known as 
“The Conception Vessel,” sym-
bolizes release and letting go of a 
previously useful idea, thing, emo-
tion, heuristic truth, worldview etc. Releasing of little, or subtle things 
can be of great importance to allow 
new things to occur in your life. Think 
of exhaling, and letting go of things 
that once served you, but can now be 
released. When the releasing and storing functions of 
the Central and Governing Meridians are 
in balance, it becomes easier to balance the 
other meridians as well because of the way 
the excess energy carries over and provides 
additional reinforcement where it is needed. This will allow the greatest change in your 
life as you will start attracting people and 
opportunities that support and contribute to 
your personal fulfillment. The Central Meridian is related to the 
Brain Functions and the innate intel-
ligence of the whole Soul in each of 
the cells and in the overall coordi-

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
96 Central Meridian PEAK FUNCTION:  Night/Midnight 5-ELEMENT METAPHORS ENERGY FLOW:  YIN ELEMENT METAPHOR: Global
The Central Meridian relates to the 
Creation/nurturing (sheng) functions of 
all the meridians and globally to the 
whole system. Governing and Central 
are the 2 main meridians that have global 
properties of new energy entering the 
system, stored energy, and used energy 
leaving the system. Color	
Violet: What might the color violet mean? (Also the nurturing aspect of 	
	
	
	
shadow and darkness) Season	
Creative, nurturing aspects of the 5 seasons: How does the cycle of 	
	
	
	
changing seasons nurture you or not? Climate	
All beneficial climate: Is the climate and atmosphere enriching your life? RELATED MUSCLES
	
Supraspinatus Odor	
Nurturing through smell: How do smells nurture you – or how do you 	
	
	
	
nurture your appreciation of smell? QUESTIONS TO CONSIDER
CENTRAL MERIDIAN 
METAPHOR Taste	
Creative aspects of taste: Are the tastes of your meals and experiences 	
	
	
	
nurturing your creativity and vitality? Emotion	
Nurturing aspects of emotions: Do your emotions in general flow and 	
	
	
	
enrich your life or are they blocked or toxic? E	 As the major Yin channel, Central Meridian 
relates to receiving, accepting and integrat-
ing. What do you need to receive, take in or 
accept to reach your goals? What energy 
or resources do you need? Or do you need 
to release, reject, or let go of something? Are you receiving or accepting something 
toxic or negative for you? Sound	
Creative aspects of sound: Does sound have an enriching role in your 	
	
	
	
life or are you sensitive to noise, easily annoyed? Fortifies	
The integrated body as a whole: How is the overall structure and 	
	
	
	
interrelation of all of your body parts? Does your body support and enrich your life? E	 How is the integrated whole of your life 
supporting and creating vitality, or is your 
life as a whole not creative and nurturing 
to you as a person? Personal Power	
Creative & procreative powers: Do the changes of season and stages 	
	
	
	
of your life allow you new ways to fulfill your purposes? Do you find inspiration 	
	
	
in changing circumstances and your personal evolution? E	 How is your brain functioning? Are you act-
ing wisely? Are you using your intelligence 
for your own good? Faith/Worldview	
Creative and nurturing aspects of belief: Do your beliefs and thinking 	
	
	
	
processes support your vitality and creativity?

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
98 1 Central Meridian 	 Supraspinatus MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
Assists Deltoids, initiating movement 
of the arm away from the body. This 
muscle also helps to hold the arm in 
the shoulder socket and could have an 
impact on shoulder problems. Supraspinatus C1
C2
1 STANDING / LYING FACE UP 1. Position with the straight arm about 30˚ above the body, slightly to 
the side with palm facing the groin. INDICATIONS 
People who do a lot of thinking — 
working at a desk, or work that does 
not involve big muscles, such as driv-
ing — are subject to the kind of 
brain fatigue which will affect the 
Supraspinatus. 2. Stabilize by placing your other hand on the shoulder. 3. Pressure is on the forearm, above the wrist, to push it towards the 
groin. 4. If you feel a muscle contracting or straining elsewhere, check 
the position of the test or consider the Supraspinatus inhibited. Unless a muscle is fully facilitated, it may recruit other muscles to 
compensate. Children who have learning difficulty 
will often show Supraspinatus inhibi-
tion and could benefit from balancing. Anxiety and emotional stress may also 
be involved. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P On top of the forearm, above the wrist. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Sardines, propolis, bee pollen, spiru-
lina, raw honey, water. P P o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS
Frontal Eminences, 
between the eyebrows 
and the hairline. Also, the Anterior 
Fontanel. Hold these points, particularly the 
Frontal Eminences, until the muscle locks 
while thinking about the problem if there is 
emotional stress involved. o o Test Also:  Deltoids, Upper Trapezius, Popliteus, and Pectoralis Major Clavicular and Sternal if the 
Supraspinatus feels weak or painful. The Pectoralis Major Clavicular may also be involved in emotional strain.

text:
Touch for Health 
Letters from Around the World! Touch For Health is Profoundly 
Simple and Simply Profound I Stumbled Into Touch For Health 
and It Changed My Life! As a teenager I suffered back, neck, and 
shoulder pain. All the doctors commented 
on my “very crooked spine.” No helpful 
suggestions were made except to “live with 
it.” Years later I was told by a specialist 
that I needed surgery or I would be in a 
wheelchair by age 40. I refused the surgery. 
(Now 60 and no wheelchair.) In the late 70’s, as a young naturopath, I 
luckily “stumbled” over the TFH book sticking 
out of the bookshelves in a London bookshop! Touch for Health dramatically changed my life 
and professional career. I have helped so many 
people with TFH, taught TFH to many more and met 
and shared good times with the most different 
and wonderful people from all over the world. TFH has considerably improved my life and health, 
including a spinal condition I’ve had since early 
childhood. The turning point came in 1985 when a friend 
who lived 900 miles away invited me to visit. She had just completed TFH1 and wanted to 
give me a balance. Exhausted from the plane 
trip I went straight to bed on arrival. Next 
day I received my first 14 muscle balance. Within 2 days I was jumping on a trampoline 
and my delighted husband could not believe 
the change. That very weekend I did my first 
TFH class, primarily to learn how to help 
myself. It changed my life! I shared TFH with my fellow naturopath, Matthias 
Lesch, who was also fascinated with the beneficial 
outcomes. After a while, to make TFH more 
available to the public, we introduced Touch For 
Health to the staff of a big German magazine. They were so inspired by the experience that 
they began writing a series of articles about 
Touch For Health. The response of the readers was 
overwhelming. Our first workshop immediately sold 
out. The feedback about the results people were 
getting with their friends, family, colleagues, 
clients etc. was so much more than we could have 
expected. So we offered workshop after workshop 
and to cope with the demand, Matthias, Helga, 
Susanne and I decided to form the Institut für 
Angewandte Kinesiologie and a publishing company. Eighteen years later I’m still deeply 
involved. I have had the privilege of working 
with hundreds of clients and seeing the 
profound changes TFH has made in their lives. As the saying goes, Touch For Health is 
profoundly simple and simply profound. Pauline Hogben
South Africa Today- more than 20 years later - our Institute 
is a place where people love to come to learn 
and to meet each other.
section: 2. Stabilize by placing your other hand on the shoulder.

Pauline Hogben
South Africa Today- more than 20 years later - our Institute 
is a place where people love to come to learn 
and to meet each other. We conduct more than 350 
seminars a year with presenters and Kinesiology 
methods from all over the world. For me it is 
just a miracle. How lucky I was to “stumble” over

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
101 Governing Meridian The Governing Meridian is 
connected with all the other meridians 
including the Central or Conception 
vessel. It is a storage vessel for excess or used energy before this energy is 
released with the breath. Prior to using 
the other muscles as indicators of imbal-
ances in the Soul (the whole person), it is 
important to uncover/release this stored 
energy by considering the burdens that a 
person is literally or figuratively carrying. The Governing Meridian is associated 
with the function of standing and Spine 
Function. It may also be related to Pineal 
Function that relates to the day/night 
cycles of light and darkness. The Governing Meridian is tied to the 
Central Meridian in that they both 
relate to the balance of energy in terms 
of the utilized amount leaving the Soul 
(the whole person) compared to the 
amount of energy retained and stored 
for future use. This was described in 
the Central Meridian and can be refer-
enced on page 95. The Governing Meridian relates to all 
of the other meridians and thereby all 
of the elements, and so to each aspect 
of the 5-Element Metaphors in a global 
way. Governing Meridian relates to all 
of the elements together, particularly in 
the control/destruction (Ko) cycle.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
102 Governing Meridian PEAK FUNCTION:  Day/Noon 5-ELEMENT METAPHORS ENERGY FLOW:  YANG ELEMENT METAPHOR: Global
The Governing Meridian relates to the 
Destruction/Control (Ko) functions of 
all the meridians and globally to the 
whole system. Governing and Central 
are the 2 main meridians that have global 
properties of new energy entering the 
system, stored energy, and used energy 
leaving the system. Color	
Violet: What might the color violet mean? (Also white light (sunlight), all colors 	
	
	
together) Season	
Controlling or Destructive aspects of the 5 seasons: How does the cycle of 	
	
	
changing seasons control your life and your decisions? What is destructive, or 	
	
	
needs to be let go of with the changing of seasons? Climate	
Structural or harmful aspects of climate: Is the climate and atmosphere dictating  	
	
	
your life? Is there something harmful to you in your general environment or climate? Odor	
Controlling and destructive aspects of smell: How do smells affect you? Are 	
	
	
they overwhelming to you or do they serve a valuable purpose? How do you 		
	
	
cope with the smells in your life? RELATED MUSCLES
	
Teres Major Taste	
Controlling and destructive aspects of taste: Are you controlled by your tastes, 	
	
	
or are you able to balance your desires and cravings with your wisdom and intelligent 	
	
	
choices? Do you have a taste for any foods or activities which are destructive? QUESTIONS TO CONSIDER
GOVERNING MERIDIAN 
METAPHORS Emotion	
Controlling and destructive aspects of emotions: Do your emotions in general 	
	
	
flow and enrich your life or are they blocked or toxic? E	 What transitions are you coping with, or do 
you need to make? Sound	
Controlling and destructive aspects of sound: Are you able to structure and 	
	
	
manage the sounds and music of your life, or is there discord and harmful 	
	
	
	
words or sounds in your life? E	 What subtle or dramatic shifts in energy 
allocation are you making or do you need 
to make? Fortifies	
The integrated body as a whole: How is the overall structure and interrelation of 	
	
	
all of your body parts? Does your body support and enrich your life, or are your 		
	
	
purposes thwarted by the shape and function of your body? E	 How do you feel about nighttime vs. day-
time? E	 What is your favorite season? Personal Power	
Controlling and destructive powers: Are you able to stay organized and keep 	
	
	
structure in your life with changing circumstances, seasons and stages of your 	
	
	
life? Is your personal evolution hampered by circumstances? Are you 	
	
	
	
appropriately exerting control and even your destructive powers? E	 Are you carrying something that has

text:
Touch for Health 
Letters from Around the World! My First Encounter with Kinesiology It was in May 1977 when on vacation in California. My wife aggravated an old sports injury and a 
friend referred us to a chiropractor who used 
Applied Kinesiology techniques. He gave us a copy 
of Dr John Thie’s book Touch for Health so that 
we could learn to maintain the corrections he had 
made. When I heard Dr Thie DC lecture, I took his TFH 
class (with 250 Registered Nurses) and made the 
decision to become a TFH Instructor. Of all the 
Complementary and Alternative Medicine courses 
I had seen or participated in, TFH was the most 
holistic, self-auditing and intriguing. On my return 
to New Zealand I started teaching TFH courses, as 
well as using the material in my medical practice. In 1981 I became the TFH Instructor Trainer for 
the South Pacific and trained as an Applied 
Kinesiologist. Together with my wife Joan, who also 
became a Trainer in 1982, I have taught hundreds of 
Instructors to teach others TFH’s simple, effective 
methods for empowering families to have a greater 
sense of responsibility for their own health care. TFH has changed our lives dramatically. Twenty 
years later, while still a medical doctor, I 
am now Dean of the International College of 
Professional Kinesiology Practice overseeing 52 
colleges teaching a 4 year Diploma course in 
Kinesiology to over 1200 students, in 6 languages. Through Touch for Health, Dr. John Thie has opened 
a wonderful opportunity for any person to discover 
their innate, God-given gift for helping others 
through healing touch. It is a privilege for me to 
be a part of John’s dream of bringing the simple 
techniques of kinesiology to people everywhere and 
to offer those who desire it the opportunity to 
take the next step and become a professional in 
the field of energy medicine. TFH and Metaphors Help 
Psychologically, Emotionally & 
Physically As a Clinical Psychologist, I continually 
search for effective and enjoyable ways of 
facilitating positive change and maintaining 
productive vision and behavior. For many 
years I have incorporated TFH Kinesiology, 
enhancing the benefits of my work. For me, 
the key aspect is the Awareness developed 
through introspection, dialogue and muscle/
energy balancing. Sometimes the TFH balancing 
is the necessary catalyst for shifts in 
posture and attitude which allow us to move 
from Awareness to positive life changes. Sometimes the Awareness of the larger context, 
or specific incidents or patterns in our 
lives, is needed to allow our energy balancing 
to really work and “hold.” Alice Vieira, Ph.D 
Clinical Psychologist
Specialized Kinesiologist Bruce A J Dewe MD NZRK MICAK
www.ICPKP.com
section: 2. Brace front of shoulder on opposite side. Push elbow downward.

summary: Touch for Health en Applied Kinesiology als holistische benadering voor zelfzorg en gezondheidsempowerment.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
107
E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Stomach Meridian The Stomach Meridian func-
tion involves receiving potential nutrients 
and initiating the digestive functions. The 
35 million cells in the stomach have 
multiple functions. The Stomach 
Function involves taking in new mate-
rials and new ideas, mixing them and 
temporarily storing them for further 
assimilation. It is a moment in 
the cycle of seeing what is avail-
able and tagging it for use in 
other functions of the Soul. The Stomach Meridian is par-
ticularly associated with the 
mouth as the beginning of 
the digestive system, but must 
necessarily involve the sense of 
smell, vision, etc. As it is often 
said, digestion truly begins 
in the brain and the Stomach 
Function has a strong mental/ 
emotional component as well as a 
dietary component. We come into 
first contact with potential nutri-
ents and new ideas through what 
we see, smell or hear and digestion 
occurs best when it involves a full 
appreciation with the five senses. Sometimes we don’t see what 
we’re taking in (watch what we eat), or we swallow something with-
out chewing, or without realizing that it’s 
poison. Therefore, we may need to reject, 
or vomit out, a food, idea or energy. Even 
though the process can be disturbing, we 
can feel much better for it! ELEMENT METAPHOR The Earth Element metaphor corresponds 
to the foundation, the ground, the soil, 
the dirt. The components that form the 
Earth (salts, minerals and ores), sym-
bolize Metal which arise from the Earth 
Element. The Earth Element is controlled 
by Wood which is symbolized by the roots 
or trees holding the earth in position. The 
Earth Element in turn controls water like 
a container, giving it form, as in a lake or 
a river. The Earth element is associated 
with the Season of Late Summer, and is 
said to be a time of transition not only 
between Summer and Autumn, but also 
between each of the seasons. The direc-
tion associated with the Earth Element 
is not North, South, East, or West, but 
Center. Thus, the 5 Elements are some-
times represented with Earth Element at 
the center, with the other four Elements 
at the cardinal directions.

5 3 Stomach Meridian Pectoralis Major Clavicular MUSCLE FUNCTION
This chest muscle helps bend and turn 
the arm at the shoulder. Contracting 
this muscle also elevates and opens 
the chest. MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS Pectoralis Major Clavicular T5
3 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE UP INDICATIONS 
This muscle is particularly associated 
with the Stomach Meridian Function 
(and emotional function) of process-
ing and digesting incoming material. We prefer to start balancing with the 
Stomach Meridian after the Central 
and Governing Meridians because the 
emotional/digestive component is so 
frequently a crucial component in our 
goal-balancing process. 1. Position the straight arm 90˚ from the body, palm out and thumb
	
toward the feet. 2. Stabilize opposite shoulder. 3. Pressure is on the forearm to bring the arm 45˚ down and out from 
	
the body. Down and out on the top of the 
forearm. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P FOODS FOR BALANCING
Especially for people with allergy related 
problems or sensitivity. Vitamin B as 
found in wheat germ, whole grains, liver 
and brewer’s yeast, tripe. Chew food 
well and avoid sugars and sweets,espe-
cially before meals. Often hydrochloric 
balance is disturbed. P
P NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS
Frontal Eminence, 
usually a bulge on 
the forehead between 
the eyebrows and 
the hairline. Hold 
these points until 
the emotional stress no longer affects the 
muscle. o
o Test Also:  Latissimus Dorsi, Pectoralis Major Sternal, Popliteus, Abdominals  and Sartorius If there is 
pain in the Pectoralis, check the Middle Trapezius. Emotional tension may also be the result of mental 
strain involving Supraspinatus.

112 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Stomach Meridian Levator Scapulae MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
This muscle has its origin in the first 
four cervical vertebrae. It has a major 
role in keeping the head held up and 
even. Levator Scapulae C5
T8 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE UP INDICATIONS 
Inhibition in these muscles can result 
in the head dropping forward, and 
or twisting or tilting to one side. Chiropractic adjustment of the neck 
may be necessary. 1. With elbow bent and forced down against the side of the hip and 
with head tilted back towards elbow on same side. 2. Stabilize the shoulder on the same side. 3. Pressure is against the inside of the upper arm near the elbow to 
move the arm away from the side. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Inside of the upper arm near the elbow. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Vitamin B as found in wheat germ, 
whole grains, liver and brewer’s yeast 
is helpful. Usually the hydrochloric 
acid balance is disturbed. Eating tripe, 
rare meats, chewing foods well, and 
avoiding sugars and sweets (especially 
before meals) are beneficial. P NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS
Frontal Eminence, the 
bulge on the forehead 
between	 the eyebrows 
and the hairline. Hold 
these points lightly 
until the emotional stress no longer switches 
off the Indicator Muscle. o
o P Test Also:  Rhomboids, Neck Muscles, Teres Major and Minor, Pectoralis Major Clavicular, and 
Latissimus Dorsi, especially if the Levator Scapulae is painful.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
114 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Stomach Meridian Neck Muscles MUSCLE FUNCTION MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS Anterior Neck Flexors
These muscles are found in the front and 
sides of the neck. They help to hold the head 
up and keep the ears and shoulders level. Anterior Neck Flexors C2 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE UP 1. Bring the head forward, (bracing between the shoulder blades if 
standing.) Posterior Neck Extensors
These muscles are found in the back and 
sides of the neck. They help to hold the 
head back over the shoulders. If they are 
inhibited, the head tends to pull forward 
like a turtle sticking its head out. 2. Pressure is against the forehead to extend the head. 3. Check also with flexed head turned 10˚ and 45˚ to each side, 
exerting pressure each time at the highest point of the forehead. Posterior Neck Extensors C2 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE DOWN INDICATIONS 
They are particularly vulnerable to injury 
from whiplash. They are also related to 
the functioning of the sinuses, which 
are important in drainage from the head 
and scalp as well as immune responses. Sometimes associated with allergies and 
Asthma. Headaches and shoulder tensions 
can stem from the head balancing improp-
erly on the spinal column, because the 
neck forms an “S-curve” due to weak 
muscles in the front. 1. Bring the head back, (bracing at the top of the chest if standing). 2. Pressure is against the back of the head to push it forward. 3. Check also with head turned to each side. Apply pressure to the forehead or back 
of head. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P Note: With sinusitis, place 
person on 35º slant with the 
head down while balancing. CAUTION: If the person is uncomfortable about having their neck muscles tested, do not 
test, but show them the correction reflexes and notice if moving the head through the range 
of motion is more comfortable after working the reflexes. STANDING OR SITTING P
P
P FOODS FOR BALANCING
Vitamin B regularly – as found in wheat 
germ, whole grains, liver, yogurt and brew-
er’s yeast. With sinusitis, organic iodine in 
kelp and raw or rare fish can be helpful. P P LYING P
P
P
P
P NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS Ramus of the jaw, level 
with the mouth. o Test Also:  Pectoralis Major Clavicular, Sartorius, Gracilis, Soleus, and Gastrocnemius.
