text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
202 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Liver Meridian PEAK FUNCTION:  1-3 AM 5-ELEMENT METAPHORS ENERGY FLOW:  YIN ELEMENT METAPHOR: WOOD Color	
Green:  Is there enough new growth in your life, or are you giving birth to more 	
	
	
new ideas, projects, etc. than can be sustained? E	 Do you have enough structure in your 
growth so that you can achieve your 
purposes or do you need to allow yourself 
more freedom in your growth to be ful-
filled? Season	
Spring:  What in your life needs to be revived, renewed, or reborn? What seeds 	
	
	
do you need to plant? E	 How does the image of green growing 
things relate to your life and your goals? Climate	
Wind:  Do you need “a breath of fresh air,” a “Spring cleaning,” or are you 		
	
	
being blown away by uncertainty and change? E	 Do you have enough roots to sustain your 
growth, and enough fuel to sustain your 
passions? Odor	
Rancid:  What do you need to let go of that was useful, but is now rancid? Do 	
	
	
you feel stagnant physically, emotionally, mentally or spiritually? RELATED MUSCLES
	
Pectoralis Major Sternal      
	
Rhomboids Taste	
Sour: Has something that was good gone bad? Do you need to make  lemons 	
	
	
into lemonade? Emotion	
Anger: Is your anger directed appropriately (yourself, a person, a thing)? Can 	
	
	
you feel outrage, or passions in general? QUESTIONS TO CONSIDER
LIVER MERIDIAN METAPHORS Sound	
Shouting:  Do you need to shout/voice  your feelings and ideas? Are you 	
	
	
	
“blowing hot air,” out of self-importance, fear or insecurity? E	 How are you handling multiple responsibil-
ities? Fortifies	
Ligaments: Do you take precautions? Do you need to “push the envelope”? Running on reserves or secondary systems? E	 Are you open to too many things and 
becoming overwhelmed/toxic, or do you 
need to absorb more, literally or figura-
tively? Personal Power	
Birth:   What do you need to let out, to give birth to? Need a fresh start? Starting too many new things? E	 What needs detoxification in your life, 
what do you need to let go of? Faith/Worldview	
Infancy/Early Childhood or Intuitive-Projective Faith:  Is there only one 	 	
	
	
correct  viewpoint? Do you use personal intuition, and dream imagery to find 	
	
	
meaning? Are you dwelling in your own dream-world, ignoring cause and effect, 	
	
	
assuming that what seems right to you will be accepted by others? E	 What do you need to bring into your life 
that will be cleansing, purifying or nourish-
ing?

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
204 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 12 Liver Meridian Pectoralis Major Sternal MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
Moves the arm in, turning and drawing it 
forward. Pectoralis Major Sternal INDICATIONS 
Problems associated with it are glaucoma 
and spots in front of the eyes, as well as 
liver conditions. All the blood that that 
absorbs food from the intestinal tract goes 
directly to the liver where detoxification 
takes place. The liver is one of the few 
organs that, if damaged or partly removed, 
can regenerate itself. Poisoning from toxic 
fumes, alcohol, and rancid foods is detox-
ified in the liver. Overloading it can pro-
duce long lasting headaches which often 
respond well to repeated balancing of the 
Pectoralis Major Sternal. 12 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE UP T5 1. Position the arm straight forward and level with the shoulder, palm turned out, thumb toward the feet. 2. Standing position, stabilize on either shoulder as needed. 3. Lying position, stabilize opposite shoulder. 4. Push arm outward and upward at the forearm. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Against the forearm upwards and 
outwards 45º. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Liver, and foods containing vitamin A 
(green leafy vegetables, parsley, green 
peppers). Avoid fried foods and sweets 
which contain fats, alcohol, carbonated 
drinks and caffeine. P P o
o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS On the natural hairline, 
about 1 1/2” to each 
side of the midline. Test Also:  Anterior Deltoid and Popliteus.
section: 4. Push arm outward and upward at the forearm.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
206 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Liver Meridian Rhomboids MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
The Rhomboids are in the back of the 
shoulders and they pull in and turn the 
shoulder blade. They are used with the 
Levator Scapulae and are rarely found 
weak. Rhomboids SITTING / STANDING T5 1. With the elbow bent and held against the side of the ribs, the 
shoulder stabilized. INDICATIONS 
The Rhomboids are related to the liver and 
may be affected by any condition which 
might affect the Pectoralis Major Sternal. 2. Pressure is against the inside of the upper arm to pull it away to 
the side & slightly forward (similar to the Levator  test, but with 
the shoulders kept level). FOODS FOR BALANCING
Liver and foods containing vitamin A 
(green leafy vegetables, parsley, green 
pepper). Avoid fried foods and sweets that 
contain fats, alcoholic beverages, carbon-
ated drinks, and caffeine. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Outward against inside of arm. P o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS Anterior Fontanel, the 
baby’s soft spot on the 
top of the head. Test Also:  Levator Scapulae, Anterior Serratus, Trapezius, Anterior Deltoid, and Latissimus Dorsi

text:
Touch for Health 
Letters from Around the World! his Bladder meridian. By means of the Meridian 
clock we brought his energies into balance and 
strengthened his weak muscles with the help of 
the NL points. Meanwhile in forehead holding I 
made him talk about why he struggles to meet 
the expectations, and I told him to let go of 
his anxiety and begin to take steps towards 
self-realization. The next time he visited me 
there was only a little difference between 
the length of his legs. After the Step-test 
correction he received the Tibetan energy octet 
as reinforcement. I told his mother to let go of 
her perfectionism. (It turned out that she’s been 
suffering from a persistent constipation since 
her nursery days. Later I worked with her: her 
Colon-meridian needed backing.) One month after 
the first visit the child was walking steadily, 
the difference in the length of the legs 
disappeared and never returned! “It Is In Giving That We Receive”  
	
 — St. Francis of Assisi I started to study kinesiology in 1995 with a 
Dutch Instructor Aria den Hartog. In the same year 
I became a Touch for Health instructor. I had 
the honor of taking part in a Masterclass in the 
Netherlands at John F. Thie. I got to know Aria 
as an instructor with outstanding personality, 
I considered her as my idol. When I first met 
John, I immediately felt him very close to myself. His informal, warm and friendly personality, and 
his consistent attitude and tremendous knowledge 
fascinated me. For me, he represented the GREAT 
TEACHER, the ideal Master, who incorporates 
humanity and knowledge at the same time. Since 
than I have participated in some more of his 
lectures, and the light of his character covers 
the students to this day. I can only express my 
gratitude and love by using and teaching Touch for 
Health, in order to make it available to anyone. I could go on with telling you how I eliminated 
the jetlag effect by using the meridian clock, 
or how my veterinary surgeon student treats and 
heals his horses and other animal patients. The 
animals let you know their emotional problems 
via the muscle test. They prefer and like very 
much the meridian strengthening. Racehorses 
recompense the continuous strengthening of their 
Governing meridian with an improvement in their 
results. They sometimes even exact the holding 
of different NV points. John asked me to write down one of my dramatic 
cases that I have experienced during my work as a 
therapeutic, or one which has been encountered by 
my students. I think that the method has brought 
dramatic changes into the life of each of my 
students! The disappearance of a headache after 
a few minutes’ massage applied to an NL point, 
the vanishing of a child’s constipation and the 
wetting of his pants are dramatic changes of 
life.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
209
E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H Lung Meridian The Lung Meridian performs 
a vital function in Touch for Health. While survival may be difficult without 
water and food for more than a week 
or two, we usually cannot live without 
air for more than about three minutes. Each lung is an essential organ that 
serves the primary function of respira-
tion and the exchange of gases (mainly 
oxygen and carbon dioxide), operating 
as the foremost and fundamental source of life energy as well as an important 
channel of elimination. The lungs also 
supply the air flow necessary to speak, 
sing, and make other vocal sounds. In 
addition to drawing in air (oxygen), the 
Lung Meridian Function is said to draw 
in or release Chi, and regulate the status 
of the Chi in the whole Soul. ELEMENT METAPHOR The Metal Element Metaphor corre-
sponds to the hardened, condensed 
materials that form within the earth. The Earth Element gives birth to Metal, 
symbolized by the condensed ores, min-
eral, or salts which form within the 
earth. Metal in turn gives birth to Water, 
which is symbolized by the condensation 
of water on a metal surface. Metal is 
controlled by Fire, which can first melt 
and purify, then forge and transform 
into a new specific shape for another 
purpose. Metal controls Wood just as 
a metal blade can chop and transform 
wood into many useful shapes. The 
Metal Element may be associated with 
hardness used to protect yourself, the 
“armor” you wear, the tools or weapons 
you carry, or the metallic objects you 
possess for  adornment or decoration.

ity to regulate breathing. Sometimes if

adjustment may be necessary. T3

side is held. 4. Pressure is against the forearm to bring the arm toward the feet. PRESSURE

Weakness will make it difficult to raise 
the arm. It has bursae under it, between it 
and the bone, which can become inflamed 
and even have calcium deposits in these 
water sacs. Consider any pain getting into 
position, or against pressure, the same as 
inhibition. Lung problems such as bronchi-
tis , pleurisy, pneumonia, congestion, and 
flu will usually affect the Deltoid. 1. Hold arm out to side, bent elbow level with shoulder. Stabilize at 
the shoulder. 2. Pressure is against the elbow to push it down to the side of the 
body. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Just above elbow. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Food rich in vitamin C (green pepper, cit-
rus) and eating lung tissue and spleen is 
beneficial. Increased water intake may also 
be indicated. P P o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS Anterior Fontanel, the 
baby’s soft spot on the 
top of the head. Test Also:  Teres Major, Supraspinatus, Pectoralis Major Clavicular, Pectoralis Major Sternal

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
218 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Lung	Meridian Diaphragm MUSCLE FUNCTION
The Diaphragm separates the chest cav-
ity from the abdominal cavity and is 
the chief muscle used in breathing. It is 
essential to comfortable speaking and 
singing. MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS Diaphragm T12 SITTING / STANDING / LYING FACE UP 1. Take a deep breath and hold it in for at least 40 seconds. After 
working the reflexes, time will usually increase by 1/3 to 1/2. INDICATIONS 
When the Diaphragm is not functioning 
properly, it is associated with unusual 
symptoms because of the many neu-
rological centers near it, as well as 
the holes for the esophagus, aorta and 
vena cava (large blood vessels), the 
vagus nerve and the large lymphatic 
vessel. When functioning ideally, the 
Diaphragm  assists in the function of 
these systems. A disturbance in the 
muscle balance of the Diaphragm can 
cause breathing difficulties, hiccups, and 
reduce breath holding time. Its malfunc-
tion is a great mimic of many symptoms 
of other conditions. 2. Alternatively, Circuit-Locate the Diaphragm by placing two fingers 
over the Diaphragm at the tip of the breastbone (the Xiphoid 
Process) and testing ANY previously strong indicator muscle to 
see if it becomes inhibited. You can also test to each side of the 
breastbone. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Circuit-Locate Diaphragm and test IM. P FOODS FOR BALANCING
Food rich in vitamin C (green pepper, 
citrus) may be needed. o P NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS
Anterior Fontanel, the 
baby’s soft spot on 
the top of the head. Test Also:  Teres Minor, Psoas, Abdominals and Neck Muscles.

text:
Touch for Health 
Letters from Around the World! TFH Has the Answer! TFH: Nature’s Blessing Studying medicine at the University of Freiburg, 
I had the impression that there should be more 
answers to health problems than my education in 
conventional medicine could give. In 1987, I 
came into contact with Kinesiology and Touch for 
Health. I have an 83 year-old mother who has 
various health ailments. I have been working 
with different doctors to help alleviate 
her discomfort through diet, good water, 
supplements, appropriate exercise and 
medication. When I learned that Touch for 
Health was open to anyone, I was immediately 
attracted to it. The weekend TFH courses with 
Matthew Thie and the weekly meetings with 
Dr. John Thie have taught me how to use TFH 
techniques to help keep my mother comfortable 
at home. I no longer have to drive 50 
miles each way to take Mother to the massage 
therapist or acupuncturist every other week. The TFH technique is excellent for anyone who 
has chronic health problems. When Mother gets 
up from a chair, she is able to maintain her 
equilibrium. In fact, she now has more stamina 
because I balance her with all 14 muscles! What a blessing TFH is to the planet. As a neurologist and psychiatrist using a lot 
of technical equipment to diagnose neurological 
health disorders, I again felt unsatisfied with 
the answers I was able to give to patients with 
chronic health problems when no organic cause 
could be found. In 1995, my husband, Michael, and I joined a 
class with Dr. Thie in Freiburg, about Touch 
for Health. We both were impressed by his open-
minded character and his attitude towards health 
and healing. We decided to take part in one of 
his TFH classes in Santa Monica, California. It 
was a great experience and confirmed our ideas 
about natural healing methods. We immediately 
started preparations to build our own practice 
in a little village in southern Germany, in the 
spring of 1998. Betty Man Today, we use a lot of Kinesiological techniques 
and Touch for Health in our work. We are 
grateful to witness the wonderful results in 
changing peoples’ lives. Drs. Michael and Karin Rupprecht
Germany

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
221
E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H Large Intestine Meridian The Large Intestine Meridian 
Function is to absorb the end prod-
ucts of digestion and hold the waste 
materials until they can be eliminated. Approximately 80% of the material enter-
ing the large intestines is absorbed since 
it is mostly water. The Large Intestine 
Meridian Function is very important in the metabolism of water, as it extracts 
water from waste materials to either 
reabsorb or send on to the bladder. It is also crucial in the elimination of 
waste materials, as imbalance in the 
Large Intestine Function may result in 
physical, mental, emotional, or spiritual 
toxicity. ELEMENT METAPHOR The Metal Element Metaphor corre-
sponds to the hardened, condensed 
materials that form within the earth. The Earth Element gives birth to Metal, 
symbolized by the condensed ores, min-
eral, or salts which form within the 
earth. Metal in turn gives birth to Water, 
which is symbolized by the condensation 
of water on a metal surface. Metal is 
controlled by Fire, which can first melt 
and purify, then forge and transform 
into a new specific shape for another 
purpose. Metal controls Wood just as 
a metal blade can chop and transform 
wood into many useful shapes. The 
Metal Element may be associated with 
hardness used to protect yourself, the 
“armor” you wear, the tools or weapons 
you carry, or the metallic objects you 
possess for  adornment or decoration.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
224 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 14 Large Intestine	 Meridian Fascia Lata MUSCLE FUNCTION
This very long muscle has its bulk on the pelvis 
and then a very thin band of tissue runs down 
the side of the leg to just below the knee. It 
helps bend the thigh, draws it away from the 
body sideways, and keeps it turned in. MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS Fascia Lata 14 L2 STANDING / LYING FACE UP 1. Raise the leg up and 45º to the side, the foot turned in. When 
standing, a chair for support may be needed. INDICATIONS 
With weakness, the legs may tend to bow, 
the thigh turning outward. When it is not 
functioning ideally,  the foot is turned out-
ward and the toes are not used in the most 
effective way to give power to the forward 
thrust in walking or running. This muscle 
is particularly associated with the Large 
Intestine Meridian Function of absorbing 
the beneficial nutrients before waste prod-
ucts are passed on. Approximately 80% of 
the material entering the large intestines is 
absorbed. In addition to intestinal problems 
of constipation, spastic colon, colitis, and 
diarrhea, there can also be chest soreness and 
breast pain with menstruation. 2. Stabilize the opposite hip. 3. Pressure is against the outside of leg above the ankle to push 
the leg down and in towards the other foot. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Against the outside of leg above ankle. P FOODS FOR BALANCING
Increased water and fiber intake, yogurt, 
buttermilk. For persistent bilateral weak-
ness, iron may be helpful and can be found

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
226 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Large Intestine Meridian Hamstrings MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
These very strong muscles on the back of each 
thigh flex the leg, turn the leg sideways when 
the knee is bent, and are very important for 
walking, running and turning while running. They are particularly associated with the Large 
Intestine function of absorbing the beneficial 
nutrients before waste products are passed on. Hamstrings L4
L5 STANDING / LYING FACE DOWN 1. With the leg bent so the angle of the calf and thigh is slightly more 
than 90˚, exert pressure in the middle of the Hamstring to prevent 
cramping. If cramping occurs, use less knee flexion, more pressure 
on the Hamstrings. INDICATIONS 
They can be injured if not functioning opti-
mally when running and turning the foot. Inhibition in these muscles is associated with 
bow-legged or knock-kneed posture, as well 
as restlessness, fatigue, weakness of the legs, 
toxicity, constipation, colitis, hemorrhoids and 
headaches. Weakness can also come from the 
sacrum not moving between the hip bones 
during breathing. Lying on the floor with the 
knees bent to the chest and rolling from side 
to side while doing deep abdominal breathing 
may loosen up the sacrum. 2. Pressure is against the back of the Achilles tendon to straighten 
the leg. 3. Don’t let the pelvis tilt or the low back curve. 4. Make sure the direction of the test is straight, not sideways. 5. When standing, brace at the knee on the side of the test. A chair 
for support may be needed. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Downward against the back of lower leg. NOTE: 
Check also 
Abdominals, 
Sacrospinalis, 
Adductors and 
Quadriceps, 
especially if 
Hamstrings 
cramp easily or 
have tightness. Repeated test-
ing and Neuro-
lymphatic 
massage may 
be beneficial 
also. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Foods rich in vitamin E (wheat germ, green 
peas, green leafy vegetables), Tripe (stomach) 
and rare meats. Chewing the food well is help-
ful. Avoid concentrated sweets and refined 
flour products, when hamstring tightness and 
cramping are recurring. P
P o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS Posterior Fontanel, the 
baby’s soft spot on the 
back of the head. Test Also:  Latissimus Dorsi  and Pectoralis Major Clavicular.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
228 E L E M E N T
METAPHOR
5 Large Intestine Meridian Quadratus Lumborum MUSCLE TEST POSITIONS
MUSCLE FUNCTION
This muscle flexes the vertebral column 
sideways, drawing it toward the hip. It assists the action of the Diaphragm 
in breathing. It is a major stabilizing 
muscle of the lower back. Quadratus Lumborum STANDING L4
L5 1. With feet together, lean hips to one side and shoulders to the other. 2. Place hand on the extended hip and the other on the opposite 
shoulder. INDICATIONS 
It is a very strong muscle and is very 
important to check when any back 
pain is present. When it isn’t function-
ing properly it can effect the function 
of the Diaphragm, and so can be 
associated with almost any symptom. Weakness in one side of this muscle 
will show in the posture as an eleva-
tion of the last rib on the weak side 
and a curve in the Lumbar Vertebrae. 3. Pressure with both hands toward the midline to straightn the torso. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
Inward against the shoulder and hip 
simultaneously toward the midline. LYING FACE UP / FACE DOWN 1. Test with the person gripping the table edge using their hands to 
keep their torso stable. 2. Take both legs out to one side. 3. Pressure is at the outside of the ankle to press both legs toward 
the midline. FOODS FOR BALANCING
Food rich in vitamin E, A, and C may 
be helpful in balancing weakness in 
this muscle. 4. Lift the legs high enough that the feet will clear the table or floor. PRESSURE 
TARGET
P
At the outside of the ankle to press both 
legs toward the midline. o NEUROVASCULAR 
HOLDING POINTS P
P Parietal Eminence, 
usually a ridge 
between the ear and 
the top of the head. Test Also:  Psoas, Gluteus Medius, Gluteus Maximus, Piriformis, Sacrospinalis and Fascia Lata.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H

these traditional energy patterns. Two useful 
approaches are presented in this manual. ASSESSMENT 
BALANCING In every scientific work there is the science 
part — empirical or experimental evidence 
that a technique is effective, repeatable and 
transferable for the desired outcome — and 
there is an artistic and spiritual part — the 
subtle, personal differences which work best 
for the individuals involved, whether they 
adhere to the traditional or prescribed meth-
ods or not. Different Models of TFH Energy Pattern 
Assessment Thus far we have learned to use the muscle 
test to get a sense of energy flow in the merid-
ians and the muscles, as well as different 
touch reflexes to enhance and balance the 
flow. The easiest way to begin working is 
to check and balance one muscle at a time, 
working through the 14 or 42 muscles. We 
can also check all of the muscles first, and 
then assess the overall pattern of energy to 
decide on a logical place to begin balancing. Often, we can find a key point to work on 
that will balance some or all of the muscles, 
not just one muscle. No One Right Way Touch for Health is both a science and an 
art. We show two models of assessment bal-
ancing (the simple, open-ended approach and 
over-energy, closed-system model) because 
these models are ones that have a long his-
tory and tradition of use and are good exam-
ples of what has worked for a lot of people. At the same time, providing 2 models helps 
us to see that there is not only one right way 
of doing things in Touch for Health. Everyone who uses the TFH methods brings 
with them knowledge as well as their per-
sonal intuition and insight. Everyone who 
practices Touch for Health eventually finds 
their own particular way to do each tech-
nique that is most effective in helping start 
the natural healing system for each person 
in the encounter. This is okay and part 
of the art and spirit of TFH. At the same 
time, it’s very helpful that the Faculty of the 
International Kinesiology College regularly Most of the patterns we use in TFH are 
derived from standard Acupuncture Theory, 
but they are presented, to the extent possible, 
in the simplest practical terms. Some people 
will find an open-ended approach easier and 
more fun to use, while others will feel more 
secure and be more effective with more of 
a “closed model” with more fixed rules for 
interpreting the energy patterns. Over the 
30 years of the worldwide development of 
the TFH system, a variety of effective pro-

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
233 meets and maintains consistency in the prin-
ciples and syllabus of the TFH curriculum, 
so that the standard “language” of TFH 
remains universal. using the Acupressure Holding Points to 
Sedate. We can also Circuit-Locate (CL) to gain addi-
tional confidence in our assessment. Simply 
Circuit-Locate the reflex related to the key 
muscle and recheck any of the other inhib-
ited muscles. Then, continue to CL this “key 
reflex point” and check any other inhibited 
muscles. They should all test strong while 
circuit locating this one point. Now you can 
be confident that balancing the key muscle/
meridian will balance the whole system. Even so, sometimes we still have to balance 
some muscles individually. It’s okay, don’t 
panic. Just use the usual reflexes. The important thing to remember is that 
when using a particular model you need to 
know the assumptions of the model. You 
cannot know or perceive everything at any 
one time so we develop models that simplify 
our experience for specific purposes. As long 
as you recognize the limitations of a model— 
the assumptions and limited purpose of the 
model— we can get the best results, and rec-
ognize when we need to expand our model, 
or switch models entirely. In the simple assessment model, when we bal-
ance the energy in the Central and Governing 
Meridians first, we assume that this allows 
the used or excess energy to leave the body 
with the breath. We then look for patterns 
of under-energy based on inhibition of any 
of the other 12 muscles. There are 4 kinds 
of patterns represented on the “Wheel” and 
2 energy patterns in the Five Element dia-
gram. In an open-ended approach, we make 
a guess at the best place to start based on 
the overall combination of patterns and our 
personal intuition or preference. When we 
balance a key muscle/meridian that seems to 
be involved in several patterns, it will usually 
balance all or most of the other meridians. Any that remain inhibited can then be bal-
anced individually in the usual way. We then 
check for any residual over-energy using the 
Pulse Points or the Alarm Points and balance In the over-energy, closed-system model, 
we do not make any assumption about 
over-energy. We check all the meridians for 
under-energy, as indicated by an unlocking 
muscle, and then we check for over-energy 
as indicated by the alarm points. Sometimes 
a muscle that unlocks when tested actually 
has too much energy flowing to it, “blowing 
the circuit.”  So an unlocking muscle is con-
sidered over-energy if indicated by the asso-
ciated alarm point. The assessment is made 
based on the pattern of over and under-en-
ergy together, with more specific rules for 
discovering “true” patterns.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
234 ALARM POINTS Sometimes a muscle that tests somewhat weak 
at first seems to get even weaker as you work 
with the Neurolymphatic and Neurovascular 
Points. One possibility is that the merid-
ian involved already may be over-energized. Weak muscle tests usually mean the merid-
ian needs stimulation, but an over-energized 
meridian can “pop the circuit” and make its 
associated muscle weak. Sending even more 
energy into the meridian can make the mus-
cle even more inhibited. Alarm Points help us 
determine the energy status. We can check ALL of the alarm points 
when we do an assessment balance. First we 
check and balance Central and Governing 
Meridians in the usual way. Then we check 
the remaining meridians and make note of 
the unlocking muscles. Circuit-Locate all of 
the Alarm Points (check both sides for bilat-
eral alarm points — IM change on one or 
both sides indicates over energy in the merid-
ian) to check each meridian for over-energy: 
Lightly hold each alarm point one at a time 
and check the IM. Then we make our assess-
ment based on both over and under-energy. To find out if a meridian is over-energized, 
first find and test a strong Indicator Muscle 
(IM). Lightly touch the Alarm Point associ-
ated with the involved meridian and test the 
IM (check both sides if the alarm point is 
bilateral). If the IM goes weak while holding 
the alarm point, over-energy is indicated. To balance, find the Acupressure Holding 
Points on the reference page for the asso-
ciated meridian. Locate the Sedate points. Hold them lightly (There will be two pairs 
of points. First hold one pair, then hold the 
second pair). Now the weak muscle will be 
strong. Retest the IM while touching the 
Alarm Point.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
236 The Wheel Energy Patterns in the 24-hour Graphic The Wheel represents the relationship of the 
12 major meridians in a 24-hour cycle. The 
time of day is listed for the peak energy level 
of each meridian. The muscle-test thumbnails 
for the Central (Supraspinatus) and Governing 
(Teres Major) meridians are shown in the 
upper corners of the wheel, since they function 
more as storage vessels related to all of the 
meridians, rather than as individual parts of 
the 24-hour cycle. The muscle-test thumbnails 
of the primary tests for the other 12 meridi-
ans are shown on the outside of the wheel in 
both standing and lying positions. Above each 
thumbnail is the spinal reflex associated with 
the given muscle. On pg. 235 is a figure illus-
trating the Alarm Points, which we can use to 
check for over-energy (see further explanation 
on pg. 234). The 24-hour cycle is a shorter 
cycle related to one day and one night and 
related metaphors, while the 5-Element cycle 
is a longer cycle, related to the 5 seasons of a 
year or 5 phases in a life cycle, etc. THE BEAVER DAM A block in the 24-hour meridian cycle is indi-
cated by three or more consecutive meridians 
inhibited in a clockwise direction. We call 
this a Beaver Dam because clearing the block 
“upstream” in the 
cycle will generally 
clear all the subse-
quent blocks follow-
ing in the 24-hour 
cycle. Consider the 
metaphors and use 
the balancing points 
related to the first 
meridian in a clock-
wise direction. U
U
U U O O U
U U If you check for over-energy, look for two or 
more under-energies following an over-en-
ergy. Start with the first under-energy, 
FOLLOWING AN OVER-ENERGY merid-
ian in clockwise order. If the over-energy is in 
the middle of a series of under-energies, and 
not followed by at least 2 under-energies, it’s 
not considered a beaver dam pattern. Look for 
another pattern.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
238 If you check for over-energy, look for one 
over-energy and three under-energy meridi-
ans (more than one over-energy meridian in 
a square is considered random, and so not a 
square pattern). Start with the first under-en-
ergy, following an over-energy meridian in 
clockwise order. TRIANGLES There are 3 Yin and 3 Yang meridians which 
travel in the same direction on the arms or legs. These are represented by blue Triangles on 
the 24-hour graphic. If 2 or 3 meridians 
are inhibited, work 
the reflexes and con-
sider the metaphors 
of the first imbalance 
in a clockwise order, 
or the one closest to 
time of day. O SPOKES U Spokes on the wheel indicate the Midday- 
Midnight relationship. Meridians that are 12 
hours apart in the 24-hour cycle are related 
because one is at its highest flow while the other 
is at its lowest ebb. If both are inhibited, start 
with the meridian closest to the time of day, or 
the one that seems 
to be involved in the 
most aspects of the 
overall pattern. U If you check for 
over-energy,  look for one over energy and 
two under energy meridians. Start with the 
first under-energy, following an over-energy 
meridian in clockwise order. Two over-ener-
gies and one under-energy, or three under-en-
ergies is considered random, not a true trian-

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
240 Two Energy Cycles Two interrelated cycles of energy interact to 
keep the balance of all things in the universe. They are the Creation and the Control/ 
Destruction cycle. THE 5-ELEMENT 
CYCLES THE SHEN CYCLE The SHEN (also known as Sheng) or Creation 
cycle is the generating and nurturing cycle, 
which moves consecutively in a clockwise 
circle through the elements. This is also 
called the Mother-Daughter relationship, in 
which each meridian feeds the next one in 
the circle. The Yin Meridians, shown in the 
diagram on the inside of the circle, feed the 
other Yin Meridians. The Yang Meridians 
on the outside of the circle feed the other 
Yangs. This traditional Chinese paradigm represents 
all aspects of the universe and natural cycles of 
the earth (rather than the chemical elements of 
Western science). As a product of folklore, it 
is a metaphor portraying immutable truths. As 
such it covers the phases of the life cycle. FIRE u u EARTH WOOD u u METAL
WATER From the birth phase came WOOD The surging upwards phase produced FIRE u In its mature adult stage was EARTH In the decay phase, METAL Shen Cycle In its death/rebirth phase, WATER

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
241 Wood is said to be the mother of Fire, feed-
ing it by providing fuel. Fire is the mother of 
Earth, creating it from the ashes of the wood. Earth gives up its ores to create Metal. Metal 
creates Water. We might think of water 
springing from the depths where the metals 
are, or water condensing on a metal surface. Water nurtures the Wood, feeding its roots. FIRE WOOD EARTH THE KO CYCLE The other energy cycle is the KO cycle: the 
Control cycle or the Destruction cycle. This is 
also called the Grandmother-Granddaughter 
relationship in which each element has a con-
trolling influence or destroys the second ele-
ment from it, in the clockwise cycle. This is 
represented by a star pattern on the graphic. Therefore, Wood is called the “grandmother” 
of Earth and destroys Earth by penetrating it 
and breaking it up, but Wood can also con-
trol Earth, and give it structure by holding 
it together with its roots. Think of plants 
preventing erosion on a hillside. Earth can 
destroy Water by interrupting its flow, or 
absorbing it. Earth controls Water, and gives 
it special properties by containing it and giv-
ing it form. Water can destroy Fire by dows-
ing it, but it can control and structure fire for 
cooking or as in a steam engine. Fire destroys 
Metal by melting or even evaporating it, but 
it can control Metal by forging it and creat-
ing tools, like an axe. Metal destroys Wood 
by chopping it, but it can also control Wood 
and give it structure by trimming and shaping 
it, or by creating lumber and construction. METAL
METAL WATER Ko Cycle There are many aspects of creation tradition-
ally categorized by the Five Elements such 
as the emotions, the five senses, the seasons, 
climates and the phases in the life cycle of a 
person, idea, etc. We have included 10 tradi-
tional categories, but there are more that can 
be found in Acupuncture texts. We have also 
included an 11th category based on phases of 
Cognitive development and types of Beliefs, 
or ways of perceiving reality. Since these 
phases do tend to correspond with phases of 
personal growth and development, they map 
well with the 5 Element phases of Seasons 
and Life Cycles. Depending which muscles

242 are out of balance, we can contemplate the 
possible symbolic meaning of any of these 
5-Element Metaphors in the context of our 
life and goals. Simple Model Look for a series of meridian imbalances going in a clockwise direction using either Shen or Ko cycles. Correct the first unlocked muscle in the series- but In our two dimensional model, one energy 
cycle is superimposed upon the other, and it 
should be remembered that each cycle affects 
the 5 Elements in their entirety. Energy 
moves around the 5 Elements in a clockwise 
direction and takes the shortest route. It will 
use either cycle to do this, sending energy 
through the meridian system as efficiently as 
possible, depending on the changing needs of 
the person. always prefer to start with a YIN meridian, on the INSIDE of the circle (Yin is in. Begin with Yin). Over-Energy Model Check ALARM POINTS to establish over energy pattern. Use a light touch on alarm points. Start with the FIRST YIN UNDER-ENERGY Meridian AFTER ANY OVER-ENERGY (going in a clockwise direction using either Shen or Ko cycle). Assessing Patterns in the 5-Element 
Diagram You may Circuit-Locate to confirm the 
appropriate reflex point. Hold the reflex 
point for the key muscle/meridian to see that 
this will strengthen the muscle. Then con-
tinue to hold the same reflex point and retest 
other under-energy muscles. These should 
now lock, showing that your one point cor-
rection will move energy to balance all other 
meridians. Rub or hold and Challenge the 
reflex in the usual way. Recheck all under 
energy muscles to confirm that they are now 
locked. If any are still inhibited, balance indi-
vidually in the usual way. Recheck all Alarm 
Points - all should now be clear. Should there 
be residual over-energy, use the Acupressure 
Holding Points to Sedate the Meridian. Just as when we assess patterns in the 24-hour 
Cycle, we have the choice of looking at the 
possible patterns in the 5-Element Diagram 
with or without over-energy. Check and cor-
rect Central and Governing, then check the 
other 12 Meridians and mark on the diagram 
(You can copy the diagram for personal use). Then you can also check the alarm points and 
mark the over-energy meridians (If the muscle 
unlocks, but the alarm point indicates over-en-
ergy, we assume it is unlocking because of 
too much energy, so mark it over-energy, but 
remember to recheck the muscle as well as the 
alarm point).

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
245 EXAMPLE The Mother-Daughter Relationship: WOOD ACUPRESSURE 
HOLDING POINTS 
THEORY feeds or gives energy to FIRE,  FIRE feeds or gives energy to EARTH, EARTH feeds or gives energy to METAL, METAL feeds or gives energy to WATER, WATER feeds or gives energy to WOOD. The Ko Cycle of controlling energy rela-
tionships is represented by the inner “star” 
pattern involving every other element in 
clockwise order. Now that we have some appreciation of the 
5-Element Cycles, we can delve into some of 
the detail of how the meridians interrelate 
when we use the Acupressure Holding Points 
(AHP). Unless you memorize the actual acu-
puncture points along the meridians (which 
is beyond the scope of this manual), you will 
still simply look at the diagram of the AHP 
for the Meridian to know where to hold. This explanation will simply give you some 
fascinating background information so you 
know what’s going on when you use the 
AHP. The diagram on pg. 247 illustrates the 
principles. EXAMPLE The Grandmother-Granddaughter Relationship: WOOD controls EARTH,  FIRE controls METAL, EARTH controls WATER, METAL controls WOOD, WATER controls FIRE. Within each element, Yin Meridians are rep-
resented on the inside of the circle and Yang 
Meridians on the outside of the circle. Yin 
Meridians feed/control other Yins and Yang 
Meridians feed/control other Yangs. We can 
tap into these relationships by connecting 
specific points on two meridians. On each 
meridian there is a point related to each of the 
5 Elements, represented by a repetition of the 
5 Elements “within” the meridian. The excep-
tions are Circulation-Sex and Triple Warmer 
Meridians. In acupuncture theory, they are 
considered “expressions” of Heart and Small 
Intestine respectively. Therefore, except when 
using the AHP specifically for them, they are 
not directly involved with the other meridians. The Shen Cycle is the nurturing, generating, 
creating flow of energy. Looking at the 
5-Element Chart and the Circle representing 
the Shen Cycle, it can be said that one ele-
ment “feeds” the next element immediately 
following it (always moving in the clockwise 
direction).

Acupressure Holding Points Tonification: Strengthening with the 
Acupressure Holding Points To inhibit a muscle using the AHP, we 
Sedate the related meridian. To Sedate, we 
take energy away from the meridian by con-
necting the points of the “daughter” element 
on mother and daughter Meridians. Then we 
want to control that flow of energy so that 
it is balanced. We connect the points of the 
“grandmother” element on the grandmother 
and granddaughter meridians. To facilitate a muscle using the AHP, we 
Tonify the related meridian. To Tonify, 
we first connect the points of the “mother” 
element on mother and daughter Meridians. Then we want to control that flow of energy 
so that it is balanced. We connect the points 
of the “grandmother” element on the grand-
mother and granddaughter meridians. text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
248 Luo Points Wood points of each meridian in order to 
complete the sedation process. When we 
look at the 5-Element Diagram, we see 
they are LV1 and SP1. Look at the Spleen 
Meridian Reference page to see where these 
points are located. There is a number within each element that 
is outside of the AHP 5-Element group. This 
is the Luo Point on the meridian. Luo (pro-
nounced low) Points are sometimes referred 
to as Junction or Passage Points and are 
bilateral holding points that balance two 
meridians within the same element. This is 
called the Husband-Wife relationship, and 
it is considered the closest and most effective 
relationship to use for balancing when appro-
priate. Additional Aspects of the Law of 	 	
5-Element Diagram The black arrows illustrate the direction 
and order of the Shen and Ko cycles. The 
red lines connecting Yin and Yang meridi-
ans in adjacent elements show the Midday-
Midnight relationships. The peak time is 
shown in 24-hour notation at the center of 
each meridian (11-13 for Heart means Heart 
is at its peak energy between 11AM and 
1PM, while 9-11 means Spleen is at its peak 
between 9AM and 11AM) While meridians are not the only channels 
of energy in the body (veins and arteries 
circulate blood, and the lymph system has 
its own circulation), an inner energy unites 
the body’s organic action as a whole. It is 
this deep interaction of organs and energy 
we deal with when we use the 5-Element 
approach. Pulse Points on the 	 	
	
	
Law of 5-Element Diagram Between this deeper, inner organic circula-
tion (5 Elements) and the more superficial 
circulation of energy (Wheel pattern of the 
12 meridians), there are specific connective 
channels between coupled meridians within 
each of the 5 Elements. These are called Luo 
Points. When one meridian has over-energy and the 
other has under-energy, hold the Luo Points 
for the under-energy meridian. Hold the Luo 
points on the “low” meridian, BOTH SIDES 
AT THE SAME TIME. The Elements are labeled in blue: Left 1-3 
and Right 1-3. This gives you the finger 
positions for the left and right wrists to find 
over-energy in each of the meridians, deep 
pressure for Yin meridians and light Pressure 
for the Yang meridians. Note that the Fire 
Element is divided between the left and right 
wrists- Small Intestine is the first light touch 
point on the left wrist, and Heart is the first 
deep touch point; Triple Warmer is the third 
light touch point on the right wrist and 
Circulation-Sex is the third deep touch point 
on the right.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
249 Luo Points Spleen: SP4
	
On the Spleen Meridian, 4 body” from the nail root of the 
big toe and toward the ankle. Stomach: ST40
	
On the Stomach Meridian, on the outside of the leg, mid-
way between the anklebone and the kneecap. Lung: LU7
	
On the Lung Meridian, 2 body” above the first crease on 
the wrist at the base of the hand. Large Intestine: LI6
	
On the Large Intestine Meridian, 3 body”above the wrist 
crease at the base of the hand. Kidney: K4
	
On the Kidney Meridian, on the inside of the leg level with 
the middle of the anklebone. Bladder: BL58
	
On the Bladder Meridian, on the outside of the leg, near 
the bottom of Gastrocnemius (calf) muscle. Liver: LV5
	
On the Liver Meridian, 5 body” above the inside anklebone. Gallbladder: GB37
	
On the Gallbladder Meridian, 5 body” above the outside 
anklebone. Heart: H5
	
On the Heart Meridian, 1 body” above the wrist crease at 
the base of the hand. Small Intestine: SI7
	
On the Small Intestine Meridian, 5 body” above the wrist 
flexure, outside aspect of the arm. Circulation-Sex: CX6
	
On the Circulation-Sex Meridian, 2 body” above the wrist 
crease at the base of the hand. Triple Warmer: TW5 
	
On the TripleWarmer Meridian, 2 body” from the wrist 
crease at the base of the hand on the backside of the wrist. The Luo Points can all 
be found bilaterally. Used with Permission, IKC 
Coursebook II, pg. 15.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
251 turning the head towards a sound when in 
the face down or crawling position as a baby. You’ll find this particularly useful if your job 
involves turning the head frequently (taxi or 
truck drivers, checkout operators, tennis audi-
ences etc). You may also find it helps make 
reading easier, more fluid and enjoyable. ADDITIONAL 	 	
	
BALANCING 	
	
	
TECHNIQUES TECHNIQUE Auricular Exercise: Start with a strong Indicator Muscle. Have the person turn their head to the left and check the IM. Repeat while turning to the right. Auricular Exercise If the muscle unlocks with the head turned to either side, or both sides, stretch both ears and recheck. Previously, we learned to stretch the ears to 
“Tune In.”  Now we will use the Auricular 
Exercise with an Indicator Muscle for addi-
tional benefit. The ears seem to function as 
antennae to draw energy into the body. We 
also associate this exercise with the reflex of Firmly take hold of (and unfold as you do so) the turned over part of the ear. Pull firmly away from the opening of the ear. Continue around and down to the ear lobe, pulling away from the orifice. You will know you have done a good job when the ears begin to feel warm, and the color comes to the surface of the skin. Now retest with the head turned as before. The muscle should be strong no matter which way the head is turned. You can also ask the person to turn their head “just a little more” to the left and Check the IM. (This may reveal movement avoidance). Repeat on the right side. If the muscle unlocks on either of these, repeat the balancing exercise. You can also try unfolding the ears with the head turned to the “affected side’— the one that switches off the IM, or feels more tense or painful — as you do the balancing procedure. The Auricular Exercise: Check IM with head turned to left and right. “affected ear,” is the ear turned towards the back when testing.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
252 Visual Inhibition Sometimes you find yourself falling asleep 
or becoming very tired when reading. If the 
energy is not flowing or balanced related to 
the back and forth eye motion of reading, 
the effort to compensate can be draining and 
generally inhibiting. Getting the energy flow-
ing related to eye movement and vision often 
does improve our actual eyesight, as well as 
help us to be more clear-headed, alert and 
comfortable. Auricular Exercise. We can “Switch On” while moving our eyes 
around and looking in different directions 
for a quick clearing.  (See Switch On) We 
can also use our muscle checking to see 
which specific motion or direction seems to 
be affected. Stretch and unfold ears. TECHNIQUE Visual Inhibition: Use an Indicator Muscle to check You may find that this dramatically increases 
range of motion and comfort, as well as your 
ability to focus and stay alert. The standard 
TFH protocol is to use this exercise with the 
range of motion turning the head, but this exer-
cise helps with any range of motion and also 
helps to wake us up and tune in to whatever 
we want to focus on. Try it turning at the hips, 
or with any stretch like touching your toes. It 
is also helpful with restoring range of motion 
when rehabilitating any injured muscle. for inhibition in response to any movement or use of the eyes. For example, have the person hold their head still, then check the IM with the eyes gently rolled as far as possible to the left, then to the right. If the indicator muscle unlocks in any direction, use any or all of your “Switch On” exercises to balance the energy and then recheck. The muscle should be strong. (Hold one hand over the navel and rub the depressions under the ends of the collarbones. Switch hands and repeat. Do the same rubbing above and below the lips and on the tailbone).

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
253 Visual Inhibition Aspects to Check: Look left/right
Look up/down
Look at diagonals (repeat above eye 	
         
    movements with eyes closed)
Look near/middle/far 
Look at objects in a mirror
Read aloud and silently Check IM while reading aloud and silently. You might try looking just a little further in 
each direction as with the Auricular Exercise. But be aware of the person’s comfort level 
and be careful not to strain their eyes if they 
are already bothering them. You can also 
try looking in the inhibiting direction, while 
working the reflexes. Check IM with eyes neutral. The Switch On points at K27 (depressions 
below the ends of collarbones) are associated 
with all of the eye movements. The endpoints 
of Central and Governing (just above the 
upper lips and below the lower lips) are asso-
ciated more specifically with up/down. The 
beginning of Governing  (tailbone) is associ-
ated more specifically with front/back, near/
far, rear vision or mirror vision. Check IM with eyes looking in each direction.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
254 Figure-8 Energy Flows Of the many subsidiary energy flows to the 
primary meridians, Tibetan medicine men-
tions one which circles in a Figure-8 pattern 
along the body’s front and back, as well as in 
smaller patterns around the body. Sometimes 
balancing using the following exercise results 
in immediate positive change in  healing time, 
joint mobility and pain. “infinity sign” will oppose and balance both 
directions. The cloverleaf is an alternative 
option. Some people enjoy tracing in “all 
directions” when the muscle  unlocks in both 
directions of a diagonal. You can use an Indicator Muscle to check 
Figure-8’s over the front and back of the 
body, the face, the torso and legs (above and 
below the waist), back of the head, above 
the head and beneath the feet. You can also 
check over any particular part of the body, 
over a muscle, or over an injury. Many 
people have reported benefits tracing over 
wounds or even old scars. The illustration 
shows 8 major areas, but there are many 
more including those on the sides of the body. To check for a disturbance in these patterns, 
use your hand to trace diagonal lines over the 
body,  checking an Indicator Muscle on each 
pass. For example, make a rapid pass from 
one shoulder to the opposite hip and check 
the IM; then from the other shoulder to the 
other hip and check the IM. The second two 
passes are upward from hip to shoulder. Make a rapid pass from one hip to the oppo-
site shoulder, then from the other hip to the 
remaining shoulder. EXAMPLE If the IM unlocks after tracing from the right shoul- If the Indicator Muscle unlocks after any 
pass, balance by tracing a Figure-8 pattern 
with your hand in the “strong” direction. Use your hand to rapidly trace several fig-
ure eights in the opposite direction(s) to the 
inhibiting direction(s) and recheck. (Trace in 
the direction opposite the one that unlocks 
the Indicator Muscle.) Now the Indicator 
Muscle should be unaffected by passes in 
any direction. You may repeat this sev-
eral times until challenging the previously 
“weak” direction is clear. Usually we trace 
an “upright 8”, but if the IM unlocks in 
response to both diagonals in a particular 
direction (up or down), tracing a “lazy 8” or der to the left hip, but tracing left hip to right shoulder didn’t unlock the IM, begin at the left hip and trace a figure 8 from left hip across to right shoulder, over to left shoulder, then down and across to right hip and back over to left hip again. 256 TECHNIQUE Surrogate Testing Surrogate Testing: To be most thorough, check Some people, for whatever reason, find it more 
difficult to see the shifts in energy with the mus-
cle check. In such a case, Surrogate Testing is 
a very effective alternative. You’ll need a third 
person to assist you by acting as the “surro-
gate.”  They don’t “take on” the energy of 
another person, but simply allow their muscles 
to indicate the energy flow of the other person. and balance the surrogate to be sure all muscles are starting out strong, perhaps with a goal like, “I am a clear surrogate.” Now have the surrogate touch the person being balanced. A hand on the arm or shoulder is sufficient; just be sure “the connection” is made and maintained throughout the test. Surrogate Testing or “Substitute”  Testing is an 
excellent way to test infants, those too young to 
understand the testing procedures (or pets, who 
respond very well to energy work, but don’t 
understand muscle testing procedure), people 
suffering paralysis or those immobilized by 
injury, casts or recent surgery. Now check the muscles of the surrogate. Imbalances in the energy field of the “untestable” person will be indicated as corresponding inhibited muscles in  the surrogate. Whenever possible, work the appropriate points on the untestable person. Then recheck the surrogate to confirm the muscle/meridian has been balanced. Maintain contact between the untestable person and the surrogate when you recheck. When finished, anchor by testing the IM while the surrogate makes the statements, “I am no longer the surro- gate” and “I am (their own name).” This is an amazing, invaluable technique. Even 
an unconscious or comatose person can be bal-
anced. Just make sure their “advocate” gives 
permission. You can also use any Indicator Muscle as a 
“surrogate” for a muscle that you can’t test. Bring your awareness to the muscle you want 
to check, call out the muscle name, and check 
the IM. Balance with the usual reflexes. Surrogate Testing

AND WALKING Cross-Crawling to music is energizing and 
fun. Choose music that has a rhythm that 
encourages easy Cross-Crawl movements. Start with an even pace, then try speeding 
up, slowing down (slow motion “Tai Chi” 
Cross-Crawl is very amusing) and try to find 
combinations that are challenging. Study the 
different “Gaits” in the  following section to 
give you some ideas of different Cross-Crawl 
movements. If you start out favoring homo-
lateral movement (arm and leg on the same 
side) or feel awkward making the movement, 
and then you get the rhythm of the Cross-
Crawl movement, it’s a clear indication that 
you just improved right/left brain integra- Cross-Crawl A Cross-Crawl movement is made when we 
move opposite arm and leg together as in 
walking. We can do this as an exercise to help 
integrate the left and right brain hemispheres, 
improve concentration, enhance learning, 
move lymph, improve ease and comfort see-
ing and reading, reduce stress and be better Cross-Crawl: Sample positions for using 
Cross-Crawl with opposite arms and legs 
in a variety of movements.

265 tion. Watch for improvements in all your 
physical and mental activities! Standing in old, run-down 
shoes with worn heels and 
arches sagging is one of 
the worst things for the 
back. It would be better 
to work without shoes if 
they’re not in good shape. Standing for any length 
of time in such shoes, the 
arch can become tired and 
drop. Wearing a lower 
heel makes better use 
of the arch and putting 
some support in the arch 
helps prevent foot fatigue. Sometimes the simplest orthopedic correction 
has a dramatic effect on the whole posture and 
balance of a person. Walking Walking with arms swinging opposite to the 
legs has a Cross-Crawl effect on the brain 
and is good for general health. A good brisk 
walk of a mile twice a day is generally recom-
mended. Walking is one of the few exercises that ben-
efits all parts of the body. Push off with all 
the toes, landing on the heel. Roll down the 
outside of the foot and let the arms swing, 
hands empty. This gives you good motion in 
the spine; none of the muscles are locked, all 
are flowing. Observe the heels of the shoes. They should wear evenly and not be allowed 
to run-down more than a quarter of an inch. Homolateral Movement Cross-Crawl Exercise

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
266 TECHNIQUE Cross-Crawl Integration Exercise Cross-Crawl Integration Exercise This technique helps enhance the neurological 
organization of the brain. You may be familiar 
with the tendency of the right brain hemi-
sphere to control the left side of the body, and 
the left side of the brain to control the right 
side of the body. There is also a separation 
in the functions of the brain, with intellectual 
concerns, voluntary and involuntary muscle 
control, internal regulation of the body func-
tions and emotional centers all occupying dif-
ferent areas within the brain itself. At times, 
the neurological balance requires dominance 
of one hemisphere over the other and one side 
of the body over the other. After sufficient 
development through infancy and early child-
hood, the right and left sides being developed 
more or less equally or bilaterally in muscle 
coordination and handedness, intellectual or 
academic learning can proceed normally. This 
development involves a period of homolateral 
crawling before cross-lateral crawling occurs. •	 Do the usual prechecks. •	 March in place for 10 repetitions making sure that the knees bend well and the shoulders swing freely, and the head is held stationary with the eyes looking straight ahead. If any muscle that was previously strong becomes weak, Cross-Crawling would probably be helpful. •	 Check an Indicator Muscle for comparison. First check the opposite function, that is, homolateral crawling. You can march in place moving the arm and leg on the same side of the body, but it is even more effective when done lying face up. Look straight up or straight forward. Repeat the motion 10 times and then recheck the IM. Now repeat the Cross-Crawl and recheck the IM. It’s okay if the IM is strong after both movements, but if it’s strong on homolateral crawl and weak on Cross-Crawl, it indicates an inhibition in coor- Sometimes when we balance someone, they 
notice a positive change, but as soon as they 
start moving around the balance “doesn’t hold.” 
They go right back to habitual postures or 
symptoms return. This is your first clue that you 
might want to check Cross-Crawl. If walking 
around is switching off your energy, you want 
to reset the system so that it is energizing instead. dinated, bilateral brain function. At the least, we want the IM to be strong following Cross-Crawl. •	 Do the Cross-Crawl exercise, preferably lying on your back, for 3 full minutes.  (Move your eyes back and forth through a figure-8 and in all directions to enhance the benefit). •	 Recheck the IM. It should be strong. 267 cises both with the eyes opened and closed 
and holding the tongue on the roof of the 
mouth just behind the front teeth to force

continue the Cross-Crawl exercise. of coordination returns, then check and bal-
ance the Cross-Crawl action. First recheck 
the muscles in question and balance them 
again using the usual methods (This is assum-
ing that they do switch on in the usual way 
but become inhibited again once you start 
walking around). Cross-Crawling ties into the circuitry devel-
oped during the normal crawling motions 
of the infant. Crawling or creeping involves 
moving the right arm and left leg forward at 
the same time. Then the left arm and right 
leg are brought forward. Cross-Crawl is very 
useful, especially with children and people 
who experience learning and/or coordination 
difficulties. When working with little ones, 
it’s probably necessary to perform the exercise 
when the child is passive. Adults can usually 
do the exercise unassisted. With the person 
lying face up, pick up his right arm and leg 
together and raise the arm back behind the 
head, the leg up with the knee bent as if to 
crawl. Return them back down to the side of 
the body and repeat by picking up the left arm 
and leg. For Cross-Crawl, do the same with the 
opposite arm and leg. If you have coordination difficulties or all the 
weak muscles (not locking) are found on one 
side of the body, or if the same muscles are 
regularly inhibited, this can be an indication 
that there is a problem with the cross-coor-
dination between the right and left sides of 
the body and brain. Check and balance the 
Cross-Crawl motions. Three minutes of these arm and leg motions 
is a quick invigorating and integrating exer-
cise that can easily be repeated three or four 
times per day. Use as many different contra-
lateral  (Cross-Crawl) movements as can be 
devised for a general exercise and improved 
flexibility. The Cross-Crawling benefits may 
be enhanced by looking in different direc-
tions with just the eyes while doing the exer- Assisting with Cross-Crawl: Some people, especially children, need 
assistance and can even remain passive while you move their limbs 
through the motions.

like cracks in the sidewalk, you can just reach

more energized by walking, and more alert 
and enthusiastic. If you find walking more 
draining than invigorating, or the immediate 
benefits of your balancing don’t seem to last 
or “hold,” check and balance the gaits. Ideally

If either side is inhibited, use the Anterior Deltoid reflexes or Circuit-Locate to see which reflexes are appropriate and balance. Then test each leg in the forward position, as in walking. Circuit-Locate to see which reflexes are needed to balance any inhibition (try Quadriceps or Abdominal reflexes). Now test the opposite arm and leg simultaneously. Repeat with the other arm and leg. but with a little extra attention to stabiliza-
tion, you can test them standing up. In each 
test, you check the arm and the opposite leg 
each by itself, and then you check them at the 
same time. While the individual limbs might 
test “strong,” when we check their combined 
function we may find inhibition or lack of 
integration. If one or both limbs go down 
under simultaneous pressure, work the gait 
reflexes. Note that these may well be the most 
tender, painful points to massage in the TFH Anterior Gait test

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
270 TECHNIQUE Contralateral Gait Test: Sit with arms crossed, hands on opposite shoulders. Twist the torso to one side. Test by untwisting the shoulders, and balance (abdominals) if needed. Repeat twisting to the other side. Lift each knee towards the oppo- site shoulder and test by pushing down and out. Balance (Quadriceps or Fascia Lata) if necessary. Now check the torso and opposite leg in combi- nation. First, twist torso bringing the left shoulder forward and bring the right leg up towards the left shoulder. Push leg down and out, shoulder back and out. Repeat with the other shoulder/leg. Additional tests: You can also try pulling the oppo- Contralateral Gait Test site arm and leg away from the body (adductors/ lats) and test Psoas vs. PMC. Alternatively test the opposite shoulder/ knee. Sit with arms crossed, hands on opposite shoulders. Twist the torso to one side. Test by pushing back through the angle of the forward shoulder, and balance (abdominals) if needed. Repeat twisting to the other side. Lift each knee and test by pushing down and in. Balance (Quadriceps or Fascia Lata) if necessary. Now rotate the torso and each leg in combination as follows and note the results. First, twist torso to the right and push the raised left leg. Push leg down and in, shoulder back and out. Repeat with the other shoulder/leg.

271 Balancing the Gaits The Gait reflex points are located just above 
the area where the toes and foot meet, in the 
spaces between the tendons. Use firm mas-
sage on both feet. First, warn the person that 
these are very tender points. Have the person 
agree to tell you if it is too painful, instead 
of kicking you in the teeth. STOP IMME-
DIATELY if they tell you.  (See the Release 
Technique below for an alternative). Brace 
firmly underneath the foot while rubbing 
between all of the tendons and on the sides 
of the foot, and underneath at the ball of the 
foot, especially on all points shown. Have the 
person take a short walk again to see if they 
notice a difference. Front Rear Contralateral Lateral Gait Reflex Holding Release Technique As with the Neurolymphatic Release tech-
nique, this provides a gentle alternative to 
vigorously massaging points which may be 
too tender or ticklish to endure. TECHNIQUE The Gait Reflex Holding Release: Gently hold all of the points with one hand by placing your thumb at the ball of the foot, and four fingers touching the spaces between the tendons. At the same time, hold the opposite hand in a similar way, mapping the points on the foot to the hand. Repeat with the other hand and foot.

emotional and physical pain. T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
274 TECHNIQUE Time of Day Balance When we assess an energy pattern on the 
24-Hour Wheel, we can always fall back 
to the under-energy Meridian closest to the 
current Time of Day. Based on that princi-
ple, we can do a quick 14-Muscle Check and 
balance by simply working all the reflexes 
for the muscle/meridian closest to the time 
of day. Dr Jim Reid, D.D., found that this 
technique quickly eliminates a lot of aches 
and pains. Ideally we will actually Muscle 
Check the 14 meridians, but we can also just 
assess how we are feeling, work the points 
and notice any positive changes. Time of Day: Do the usual pretests. Assess your goal, comfort, energy and any aches or pains on a scale of 0-10. Now check at least 14-muscles and note which are inhibited. Work some or all of the reflexes for Supraspinatus (Central, pg. 98) and Teres Major (Govrening, pg. 104) – SR, NL, NV, Meridian, etc. Now pick a muscle related to the Meridian for the time of day, and work the reflexes. Work some or all of the reflexes for the muscle, SR, NL, NV, Meridian, etc. Recheck the inhibited muscles and they most likely will all be switched on. (Strengthen any muscles that are still inhibited TIME OF DAY BALANCE FOR “JET LAG” in the usual way). Reassess any aches and pains, Many people have reported that this tech-
nique alleviates “jet lag.”  When crossing 
time zones, by changing your sleep/work 
schedule, etc., you can simply “reset” your 
energy for the appropriate “time of day.” 
You can work the reflexes for each new time 
zone, or simply reset to the new time when 
you arrive at your destination. Tracing all 
the meridians starting with the meridian 
related to the time of day can also help with 
this. A 14-Muscle Balance is a wonderful 
way to raise the body’s energy and balance 
physical and emotional pain and tension. comfort, energy level, and goals on your analog scales.

inhibiting or sedating applications of touch 
reflexes. We feather or pinch the muscle, trace 
the meridians backwards, etc. One expression 
of pain is over-energy in the meridian. We can 
use any inhibiting reflex to disperse the excess 
energy and reduce the pain. All of the reflex systems which we normally 
use to facilitate muscles can be used in the 
“opposite” way to inhibit or sedate, which 
also often  relieves pain. Temporarily sedat-
ing a muscle allows it to relax long enough to 
regain a balanced muscle tone. We can use

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
279 any of the same energies, the same systems, 
although the effectiveness will vary with dif-
ferent people and different problems. cause a muscle to weaken if it has just been 
tested. Applying pressure towards the attach-
ments at the origin and insertion of a muscle, 
or pinching in the belly of the muscle, will 
inhibit it. Tracing the meridian from the end 
to the beginning, or using the acupressure 
holding points for sedation will also sedate 
the muscles related to that meridian. On the Neurolymphatic areas, where deep 
massage would strengthen the muscle, a very 
light touch or brushing over the skin with 
the fingers will have a weakening effect. On 
the Neurovascular points, pushing hard, 
massaging firmly, or tapping on the area will Always retest the muscle to be sure it is left 
in the normal state. Because of the innate 
powers within the body, weakening a mus-
cle, either on purpose or accidentally, is 
not nearly as effective or long lasting as the 
strengthening techniques. Keep in mind that 
what we are trying to achieve is a better bal-
ance in the body, equal relative strength and 
freedom from tension. Tension is the result 
of muscle weakness, not muscle strength, and 
balancing the body produces a relaxed state 
of being, both in mind and in body. Reflex System 	
Sedation Technique Spinal Reflexes	
Rub across instead of 
	
up and down Neurolymphatics	
Feather instead of 
	
rubbing Neurovasculars	
Tap or massage firmly 
	
instead of holding 	
	
lightly Meridians	
Trace backwards 	
	
(except Heart*) Sedating & Re-Strengthening a Muscle for 
Simple Pain Control Acupressure Holding Points	
Use sedation points Muscles   	
Use spindles and golgis Sometimes when a muscle has been too pain-
ful to test or is repeatedly weak, it is good to 
strengthen it using one of the methods, weaken 
it using another, restrengthen it, and go back 
and forth several times between strengthening 
and weakening with the different techniques 
to give the muscle a kind of invigorating, alter-
nating power surge. This is often  very helpful 
in problem areas. Be sure to check the muscle 
and ask the person, each time, to discover at 
what point the muscles work and feel best. Remember, the alleviation of pain does not mean that the cause 
of pain has necessarily disappeared. Give injuries time to heal 
and seek medical attention for any problem that persists, worsens, 
returns, or is serious to begin with. *Out of an abundance of caution, in TFH we never use any of the 
sedation reflexes for the Heart Meridian. The acupressure holding 
points listed for sedation of the Heart Meridian are actually the 
tonification points for Small Intestine, which has a calming effect 
on the Heart Meridian. *Out of an abundance of caution, in TFH we never use any of the

280 Meridian Walking When one such point has been identified, continue This technique is often surprisingly useful 
for any localized pain, whether unexplained 
aches,  particular injuries, minor bruises or 
cuts etc. It seems to work best for more recent 
pain, but is worth trying for any pain. For 
long standing chronic pain, the following Pain 
Tapping Technique may be very effective. pressure on that spot until the pain eases in one of the two areas. If the pain at the injury/localized pain disappears or has reached an acceptable level, stop. Otherwise, continue along in the same way until you’ve traced the whole meridian in either, or both directions and all the pain spots are relieved. When pains have been relieved in this way, 
especially when there has been an injury to a 
muscle, it is extremely important to remember 
that even though the pain is gone, the injury 
is still there. Use common sense and get the 
proper attention for any pain or injury. TECHNIQUE Meridian Walking: First, identify the location and assess the pain on a scale of 0-10 with 0 being no pain and 10 being the worst pain. Find which merid- ian runs through or runs nearest the pain. You can Circuit-Locate if there is any doubt which meridian Pain Tapping is closest. Test all the muscles associated with that This technique is excellent for relieving pain, 
particularly with chronic or longstanding pain. Though it may not correct the actual cause of 
the pain, it can give you a lot of natural pain 
relief allowing you to rest and heal. Use your 
common sense and the advice of appropriate 
experts to determine what else you need to do 
for the pain/issue. The person must be able to 
activate the pain, so that it is currently present. meridian. If any are weak, and they probably will be, correct them. If they are tight, weaken them. Often, when this is done, the pain or congestion of energy flow at the pain point is relieved. To further relieve pain in an injured area, put one hand on or hold the hand just over the injury, and hold lightly (Obviously you’re not going to apply pressure to a wound, or make a pain more severe). With this technique you can help to alleviate 
pain by tapping. It may bear no geographical 
relationship to the pain site (unlike Meridian 
Walking for recent pain). Remember, this 
technique does more to override the pain 
rather than balance the actual cause, so listen 
to the message the pain is bringing you and 
consider what else needs to be done. Now, while still holding the original pain point, place

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
283 to “flip over” once. There are 33 columns so 
you can keep track of all 42 muscles during 33 
separate balances.  (See pgs. 284/286) ALTERNATIVE 	 	
BALANCING OPTIONS Postural Analysis One of the easiest ways to see the benefits of 
a 14-muscle balance is to observe the positive 
changes in our posture and attitude. When 
muscles are balanced and pulling evenly on 
the spine, our posture is affected accordingly. Balanced posture has a profound effect on 
how we feel, function and enjoy life. 42-Muscle Balancing in Anatomical Order When we are engaged in a large volume of mus-
cle-balancings on a regular basis, particularly in 
a clinical setting, it may be desirable to test all 
42 muscles in a way that is faster than the tradi-
tional meridian order. We can test the muscles 
in anatomical or “Head to Toe” order, testing 
all the muscles that are close together rather 
than by their meridian relationship. With prac-
tice, this can significantly speed up our assess-
ment of the muscles and meridian energy. If we 
test all of the muscles first and assess the energy 
pattern, it will probably be the same whether 
we test in meridian order or anatomical order. Make an assessment of your own posture. How vibrant and balanced do you feel? Do 
you feel grounded and clear-headed or dis-
connected? Are you aware of any discom-
fort, or any muscles “pulling”? Anything 
else? Now stand facing a mirror or your 
balancing partner so that you can visualize a 
plumb line passing through the top of your 
head,  traveling down the center of your face, 
torso, and evenly between your legs. Observe the degree of symmetry and balance 
of the nose and lips. Is your head level and 
held evenly, or is it tilted or turned? Are your 
ears level? Would a plumb line pass down 
the center of the sternum, through the navel, 
and evenly between the legs and feet? Are 
the shoulders and hips level? Do the hands 
fall evenly? Does one hand fall lower, or fur-
ther out to the side? Are the feet turned out 
evenly? The hands should fall comfortably 
and evenly from the shoulders. Note any 
obvious deviations from balanced posture. When we  “balance-as-you-go” in merid-
ian order, we are constantly sending energy 
“downstream” often balancing other muscles/ 
meridians, so we find fewer “weak” muscles, 
making the balance quicker. But it’s okay to 
balance-as-you-go in anatomical order if you 
prefer. The Checklist (pg. 284) is designed to 
be done lying face up (supine). The last 10 
muscles listed, starting with Posterior Neck 
Extensors, should be done lying face down 
(prone). 284) is designed to 
be done lying face up (supine). The last 10 
muscles listed, starting with Posterior Neck 
Extensors, should be done lying face down 
(prone). They are listed last so you only have

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
290 TECHNIQUE Slight asymmetry is normal, particularly in 
facial features. Postural Analysis: Do the usual prechecks and balance all 42 muscles. Reassess the posture and Turning to one side, have your partner 
imagine the plumb line again, as you stand 
naturally in profile. See if the plumb line 
falls through the ear, shoulder, top of the 
thighbone, knee and front of the ankle. Is the 
head even, tilted, or thrust forward? Is the 
back hunched? Is one shoulder twisted to the 
front or the back? Is there an exaggerated 
curve in the low back and does the belly stick 
way out in front? Are the legs at all hyper-
extended? Do you seem to be “leaning into 
the wind” rather than standing straight up? verbalize any positive improvements. Then identify and balance any individual muscles which still seem to be pulling the posture out of balance. The simple Postural Deviation Chart on pg. 291 goes a long way towards balancing most deviations you see. Note which muscles are implicated in the postural imbalances and recheck them. Even though you just balanced all the muscles, since you still see imbal- ance, some of these muscles will be inhibited when you recheck them. Balance each muscle in the usual way and reassess the posture, emphasizing and cel- If you’re in a dance studio, the mirrors will 
allow you to check your own posture from 
the back, otherwise have your partner check. Notice any deviations to a line falling through 
the center of the head, down the spine, through 
the buttock crease and equally between the 
feet. Again check the head, shoulders, hips, 
hands and feet. Look to see if the shoulder 
blades are held flat on the ribcage. See if the 
buttock creases line up and if the knee creases 
are level. Make one more quarter turn and 
observe the other profile. It’s surprising the 
different things you observe on each side! ebrating any positive improvement that you notice. Reactive Muscles Sometimes we balance the postural deviations, 
but after we start moving around, or after a 
little while, certain deviations come back. This 
may be due to “reactive” patterns among the 
muscles. At some point one muscle switched 
off in relation to another muscle, probably 
at a moment of stress, strain or trauma. This 
response may have prevented or reduced injury 
at the time, but if  some muscles continue to 
react any time another muscle is used, it can 
be a problem. Although each muscle functions 
fine on its own (locked when tested individu-
ally), when we check them in relation to each 
other, some switch off. By resetting this rela-
tionship so that the muscles can function effec-
tively together, we often see postural balance 
improve and last longer.

fine on its own (locked when tested individu-
ally), when we check them in relation to each 
other, some switch off. By resetting this rela-
tionship so that the muscles can function effec-
tively together, we often see postural balance 
improve and last longer. Now move and walk around as naturally as 
possible so your partner can observe your 
dynamic posture. Notice any compensations 
or tightness or imbalance that you feel. Have 
your partner observe how fluid or constricted 
your movements are, and what you seem to 
be expressing through your moving posture.
