text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
291 POSTURAL DEVIATION ANALYSIS CHART Postural Deviation 	
Possible Muscle Weakness Ears not level 	
Neck Muscles, Rhomboids, Sacrospinalis, Psoas, Gluteus Medius, Upper Trapezius Shoulders not level 	
Lat. Dorsi, Neck Muscles, Gluteus Medius, Upper Trapezius, Deltoids, Quad. Lum. Hips not level 	
Psoas, Adductors, Gluteus Medius, Quadratus Lumborum Shoulders twisted 	
Levator Scapulae Head level but twisted 	
Rhomboids, Abdominals, Trapezius, Sacrospinalis Pelvis twisted 	
Psoas, Fascia Lata, Sartorius, Abdominals Head / neck extended forward	
Levator Scapulae, Neck Muscles, Upper, Middle, and Lower Trapezius, Rhomboids Hands turned differently  	
Teres Minor Hand held away from body 	
Gluteus Medius Belly hanging out (sway back)  	
Abdominals, Piriformis, Psoas Belly curved in or back curved out

292 Testing Muscle Pairs It is believed that another option is to 
strengthen the reactive muscle, which will 
“encourage” it to function in conjunction 
with the reactor. In rare cases it may be 
necessary to both inhibit the reactor and 
facilitate the reactive muscle to get them to 
function together without reacting. Any muscles that must work in coordination, 
whether opposing muscles or synergistic mus-
cles, are obvious candidates for reactivity. Sometimes muscles which are “far apart” can 
also react to each other. Look to the table of 
Postural Deviations, or the Reactive Muscle 
table to get an idea of which muscles to check 
in relation to each other. Ideally, balance 
all 42 muscles, and rebalance any muscles 
related to postural deviation. Bully Muscles/Hero Muscles We might think of one muscle as the “Bully” 
which frightens the other muscle. The first 
method is to bench the bully so that the other 
muscles can come out to play. The second 
method is simply to encourage the fearful 
muscles to come out to play. Perhaps more 
productive is the idea of the “Hero” muscle, 
which is pulling most of the weight (and prob-
ably has the tension or pain) while the other 
muscles are not fully engaged. If we give the 
hero a chance to rest, and get the other muscles 
working, then when the hero returns, all can 
work together. It might be enough to simply 
encourage the “lazy muscles,” or you might 
have to rest the hero, and wake up the inhib-
ited muscles. TECHNIQUE Testing Muscle Pairs: To zero in on the muscles that are reacting to each other, test Muscle A, then Muscle B in rapid succession. If B goes weak, we say that muscle A is the reactor, and muscle B is reactive to muscle A. But if both remain strong, reverse the order of the test. Test B first, then imme- diately A. If A goes weak, then A is reactive to B. To reset the relationship, inhibit the reactor (strong) muscle by the Spindle Cell technique•. Using both hands, go to the belly of the muscle and with short, firm strokes, press in toward the center of the muscle. Retest to see that the muscle is weak, and quickly recheck the reactive muscle, which should Reactive Pattern Shortcut now be strong. Ph.D. have found that reactive muscles can 
also involve many different sets of muscles 
in combination. There is a short-cut test we 
use to determine when these reactive patterns 
are present. few seconds to reset itself. Take a breath and then

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
293 TECHNIQUE Facilitation and Inhibition For movement of the body to occur, some 
muscles need to contract and other mus-
cles need to relax in a coordinated way. Movement of a limb is brought about by the 
agonist or prime mover going from extension 
to contraction, i.e. from the position of mini-
mal strength at greatest length to the position 
of maximal strength in the shortest, most 
contracted position. Considering the simple 
example of bending the arm at the elbow and 
raising a glass of water to our lips, the Bicep 
in the arm are the prime movers or agonists, 
and must smoothly contract in coordination 
with the Tricep, the antagonists, that are 
simultaneously relaxing and extending. If 
the Tricep were to contract, we would then 
have a struggle between the agonists (Bicep) 
and the antagonists (Tricep). The outcome 
would either be no movement with the arm 
in a frozen state, or jerky, poorly coordinated 
movement either toward or away from the 
mouth, depending on relative forces exerted 
by the agonists and antagonists. Reactive Pattern Shortcut: Hold one hand over the person’s head and test an IM. If the IM unlocks, then we say that a reactive pattern is present. To balance, use the “Switch On” exercise or hold the navel while holding the ESR points. Both of these correction procedures work well at different times. If one doesn’t strengthen the muscle, try the other (or use the same one for a little bit longer). During the Reactive Pattern Shortcut, the hand is over the 
head without touching. CHALLENGE Once the IM tests strong with a hand held over For fluid, well coordinated movement to 
occur, there must be a balanced, coordi-
nated, gradual relaxation of antagonists and 
contraction of agonists. The stimulation of 
agonists is called Facilitation,  the relaxing 
of antagonists is called Inhibition. If we take 
this concept to whole body movements of flu-
idity, coordination and precision, it becomes 
obvious that a great deal of communication 
through our proprioceptive networks and 
energy circuits is necessary. the head, challenge by wiggling and squirming, attempting to use as many muscles in as many different actions as possible, including the eyes, mouth and tongue, for at least 15 seconds. Retest with the hand over the head. If this causes the IM to become inhibited again, repeat the balancing exercise(s) and retest the IM with hand over head. Do this as many times as necessary until the wiggling and squirming does not cause the IM to unlock with the hand over the head.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
299 Balancing with Food Most of these things would normally be 
included in a complete diet. However, if 
a person for some reason has a particular 
need or is not getting enough of one of these 
things, this can show up as a persistent weak-
ness of certain muscles. The TFH System can be very useful in con-
junction with common sense and sound 
knowledge of food and nutrition. We can 
use muscle checking to see which foods 
enhance our energy and balance, which foods 
are energetically neutral and which foods 
inhibit our energy. This is some very helpful 
additional information to use when we are 
balancing our diet. We can also use partic-
ular foods to balance particular meridians, 
or the whole meridians system. The Foods 
for Balancing in the Functions/Indications 
column of the reference pages lists the kinds 
of foods that typically balance or inhibit the 
muscles/meridians. These nutritional elements seem to help the 
most when they are taken in their natural 
form as food and chewed thoroughly, rather 
than in a highly concentrated pill or synthetic 
vitamin. Chewing does more than chop the 
food into smaller pieces and mix the saliva 
with the food, beginning the first stage of 
digestion and the breakdown of the food into 
the different elements and fuels. The chemical 
reactions that take place in the mouth with the 
saliva are enough to trigger the brain to call 
out all the necessary action from the rest of 
the body which will be required to process the 
food. This seems to be a very important but 
little-known function of the mouth, which is 
evidently a very sensitive mechanism. Nutrition Lately there has been a lot of commotion 
about good nutrition -vitamins, proteins, 
carbohydrates and health foods. Much new 
information is coming out all the time, but 
the basics are clear. Proper nutrition is abso-
lutely essential to good health. The simplest 
guide is to eat only whole foods. If a food is 
processed or broken down into another form 
- avoid it. Stay as close to the natural state of 
the food as is reasonable. The ground rule is 
always, “fresh food is best.” Frozen food is 
usually better than canned, but the goal is to 
eat a variety of natural foods in their natural 
state and to eat all the different parts of them. Strengthening with Food Persistent weakness in a particular muscle 
is a possible clue to nutritional deficiency 
sometimes associated with the muscle. The 
muscle test does not determine specific clini-
cal nutritional deficiency, although it can be 
a clue for those who are trained in this kind 
of assessment.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
300 TECHNIQUE A healthy person should drink a minimum of 
1/3 of an ounce of water for every pound of 
body weight, and at least double that in times 
of stress or illness. This means drinking at 
least 6-8 glasses of water each day. A healthy 
person can easily handle up to 5 gallons of 
water a day. This means pure, distilled water. Coffee, tea, fruit juice, milk and other liquids 
don’t count. They are processed in the body as 
food, whereas what the body needs is water. We can’t substitute other liquids for water any 
more than we would want to fill the battery in 
the car with milk, the steam iron with tomato 
juice, or wash the walls with coffee. Strengthening with Food: After checking a mus- cle and finding it inhibited, chew for a few moments on one of the suggested foods containing a high level of certain nutrients. Recheck the muscle to see which food switches the muscle on. This reaction is almost instantaneous, once the food has mixed with the saliva and has been in contact with the mouth. CHALLENGE Challenge by swallowing the food and retesting. If the muscles are switched off again, more of the For good performance, clarity of thinking 
and proper mind/body function, and smooth 
movement of the muscles, the body depends 
greatly on water. Without pure water the 
body’s electrical system is impaired and the 
lymph system and organ function suffers. We say that a person is Relatively dehydrated 
on a subtle energy level if indicated by the 
Hydration Pretest. This means that there 
is an energetic issue related to water. This 
may or may not relate to our physical level 
of hydration. When we drink water, we may 
not immediately be rehydrated, but now the 
information is in the system that water is 
available. Now our natural thirst may be 
activated, or we may be more aware of it, 
or distinguish thirst from hunger or other 
cravings. Try drinking water first if you have 
excessive hunger or cravings. food or another reflex is needed. Water An area often neglected is drinking water. Second only to air, water is a primary phys-
ical need. Our bodies are made up of about 
70% water. We are dependent on the fluid 
flow of the different systems in our bodies. Every living cell requires water, just as it 
does nutrients and oxygen. Water acts as a 
solvent in the body, and the purer it is, free 
from minerals, softeners, and pollutants, the 
more body toxins can be let into it and the 
more nutrients it can carry to the body cells.

301 ance, check in with yourself to see if you’re 
drinking enough pure water. The hydration 
pretest reminds us to drink water, and when

that are biocidic. If you are having digestive problems, identi-

foods, and increasing intake of biogenic 
foods, may be particularly valuable. Of 
course, things other than food and drink 
have an effect on the body and our energy. Some supplements (or the amount taken) can 
switch muscles off – as do alcohol and ciga-
rette smoke in most cases. Industrial fumes 
and airborne irritants – such as pollen or 
fungus – should be considered too. Processed 
foods often inhibit our energy and do not 
have the same nutritional value as the foods 
will generally have in their natural form. Food Testing It’s an individual matter which foods build a 
person’s health and enhance or balance their 
energy and which are draining on the system. For each of us there are some foods which 
have a debilitating effect, creating stress

303 REMEMBERING 
WELLNESS: IN-DEPTH 
GOAL-SETTING 
PROTOCOL It is very important that you LISTEN and 
acknowledge the person first. Allow  all the 
participants to be themselves and express their 
own purpose(s), pain and meaning. Is this 
YOUR purpose, or one which someone else, or 
circumstances have imposed on you? Each Soul is in charge of their own goal(s). The individual Soul is the authority in their 
Wellness process. When we remember wellness with Touch 
for Health, our aim is to assist ourselves, 
our family, friends, and clients to BE WHO 
WE ARE, WHO WE WERE MEANT, 
CREATED, AND SPOKEN FORTH TO BE. We use active listening, questioning, discus-
sion and energy balancing TFH techniques 
in a supportive dialogue process of becoming 
aware of, developing, and coming into bal-
ance with our own unique purpose(s) in life. Use Active Listening to clarify what the 
person feels, what they believe about their 
thoughts, pains, emotions, and other aspects 
of their experience of life. What do they 
believe is the cause of their feelings? What 
meaning does this have for them? 2. IDENTIFY THE ISSUE & SET A GOAL Engage the WHOLE SOUL with a goal that 
you can be enthusiastic about, that you gen-
uinely, (whole-heartedly) WANT. 10 Steps to Setting Wellness Oriented Goals The following 10 steps provide goal-setting 
guidelines that can be used step-by-step or as 
needed. Sometimes only some parts of the pro-
tocol will be needed or appropriate, sometimes 
you will benefit from each part, and of course, 
in real life you will integrate your own knowl-
edge and experience to make it more effective. 
(The Protocol is first presented here as an 
outline, followed by an in-depth and detailed 
commentary.) What do you want to improve in your life? What performance, activity, or (emotional, 
practical, physical) problem or issue do you 
want to address in the goal-setting, ener-
gy-balancing process? Is it appropriate for YOU? Is it a goal that 
you can own, accept for yourself, that you 
can feel like you deserve. (Not forced, no 
shoulds). Is it at an appropriate comfort level 
(NOT so small as to be insignificant, and not 
a goal that is really just going to stress you 
out, or is completely Utopian) Is it in keeping 
with your values and beliefs? 1. ESTABLISH  A MUTUAL UNDERSTANDING Establish a cooperative encounter/process in 
which each individual decides for themselves 
and says start or stop, more or less, yes or no, 
harder or softer, now, later, or never.

ing their desire to keep silent. On the other

be enthusiastic about, it often creates a sense 
of joy and hope for fulfillment and change

formative, creative, healing energy. 1. ESTABLISH A MUTUAL UNDERSTANDING Who’s in charge? Who’s the authority? Goal setting can help clarify expectations

encounter. Your assumptions or the assump-
tions of the person you are helping may be 
inaccurate or conflicting. Do you know 
what is most important to yourself, your 
friend, student, client, or patient? Perhaps 
the person you’re working with assumes that 
you will automatically understand “what is 
wrong” and how best to “get it fixed,” or 
they have their own preconceived idea of 
the best way to fix it, but assume that you 
are already in agreement with them, “It goes 
without saying.” Remember: Only you know 
exactly how you feel and what you think and

ple accomplishment or it can be a goal that 
will take all of your energies and life commit-
ment. What performance, activity, problem,

do you want to improve? Sometimes our 
physical pains and postures, our inhibitions 
and restrictions of movement can tell us 
something about what we do or don’t want 
to be doing, so our life goals might flow out 
of simpler, immediate functional goals. Or 
we may have a long-term goal that is blocked 
or made more difficult by physical symptoms 
that we try to ignore. If we take a moment 
to listen to ourselves, the messages that 
our symptoms may represent, often provide Determining what you are being asked to do 
is an important first step. If you are acting 
as a holistic practitioner, what are your goals 
for your clients? If you are getting help for 
yourself from another person (professional 
or family member, acquaintance or friend) 
what do you want the outcome to be in your 
lived life?

and they seem to have no idea what they feel 
their goal should be, I might use muscle test-
ing to find a general area that is appropriate. I’ll have the person say various words related 
to general categories such as family, money, 
sex, job, home, work, career, etc. When there 
is an inhibition of the muscle when you say 
the word, think about what comes to mind 
related to the word. The issue may not be the 
logical meaning of the word itself, but what 
you personally associate with it. Now that 
you have an issue in mind, formulate your 
goal related to the issue. You may need to ask yourself if your goal is 
to change someone else or to change your-
self. Often, simply recognizing that you are 
trying to change someone else can lead to a 
lot of relief as you focus on what you do have 
power to change, which is your own attitude, 
posture and behavior. If the Goal Causes Confusion You may feel conflicted about a goal. Part of 
you may really want the goal, but part of you 
may not want what goes with it. Getting bal-
anced for your goal may help you to accept the 
sacrifices involved, or it may help you realize 
that it’s not something you really want. In our model of self-discovery and self-re-
sponsibility, we don’t say that a given word 
or category (that causes the Indicator Muscle 
to change) necessarily means anything in 
and of itself. It is for the individual to con-
template what this might mean for them. If 
the general category isn’t enough of a clue 
to direct you to an appropriate goal, you 
can check more specifically. Maybe “work” 
causes the IM inhibition but nothing specific 
comes to mind related to work. You might 
try saying the names of coworkers, or feel-
ings related to the work situation, or partic-
ular tasks or projects, etc. Usually when you 
get to a more specific aspect or aspects, the 
light bulb goes on. Now that you’ve identi-
fied an issue you want to work with, develop 
your personal positive goal for this situation. Where the goal is “fuzzy” or the enthusiasm 
for a positive goal is “soggy”, there may 
be some disassociation of the Soul with the 
actual life of the person. You may know 
in your head what your goal is supposed to 
be, but you aren’t really living it, you aren’t 
enlivened by your “own” purposes, perhaps 
because you don’t really own them.

vertical scales,  bar graphs, or circles that are 
divided by percentages. The most valuable

ally achieve your goal, you’re much more 
likely to make it happen (and know it when 
you see it!)  Act as if you “know” that your 
goal is already accomplished. Now it simply 
needs to unfold. In Christianity, this is the principle of praying 
with the knowledge 
that your prayers 
will be answered. This can also be 
seen in the power 
of positive affirma-
tion statements in 
personal 
growth 
and change. “Go confidently in the direction of your dream. Act as if it is impossible to fail.”   
— Thoreau Using analog scales has been recognized as 
an important method of understanding the 
pain phenomena. They are useful in noting 
the differences in pain in different circum-
stances, in measuring and being AWARE of 
improvements (and having hope). Starting 
with a horizontal line, we can mark one end, 
“No Pain” and have the other end represent, 
“Worst Pain Ever.”  We mark the current 
pain on the scale both before and after bal-
ancing. Measuring the pain makes it more 
real, and possibly more excruciating as we 
become completely aware of it, but that same 
awareness seems to contribute greatly to the 
relief we feel. Set It Up Make note of your planned outcomes. How 
will you know that your goal is achieved? Break the goal into measurable steps. When will 
you be sure to check in on your goal to see that

it is often an excruciating process of search-
ing our hearts to find something we are truly

Then, rephrase the goal slightly in the following ways: Measuring the Attractor Value a. I will … In Touch for Health, when we balance 
energy, we improve the equilibrium of the 
person among their various muscles, merid-
ians, etc. We also improve the harmoni-
ous balance between the individual and 
the environment- the universe. We can use 
muscle-testing to measure our “attractor 
value” - the degree to which we are in tune 
with the context of our lives, and in balance 
within our surroundings. This is the kind of 
energetic state that naturally attracts into our 
lives those people and activities that allow us 
to reach our goals. In contrast, we can also 
have an energetic state that seems to repel 
people, or prevent us from getting what we 
want in life. This is something we can add 
to our usual goal balancing, so once you have 
done your usual pretests, found a goal and 
rated it on a subjective analog scale (1-10), 
follow this procedure: b. I want to … c. I choose to…. 2. Inhibition of the IM on one or more of these statements gives us more detail about the stress in the system related to the goal. 3. Test an IM while making the statement: “For my goal of ______, I have an attractor value of (a number between 1 and 1000  — a thousand being the best.) Unless your value is lower than your estimate, the IM will remain strong until you reach a maximum number — your attrac- tor value. If your first estimate causes an IM change, keep lowering your estimate until you find the maximum number for which the IM locks. Use ranges, like, “between 500 and 600,” to establish the general area. Keep narrowing the range until you locate a precise maximum number, such as 547. Make a note of the num- ber before balancing and be sure to recheck it after balancing.

and relating these ideas to your goal can 
often provide surprising insights as well

around these issues.

plex neurological/energetic construct. ABOUT THE

METAPHORS From finding an emotion related to a goal to

incorporated into our balancing sessions. The 5-Element Metaphors, the Organ 
Function metaphors and metaphors derived 
from the test motions/muscle functions offer 
a rich resource for exploring the meanings of 
our experiences, our feelings, our imbalances

related imbalances. When we think about a 
metaphor related to an imbalance indicated

step process of development through small, 
everyday insights as we deal with our prob-
lems, our life’s work, and seek our Telos, the 
purpose in life we were born to fulfill. Touch for Health  combines the Ancient 
Chinese Perception of Life Energy (the basis 
of Acupuncture and Oriental Medicine) with 
Western Kinesiology (Bio-Feedback  through 
Muscle-Monitoring) and Western Touch 
Reflexes (Neurolymphatic,  Neurovascular,

a Creative Dialogue Process (using Positive 
Goal Setting & Consideration of the Symbolic 
Metaphors of the Chinese 5 Elements & 14 
primary Meridians, as well as thinking meta-
phorically about muscle function and action) 
allows for a holistic clearing and balancing of 
physical/structural, subtle energetic, mental/
emotional & sensory/associative aspects of 
health and Wellness.

wounded, damaged or in denial. Muscle Metaphors Muscles are not used in isolation. For every

relax or be inhibited, some muscles must

326 When  using TFH for relief from pain, we 
may delve very deeply into an individual’s 
actual experience of pain. However, our 
focus remains on the whole person, the Soul, 
and the meaning that pain has within the 
context of a lived life. In order to learn what 
the pain is communicating to our conscious-
ness we may use all of our senses, emotions, 
thoughts and intuition to become very aware 
of the full experience and influence of pain. This is in almost diametric contrast to the 
popular culture of pill-popping, in which 
killing pain is seen as the only real objective. When we ignore or mask our physical pain 
with pain-killing drugs, and our  emotional/
mental/spiritual pains with socially accept-
able drugs such as alcohol, tobacco, or other 
“recreational” drugs,  we may only succeed 
in killing our own awareness  of a signal that 
some danger is present. TOUCH FOR HEALTH 
AND RELIEF FROM 
PAIN Helping People Understand and Manage 
Pain and Suffering Western cultures tend to divide the person 
into parts which seem to be distinct and 
replaceable. Although 
this approach has some 
benefits and may pro-
long life, this is a lim-
ited approach to pain 
and suffering. We tend 
to use medical termi-
nology to describe ill-
ness as some force from 
outside the body which 
we must kill. Where  a 
disease entity cannot be 
killed or cured, the per-
son then often takes on 
the name of a disease. People are labeled as 
diabetic, drug-addict, 
hypoglycemic. They 
come to see their lives, 
and their very being 
in terms of disease, and the assumptions of 
pre-defined limitations. Pain and suffering indicate a In TFH we look at pain, acknowledging that 
it has an important and useful role in our 
lives, and search for its meaning. The TFH 
approach is an ongoing process of develop-
ing our  awareness of what is happening in 
our lives, and in that context, pain is a very 
useful indicator. The TFH concept of health 
is more than the absence of pain,  dis-ease or 
symptoms. It is more than the biostatistical 
parameters of normalcy. blockage of the life energy which allows Health and Wellness. Health is the promise everyone is born with, 
a birthright of having meaning in the lived 
life of a real person, a Soul. Health is a pro-
cess of experiencing well-being,  physically, 
mentally, emotionally, spiritually, socially

dition in the physical posture before we

perception. Muscle testing and energy bal-
ancing provide us with a concrete awareness

insights into our own experience. Each unique Soul can be thought of as a set

and lower limits set by our experiences and 
design. When we have been injured in any 
of the aspects of our Soul, in order to adapt, 
we can set our thermostats to narrower lim-
its and reduced sine waves of functioning. Using muscle tests to determine imbalances is 
an effective way of changing the metaphori-
cal thermostat setting to allow for a greater 
range of functioning, greater adaptability 
and more “peak performances” and “per-
sonal bests.” Postural Improvement and

328 Pain Control and Personal Boundaries about the meaning of life and what would 
give their lives meaning. One question is 
“What would you be doing with your energy 
if you were not using it on this conflict? In sorting out your confusion? In standing 
still?”  The process of making choices that 
are truthful for the Soul, by being curious 
about what might be their purpose and 
meaning in life starts the flow. Harmony and 
balance return more quickly to the Soul. But 
we also need to recognize that not everything 
can be dealt with at once. You may not need to 
deal with every question in a particular person. You can stop at any time and do the balance 
with or without a spoken goal. There is a great 
benefit in balancing without orally speaking 
the goals, allowing the Soul (the whole person) 
to silently know why they are asking for the 
intervention by you. Whenever we begin a touching relationship 
with another person, 
we need to estab-
lish boundaries and 
safety. We need to 
ask, “Is there any rea-
son I cannot muscle 
test you?” Establish 
the idea that it’s okay 
and a good idea to 
stop immediately if 
you feel pain, or feel 
uncomfortable at any 
time. Use any pain 
increase as the same 
as a muscle inhibition 
when muscle testing. When there is pain 
getting into the test 
position or range of 
motion, I consider it 
an indication that there is an imbalance in that 
meridian. I then use the appropriate  balancing 
method and notice the change in pain as an 
indicator  instead of the muscle test. After 
you have balanced the pain in the position and 
range, you may then be able to gently do the 
muscle test. The Soul will spontaneously reorganize its cycles in the direction of health when enough self-knowledge prevails. The efficacy of what happens is more import-
ant to the intervention than the mechanism 
which allowed it to happen. We want favor-
able outcomes for the lived life of the people 
involved more than we want to understand 
the mystery of how it happened. We honor 
the mystery of this healing system that is 
designed into the Soul. We do not need to 
know and we acknowledge that we will not 
fully know how the healing really takes place. We are interested in the mechanisms of how 
things happen, but we also recognize that the 
mystery of how the healing system actually 
functions will never be completely known. It is important to remember that pain from 
our view is seen as a friend rather than an 
enemy.

T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
329 any issue we might be dealing with, positive 
or negative, in place of the word “pain”, and 
gain insight into our struggles or endeavors 
by contemplating the following aspects. first time you ever had a pain similar to this 
present pain? Is this a new or an ongoing 
pain? Have you ever been without the pain 
since it began? How long did the pain last, 
and how long did you remain pain-free? What brought it on again? What aggravates 
the pain? When the pain leaves, does it leave 
suddenly or is it gone after sleeping, or doing 
something that lets you forget about it? Points to Consider When Dealing with Pain 1. KNOW YOUR PAIN Where exactly is the pain located? Is the pain 
deep or superficial? Is it sharp, dull, aching, 
stabbing, burning? Even emotional pains 
usually have a strong physical component. Locate your feelings. Do you feel it in your 
stomach, heart, head? What kind of posture 
do your feelings create? What movements/ 
activities are inhibited? Does it seem to 
change with activity? What activity? Where 
does the pain seem to be coming from? What 
is the cause? You may know specifically, 
have a vague idea, or maybe just an inner 
knowing, or no idea at all. 3. EFFECT OF PAIN ON LIVING AND WORKING How does having this pain keep you from 
having health and an experience of joy and 
well-being? How is pain keeping you from 
performing duties either at your job, in your 
family, as a husband, etc.? Does it affect your 
sex life? If pain or disability were to disap-
pear instantaneously, would that solve the 
problem? How do you feel about resuming 
the pain-hampered tasks at home, at work, 
or socially? Perhaps your pain is a means of 
survival, or avoidance of some emotionally 
painful situation. Is there another way of cop-
ing with these issues other than becoming crip-
pled with pain? What would you truly love to 
be doing that you are not doing either because 
of the presence of pain or because you are 
afraid to cause more pain? What do you really 
desire to do, whether pain is stopping you or 
not. Perhaps pain is nudging you away from 
something you don’t want, and if you turn in 
the direction of your true goals, you will feel 
better. The Soul is made to be itself. You have 
power when you know your purpose and have 
the enthusiasm to pursue it. What does the pain look like? Relating the 
pain to a metaphor , activating the imagina-
tion and intuition relating to the pain, allows 
a more holistic sense of the pain as it relates 
to all aspects of the Soul.

ance energy for improvement of the life of the 
person, not to cure any condition or disease. It can be very important and powerful to con-
sider diagnosis and treatment to see what  is the 
greater meaning in the life of the person. Were 
you treated well, both in terms of respect and 
dignity and effective help with your injury or 
illness? Have you been labeled or pronounced 
hopeless, incurable, or maybe neurotic? 5. HEREDITARY POSSIBILITIES What do you believe about your own genetic, 
inherited  predispositions? Have members of 
your family suffered from similar pain? To 
what extent do you behave or function like 
your family, parents or role models? Do you 
feel like your genes or the influences of signif-
icant others determines your experience  of 
life, or do you have significant choices which 
can change your life? To what extent does 
this relate to your pain? 6. FALLS, ACCIDENTS, INJURIES AND SURGERY Have you had any surgery, whether or not 
you relate that to your pain? Do you have any 
scars  from surgery or other injury, whether 
they are physical or emotional scars, related 
to trauma of injury, or context of trauma? What falls, accidents, mis-steps have you had 
whether physically or metaphorically? Have 
you slipped on the ice, or taken a wrong turn 
in your career or relationship? Is your life or 
relationship “on the rocks”? 8. PRIVACY ISSUES It is important to be aware of what infor-
mation is appropriate to share or not share 
given the context of your balancing. It is 
NOT appropriate to keep a secret from a per-
son who is touching you if this might result 
in some worsening of an injury or medical 
condition.
section: 8. PRIVACY ISSUES

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
331 Using Analog Pain Scales some aspects of your life that are personal, 
private, or secret . Be aware of what sub-
jects you feel you cannot or will not or are 
forbidden to talk about. Consider thinking 
about them during a balancing session and 
see what imbalances may be revealed to you. Perhaps there is something that you DO want 
to share,  or there is important information 
regarding your health that may or may not 
relate  directly to your pain. This is an important method to assist you in 
understanding the pain. These scales are useful 
in noting the differences in the pain and how 
much improvements have been made. We can 
rate the pain today on a continuum from “no 
pain” on one end of the scale and “the worst 
pain ever experienced”  at the other end. It’s 
very powerful to have the person experienc-
ing pain rate themselves by marking on the 
scale where the pain is prior to beginning the 
intervention, and again after the intervention. Even a small change is an indication of HOPE, 
which can make all the difference. Keeping 
track of how and when the pain manifests can 
also help manage and understand the pain. 9. REMEMBERING WELLNESS When was the last time that you felt really 
good, whole and in harmony with your envi-
ronment? Have you ever had those feelings? Can you imagine it? How do you want to 
feel? How will that change your life? How 
will it feel? If you could do it today, would 
you? If not, why not? When you no longer 
have the pain, WHAT WILL YOU DO? (See 
Remembering Wellness Goal Setting Protocol, 
pg. 310) 10. SET IT UP Set a goal that you are excited and enthusias-
tic about. Decide how you will measure your 
improvement and how often you will get an 
assessment and a balancing. Planning the 
goal and desired outcome is more important 
than any other part of the entire balancing 
technique and intervention.
section: 10. SET IT UP

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
332 Each person must be given the ability to 
recognize the need for help, and our society 
must change so that seeking help of another 
is admired and not looked upon as a sign of 
inferiority. Health in man is an interdepen-
dent phenomenon in which the well-being of 
each one of us is dependent upon the well-be-
ing of each other. The extent to which we 
guard and assist the health of others is the 
extent to which others will guard and assist 
our health. CONCLUSION The goal of most health care practitioners 
in America is the same as the stated goal of 
Touch for Health: Treat the whole person, 
not merely the symptoms; maintain health 
rather than just treat diseases. The problem 
for the harried doctor is to be able to do that 
with the limited time and resources allowed 
by our current system. It is generally agreed that a great deal of 
pathology (illness) of later life is dependent 
on what happens as a result of our experi-
ences in early life, when we all had the com-
mon factor of growth and development and 
were being shaped and reshaped within our 
genetic capabilities. It is the ultimate objec-
tive of our lives to establish and maintain 
healthy growth toward being well-balanced 
people, capable of both self-sufficiency and 
interdependent relationships. Health is not 
merely a passive state of freedom from dis-
ease, but an active condition of being, of 
growth, of development, and evolution. There is an ongoing trend to expand com-
plaint, or disease-focused clinics and philan-
thropies around the country. While it cer-
tainly sounds good to help 
people with a given diag-
nosed condition or named 
disease, and “find the 
cure,” this trend can also 
be seen as a breakdown of 
proper health care delivery 
systems. Complaint and 
disease-centered medicine 
has value, and the research 
does lead to benefit for 
people who have devel-
oped serious illness, but 
the focus on disease can 
distract from the whole-person, health and 
wellness enhancement approach. The ultimate responsibility for wellness Postural and energetic balance is something 
that is not given enough emphasis in our mod-
ern cultures. It has been a grossly neglected 
area in American health care, but lately the 
cries of our educators do not always fall on 
deaf ears. Until now, except for the correction 
of major deformities or where extreme pos-
tural deviation is causing major complaints, 
posture has mostly been ignored. resides within the individ- ual.
section: 10. SET IT UP

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
333 Since postural balance has not been taught, 
many of us have learned through observation 
and mimicry of others with poor posture. We see children who have the same ways of 
walking, standing and sitting as their parents, 
even when the parents have gross postural 
faults. Poor posture and inferior use of our 
bodies spreads from generation to generation, 
and it appears to be a contagious process. It 
is our belief that good natural posture and 
improvement of the structural health of the 
public can be improved by starting to observe 
and balance the muscles and meridians with 
the TFH system. can be our advocates when we are injured or 
very sick. In the sick role we may give up the 
position of first authority as to our best treat-
ment, but we still want to be aware, to the full-
est extent possible, of our condition and out-
look, and make sure there is some advocacy 
for treatment/outcomes that are right for us. WHEN WE ARE RECOVERING/REHABILITATING, we 
may or may not have some ongoing treat-
ment regime, but we again take responsibility 
for the ongoing choices we make that lead to 
improvement in quality of life. We may need 
to accept temporary or ongoing limitations 
or challenges, but find fulfillment within our 
current range of function. However, in our 
culture, we sometimes expend a great deal of 
energy and resources prolonging life, when 
our efforts might be better spent focusing on 
having a good death. Four Health Roles When optimizing our health, we need to be 
aware of our different roles according to our 
health status. The goals for our health, and 
the focus of our activities can be quite differ-
ent, depending on whether we are: Healthy, 
Sick, Recovering/Rehabilitating, or Dying. EVEN IN DEATH, there is great potential for 
quality experiences. There are things that 
we want to focus on, and things aren’t as 
important. When in the dying role, we want 
to let go of heroic measures meant to “save” 
us, which might prolong “life” yet distract us 
from our goals. We want to avail ourselves of 
treatment that will allow us to make the most 
of the time we have with the ones we love. WHEN HEALTHY, we’re aware of our health 
and energy needs, we are proactive and 
take responsibility for the choices that lead 
to greater vitality and fulfillment. When we 
consult with a professional, we want to be 
informed and formulate our own opinion 
based on our personal knowledge of our-
selves together with the advice of experts. Your Next Step We hope this book, the seminars that we 
teach, and the trainers we train in postural 
improvement will be steps toward develop-
ing the science of health enhancement.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
334 with the freedom to be aware of their minor 
needs, and to have more of these needs met 
at their request by their own intimate group. This is not to take away from the art and sci-
ence of treating disease, but to augment and 
strengthen the forces of health in the body, 
mind, emotions and spirit in the constructive 
and creative aspects of life. We request your help in giving this infor-
mation to those in positions to lead, and 
in your own sphere of influence, to begin 
to enhance the health of yourself and your 
immediate society. The time is now for the 
wave of health enhancement with Touch for 
Health, to roll across this nation and around 
the world. The idea of developing health and vitality as 
a preventative act is an ancient and time-hon-
ored principle. To see it regain respect and 
popularity in our culture is refreshing, as 
the media and a growing number of profes-
sionals embrace it’s benefits in both health 
research and healthcare. It is still the most 
effective way to reduce the vast number of 
people who are currently being maintained in 
a relative state of ill-health. The place to begin to use all methods of 
health enhancement is in the home, where 
individual families assume the responsibility 
and joy of being aware of each other more 
fully. The improvement in communication 
skills, that will be acquired through caring 
physical contact, will be great indeed. The old saying that, “An ounce of pre-
vention is worth a pound of cure” may be 
paraphrased to say, “An ounce of health 
enhancement is worth a pound of cure.” 
Changing the attitudes required to use these 
methods effectively and enhance the health of 
our society will not be easy. Having the new 
skills delivered to those who need them will 
require patience and imagination.

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H 
353 ABOUT THE 
AUTHORS John Thie, DC Health, USC Dental School Acupuncture Study 
Group, and the Olympic Training Center 
in Warsaw, Poland. In 1978, he introduced 
Touch For Health to medical personnel 
in Peking, Shanghai, and Nanking in the 
Peoples Republic of China. He was found-
ing Chairman of the International College 
of Applied Kinesiology and was a founding 
member of the International Examining Board 
in 1975. Dr. John Thie was 
the Founder of the 
Touch for Health 
System, which is 
now being taught 
and practiced in 
over 100 countries 
worldwide, and is 
regarded as the fun-
damental and semi-
nal work in Energy 
Kinesiology 
and 
Energy Psychology. A respected Chiropractor, he was Director 
of the Thie Chiropractic Clinic in Pasadena, 
California. His practice, founded in 1957, 
was considered one of the most successful in 
the United States. Dr. Thie was a graduate of 
the University of Southern California and the 
Los Angeles College of Chiropractic. He did 
additional graduate work at UCLA. After retiring from his daily practice, Dr. Thie and his wife Carrie traveled to Europe, 
Australia, New Zealand, Japan, China, 
South America, and Africa as he maintained 
an extensive seminar and lecture schedule 
teaching and training professionals, para-
professionals, and lay people. He was often 
the keynote speaker at Kinesiology/TFHA 
national and international meetings. Dr. Thie died on August 3, 2005 and is sur-
vived by Carrie, his wife of 53 years, and their 
family of three sons and five grandchildren. His legacy is carried on by his family and 
by countless practitioners and instructors 
throughout the world who continue to touch
for the purpose of health, and pass the light 
of his enthusiasm and knowledge to future 
generations. Dr. Thie was a past faculty member of the 
Sports Medicine Department of Pepperdine 
University, the Center for Holistic Studies of 
Antioch University West, as well as the exten-
sions of UC, San Diego, Riverside, and Valley

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
354 Matthew Thie, M.Ed. for their ongoing, day-to-day optimization 
of life experience. His emphasis is on imme-
diately putting the simple techniques into 
personal practice, and making the concepts 
and techniques personally meaningful. From 
1996-2005, Matthew  worked extensively 
with his father, Dr. John F. Thie, the founder 
of the Touch for Health system, co-author-
ing articles and books, organizing seminars, 
teaching the basic Touch for Health 60 
hour program, and speaking at national and 
international Energy &  Kinesiology confer-
ences. In 2003, Matthew began training TFH 
Instructors in both English and Spanish in the 
USA, Puerto Rico, and Mexico. Also in 2003, 
Matthew co-authored the TFH Pocketbook 
with Chinese 5 Elements, and he has since 
taught the TFH Metaphor Workshop to over 
1000 students throughout Europe, North 
and South America, Australasia, Japan and 
Hong  Kong and trained over 100 TFH 
Metaphor instructors. Matthew serves as 
the TFHE President, and as part of the IKC 
Executive Board continues to teach both 
the TFH weekend classes, and the Intensive 
Clinical TFH Immersion courses that John 
held at Serra Retreat in Malibu, California, 
in addition to an extensive international 
schedule of teaching. As the third son of Dr. John & Carrie Thie, 
Matthew grew up with Touch for Health 
(TFH) 
and 
Effective 
Communication 
skills as part of his everyday family system. Matthew approaches 
the 
muscle/energy 
balancing work from 
a grassroots perspec-
tive, sharing ener-
gy-balancing one-to-
one with friends and 
family, following the 
each-one-teach-one 
model. Matthew 
studied 
Literature 
at the University of 
California at Berkeley 
and 
received 
his 
Masters in Education 
and Bilingual Cross-
cultural Teaching credential from UCLA 
in 1990. As a formally trained educator, 
Matthew presents the TFH system as a learn-
ing process that anyone can be trained to use

WORKSHOPS TFH Level 4: Postural Analysis, Review & 
Consolidation TFH Weekend Workshops TFH 1-4 Series: This 4-weekend, 60-hour training pro-
gram will give you practical hands-on experience in the 
TFH System of Energetic and Postural balancing. • 	 42 Muscles Balance, Standing, Sitting, or Lying 
(Meridian & Anatomical Order) • 	 Postural Stress Release & more! TFH Level 1: 14-Muscle/Meridian Balance • 	 PRE-REQUISITE: TFH 1&2 • 	Accurate Indicator Muscle Testing TFH Clinical Intensive • 	 Emotional Stress Release • 	 Check 14 muscles as biofeedback for posture and 
14 major Energy Meridians TFH 1-4 in IMMERSION format at beautiful Serra 
Retreat in the Malibu Hills, over-looking the Lagoon 
and Pacific Ocean. Experience balancing for your own 
authentic goals, both as demonstration for the class, 
and as independent practice with your class peers. Emphasis on each member receiving several balances 
to FEEL it, and observations of many demonstrations. NO PRE-REQUISITE • 	 Learn 5 Reflex Systems for Balancing Muscles, 
Posture and  Energy & more! TFH Level 2: One-Point Balancing • 	Assess Energy Patterns in the 24-hour wheel & 
Five Element Cycles • 	 14 Additional Muscle Tests TFH Goal-Setting & Metaphor • 	 Acupressure Holding Points & more! Goal-Setting and 111 TFH Metaphors access the power 
of Dialogue and Creative Visualization, Imagination,

text:
T O U C H  F O R  H E A L T H
356 5-Day TFH Clinical Intensive Retreat TFH Proficiency: Review, Practice and  
Practical & Written Assessment Taught by Matthew Thie, in the tradition of Dr. John 
Thie at the Serra Retreat in Malibu, California. This is a 2-day Workshop where you will work with 
other TFH’ers who have completed TFH (1-4) as well as 
current TFH Instructors updating their skills. In some 
countries it is an entry-level qualification for entering 
the professional associations. Completing this Review, 
Practice and Assessment will put you on solid footing 
to continue with the advanced TFH Synthesis Training 
(Oriented to advanced practice, Communicating 
TFH, and Instructor preparation). IKC Certificate of 
Proficiency upon successful completion. How can you get the most out of your own life? How 
can you best care for your whole Soul? How can you 
effectively participate with your family, friends and 
health-care providers in optimizing your own personal 
experience and wellbeing? TFH Clinical Seminars Taught by Matthew Thie, in the tradition of Dr. John 
Thie at the Serra Retreat in Malibu, California, and at 
other Kinesiology centers internationally. TFH Synthesis Training (ITW) Deepen your Background Knowledge of the History and
Philosophy of TFH and the Theories behind the various
Reflex Systems. Open to anyone willing to learn
through intensive participation in Class Practice and
Presentations. Also known as the Instructor Training
Workshop (ITW), the workshop is considered a import-
ant element of any Professional Kinesiologist prepara-
tion in the Self-responsibility/ Educational model. Upon 
successful completion, you will have the option of 
applying for a certificate as a TFH Instructor of TFH 1-4, 
and TFH short courses. This course is also one of the 
core requirements for the new IKC designation of 
TFH Kinesiology Instructor/Consultant. PRE-REQUISITE:
TFH 1-4; Proficiency Exam. How can you get the most out of your own life? How
can you best care for your whole Soul? How can you
effectively participate with your family, friends and
health-care providers in optimizing your own personal
experience and wellbeing? Come experience Touch for Health (TFH) for yourself:
a process of balancing your posture, energy and atti-
tude.Matthew will balance seminar participants as
case studies/demonstrations for the group, showing the 
potential of the TFH model and techniques in clinical 
practice. Participants will also practice and experience 
several balancing sessions with other participants 
during the retreat. TFH Meta-Instructor Training For tuition and more information, contact: Open to anyone who has taken TFH 1-4 and the 
Metaphors. TFH Instructors who take the Metaphor 
Workshop and Meta-Instructor Training may teach the 
TFH Metaphors and provide the official IKC certificate. Touch for Health Education
(213) 482-4480
thie@touch4health.com
